body:
Discussed with @BastiaanOlij. CC @lyuma 

To get CICD to pass, it's not as simple as disabling the platforms because the OpenXR driver is tied into the renderer.

The plan is to swap every platform other than Linux and Windows with the dummy OpenXR.

Android needs a lot more work, and we need help there. @m4gr3d 

We need to create a dummy implementation for the disabled platforms. There is a dummy implementation, but it doesn't compile because it's far from complete. Currently, the dummy OpenXR implementation re-uses the header files.

The right solution is to create a dummy folder and put header files with just the class definitions and the few methods in the app but without code. Scons can then be instructed to use the dummy folder instead of the main folder.

The platforms without OpenXR support will then compile fine.

body:
Ok, just a quick status update here now that things are starting to move fast.

Rendering now works and it looks like we're tracking properly. Rendering for now still requires the mobile renderer even on desktop (though there is a PR that makes part of the desktop renderer work) and uses a final blit from Godots rendering buffers to OpenXRs swapchain. This does render OpenXRs foveated rendering inoperable, I am looking into a solution for rendering directly into the swapchain but even then OpenXRs approach to foveated rendering does not agree with Godots render setup and I feel only VRS will solve this properly. That is next on my list once this PR is finished.
The other open issue around rendering is OpenXRs insistence that we supply images in linear color space while most devices use 32bit RBGA8 buffers which means banding. Sucks. Right now all output is in sRGB which means the image is over bright inside the headset.
Also note that lighting on Godot primitives is currently broken but that falls outside of the scope of this PR (I am going to look into this however because it has annoyed the heck out of me for some time now).

My current focus is on porting the controller logic over from the plugin. I just committed the changes for the resource classes that allow us to define our OpenXR action map and the code that generates a default map with default interaction profiles. 

Important to note here for those who are not familiar with OpenXR, OpenXR takes ownership over the bindings. Our interaction with OpenXR is over actions. We provide suggestions to OpenXR how we want different controllers to be bound to these actions however whether the XR runtime decides to listen is out of our control. It has big pro's, and big con's, but that fight is long past us and this solution was chosen. The bottom line here is that you have no control, and what you submit to OpenXR at startup can't be altered. Re-binding is handled by the XR runtime (if you are lucky, most currently don't offer this so if your player is left handed, sucks for them).

Next thing I'll be working on is making the action map actually work so we have working controllers, and after that I need to port the UI I was working on. 

Lastly, right now Android is the only platform that isn't working yet. The files are there but I'll need help from someone who knows Android more to put it all together. For Meta Quest support it is also important to find out of the official Khronos loader will be supported by the Quest.
author: BastiaanOlij

> I am not sure if I understand why we have drivers/openxr and modules/openxr. Sounds like we should want either one or the other?

This was one of the things I wanted to discuss with you @reduz , I'd like the whole thing to be in modules but we can't because part of the solution is interwoven with the Vulkan drivers and has code being executed at startup before the module system is even initialized.

We also can't move what is in modules into drivers, there are classes here that don't belong in drivers and there will be more like classes that will be part of the editor.

So I had to split the solution in two with the driver part having purely the code that interfaces with OpenXR itself, and modules the bits that are facing the end user.

author: BastiaanOlij

body:
@SaracenOne made a small change making the Vulkan version check a warning for now. Godot checks with the Vulkan driver what version of Vulkan is supported on the host system, we select the highest v1.x.x version of Vulkan that is supported by the host (currently usually 1.2.0 on desktop).

We then have an extra check with the OpenXR runtime as OpenXR will also tell us which version of Vulkan the runtime wishes to use. The weird thing here is that Oculus reports it only supports Vulkan 1.0.0.

We believe we can safely ignore this, 1.2.0 is backwards compatible with 1.0.0 so anything the OpenXR runtime wants to use should work, but we don't want to limit ourselves in using newer Vulkan features (if available) just because the OpenXR runtime doesn't want to use those. 

So far this seems to work fine.

body:
When the project name is "Test" and "Create Folder" is on, project path is `/path/to/dir/Test`. If I then change the path to `/path/to/dir/DoesNotExist` and click "Create & Edit". It creates and uses the "DoesNotExist" folder for me.

I'm not sure if this is expected. If yes, would it be better to place the button somewhere else because it's now not "that" related to the project name as before.

Also, should "Create Folder" use a CheckBox instead of a CheckButton? Because the control does not have immediate effect (the folder is not immediately created).

To summarize:

* What the "Create Folder" button does was clear: Create a folder with the project name at the current project path, and use that folder as the new project path.
* The "Create Folder" toggle is confusing:
    * When toggled off: It strips the last component from project path no matter if it's the same as project name.
        1. Turn on
        2. Set the project path to `/path/to/something/you/typed/later`
        3. Turn off
        4. And it strips `/later`
    * When toggled on: It appends the project name to the project path, even when the current path does not exist.
        1. Set the project path to `/path/that/does/not/exist`
        2. Turn on
        3. The project path becomes `/path/that/does/not/exist/ProjectName`
        4. It still reports error because the parent directory does not exist (and won't be created).
    * When "Create & Edit" is pressed: create the project folder if missing when the toggle is on.
author: timothyqiu

body:
> When the project name is "Test" and "Create Folder" is on, project path is `/path/to/dir/Test`. If I then change the path to `/path/to/dir/DoesNotExist` and click "Create & Edit". It creates and uses the "DoesNotExist" folder for me.

This is the behavior I intended. Some users might want to make the directory name different from the project name (e.g. `Card Game` project name and `card-game` directory)

> It be better to place the button somewhere else because it's now not "that" related to the project name as before.

I agree that moving the button somewhere else would make sense, but the only other place I can think of would be below the name/path fields, taking more vertical space.

> Also, should "Create Folder" use a CheckBox instead of a CheckButton? Because the control does not have immediate effect (the folder is not immediately created).

I think a `CheckButton` works better because the button immediately changes the path field.

> * What the "Create Folder" button does was clear: Create a folder with the project name at the current project path, and use that folder as the new project path.
> 
> * The "Create Folder" toggle is confusing:
>   
>   * When toggled off: It strips the last component from project path no matter if it's the same as project name.
>     
>     1. Turn on
>     2. Set the project path to `/path/to/something/you/typed/later`
>     3. Turn off
>     4. And it strips `/later`
>   * When toggled on: It appends the project name to the project path, even when the current path does not exist.
>     
>     1. Set the project path to `/path/that/does/not/exist`
>     2. Turn on
>     3. The project path becomes `/path/that/does/not/exist/ProjectName`
>     4. It still reports error because the parent directory does not exist (and won't be created).
>   * When "Create & Edit" is pressed: create the project folder if missing when the toggle is on.

I tried my best to make it intuitive and straightforward for most use-cases.

I've already considered changing the text on the button, but I couldn't decide on what to change it to.

Edit: I see two potential improvements, adding them to the root PR.

@nathanfranke Thanks for adding this :slightly_smiling_face: 

Does it prevent creating the project? It should, as users should never be able to brick their Godot editor installation entirely.

Also, I'd rephrase it slightly:

> Creating a project in the engine's working directory or the home directory is not allowed, as it would prevent the editor from starting.

The check should be performed on both `$CWD` and `$HOME` regardless of the current path of the engine binary, in case you're starting the engine from `$CWD` but you intend to install it for use with `$HOME` later on.

body:
> Note: I assume `VALUATOR_TILT*` range is `-x .. x` and `pen_tilt_*_range[0]` is always negative, have not found any documentation to check it.

Although there is no reason a driver couldn't specify a minimum greater than zero, I think it's a reasonable assumption to expect the minimum value to be `<= 0`. The real problem is the minimum and maximum values are not necessarily symmetrical. On my Wacom tablet, the values are `-64` and `63`. However, if for some obscure reason, a driver specifies a minimum greater than zero, I think it would still be correct to keep the shifted value and just scale it to be between `0` and `1` as done in this PR.

I did a small test based on this comment:

> An option to completely remove the node at runtime would be nice. Sometimes we will want optional visibility, this can be easily accomplished with code, but sometimes it might be useful to ignore "editor-only" nodes completely and that's not totally feasible. **We can remove the node only after it has been loaded and has taken resources up.**

Apparently, when an editor-only node contains some resource, that resource will be loaded anyway. The reason is that you are stripping the node on load, but any resource referenced by that node is still in the scene and is being loaded. Any chance this could be fixed? It would be the best selling point for this feature.

author: reduz

body:
Would this flag also allow runtime-only nodes? Because it would be useful to hide things only in editor too, without requiring code, and I think it could be through this same feature.

In fact, it would be best to already go one step further and do a more powerful and generic way to exclude nodes/resources anywhere. IMO the best implementation would be through a property `availability` of type `PackedStringArray` on `Node` and `Resource`. It would allow a list of platforms, i.e.:
- every export platform (e.g. `android`, `windows`, ...) plus `exported` (meaning all of them) and its opposite `editor`
- for export platforms, specific modes too, e.g.:  
    `android.x86_32`, `android.arm64.debug`, `windows.release`, `exported.release`, ...
- also `editor.editing` and `editor.running` for the specific modes of the `editor` platform

This property would have the special value `["everywhere"]` by default.

In terms of UI, you could make the button toggle just the editor-only state by default, and show a more complete context menu with specific toggles if you right-click it or if it's any value except `["everywhere"]` and `["editor.editing"]`.

For the AUTHORS.md list we chose to put a threshold of 10 commits before including people, so that it represents people who have done significant work on the engine (which makes the current list total 200 contributors, as opposed to the 1823 total contributors count if we go down to single-commit contributors). body:
Your code requires Normals, Tangents, Colors, UVs and UV2s to match.

Normal must exist. If empty Normals, we can force generate assuming smooth shading.

Once we have the normals, we can generate tangents.

We can append Colors without issue.

Given tangents exist from the previous step we can generate or combine UVs, UVs2.

1. If an UV does not exist, generate it by using the smooth grouping Normals from the previous stages. https://github.com/godot-extended-libraries/godot-fire/tree/feature/mesh-unwrap
2. Merge atlases https://github.com/godot-extended-libraries/scene_merge/blob/master/merge.cpp#L482 including bleeding by some large number like 16 pixels for 1024x1024 https://github.com/godot-extended-libraries/scene_merge/blob/master/rjm_texbleed.cpp.
3. Merge and Split by grid (lawnjelly)

Any thoughts?

author: fire

body:
> @lawnjelly do you need help porting to master?

Sure if you want to do something similar in master, that would be great, it would probably need some discussion though with the other rendering guys in terms of how it should work etc. Also bear in mind with vulkan especially, the cost of drawcalls is lower, so there will be relatively less benefit to merging, compared to 3.x, and especially with GLES2 which doesn't have instancing.

> Splitting into the basic system where merging does the checks and the ensuring the similarity is a better design.

There are two functions available simply because that's how it needs to be done from the rooms conversion system (it needs to identify mergeable MeshInstances at an earlier stage). The bound function always does the checks (only the private internal one can omit the checks).

In this PR we could choose to _not_ bind the `is_mergeable_with` function, but it is probably handy in terms of user interface etc in its own right. Say if you were selecting meshes in the editor, it would be handy to know beforehand whether they could be merged _before_ calling the function etc.

body:
While the cross product is defined in 3D, it does have a meaning in 2D, so we can give a better explanation than just pretending it's really in 3D and we look at the Z axis of the resulting vector.

![Untitled](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1646875/148265358-9065fef3-6c84-40bd-a9fa-f7bf9cb735f8.png)

This image is almost correct, the full version is that it's actually the signed area, since swapping the vectors will flip the sign of the cross product (`v × w == -(w × v)` and `v × w == -w × v`).

This property is also true in 3D, except that it's the magnitude of the vector that is the area of the parallelogram.

Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eu6i7WJeinw&list=PLZHQObOWTQDPD3MizzM2xVFitgF8hE_ab&index=10
author: aaronfranke

body: @aaronfranke Would you have a suggestion on how to phrase it for the docs?
author: akien-mga

body:
As already discussed in #7054 and #16485, cross product is only defined in 3D and 7D space.

How we compute _cross product_ here is one of [several ways](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross_product#Generalizations) to generalize cross product to other dimensions. That's why I think we should define "the cross product for 2D vectors" instead of using the term recursively in the description, thus this PR.

IINM, the "signed area" is technically the [_exterior product_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_vector_algebra_and_geometric_algebra#Cross_and_exterior_products). It's because we generalize cross product the exterior product way, that makes it produce the signed area, but not the other way around. It feels bad to say 2D cross product _is_ the signed area (like in the YouTube video). Maybe a better solution is to rename the method to avoid such awkward situation, but I don't think it's feasible.

It's helpful to add geometric meaning explanation for various vector/matrix operations. But I think it's out of the scope of this PR.
author: timothyqiu

body:
> That's why I think we should define "the cross product for 2D vectors" instead of using the term recursively in the description, thus this PR.

I agree that it should be defined, thus the geometric explanation I gave. Defining it by pointing to the 3D definition also works, and it's fine and correct, but it seems silly to me to tell people to pretend that 2D vectors are in 3D space.

> It's because we generalize cross product the exterior product way, that makes it produce the signed area, but not the other way around.

We generalize it this way because it is a very useful generalization and is the most similar to cross product in 3D. This is the most sensible generalization available. The geometric interpretation of the area of the parallelogram is also directly applicable to 3D so it gives a visual interpretation for people to think about that is applicable to both 2D and 3D.

> It feels bad to say 2D cross product *is* the signed area (like in the YouTube video).

Saying the 2D cross product *is* this generalization would be our definition, we are defining it in the docs. Another way is referring to the 3D cross product as in this PR. Yet another option is we could point to the math formula used by the engine. All of these work to define what it is we're doing.

> Maybe a better solution is to rename the method to avoid such awkward situation, but I don't think it's feasible.

Please don't rename it :)
author: aaronfranke

body:
I refer to the 3D cross product because there is not ambiguity about it. I'm OK with either ways as they make sense from different perspectives.

Let @akien-mga decide on that 😛
author: timothyqiu

body:
> Let @akien-mga decide on that stuck_out_tongue

Ouch :D

I really don't have a strong opinion either way, I think that for something like this the more explicit we can be, the better. So I think it's fine to mention that it's an analog to the (actually mathematically defined) cross product, and give some indication of what it can be used for/represent geometrically.

author: deralmas

body:
I couldn't measure any significant difference in performance.

However, I ran into an issue with sRGB handling:

In the preview when selecting the texture in the file browser LA8 is treated as sRGB, but L8 isn't. I have not been able to find the reason for this.
And in 3d with GLES3, neither L8 nor LA8 are treated as sRGB.
Setting the flag (`flags/srgb`) on import does not make any difference.

The only flag that does work is in the material itself (i.e. `flags_albedo_tex_force_srgb`).

I'm afraid we need to put this on hold until sRGB is properly implemented.
author: mortarroad

body:
Turns out standard OpenGL only has RGB and RGBA formats with sRGB support.
We should fall back onto those, if a texture has to be decoded as sRGB.

My GPU seems to support,
`GL_EXT_texture_sRGB`, which has L8 and LA8, and
`GL_EXT_texture_sRGB_R8`, which has R8,
but we cannot rely on that.

author: rcorre

body:
I did some testing and I really like how these new shortcuts work, but I have a couple of notes/nitpicks:
* Switching the locked axis doesn't give consistent feedback. Depending on whether you switch from no lock to global lock or from global lock to local lock, the node you are editing updates differently. In some cases it doesn't update at all, in others only the rotation is updated, and the full transform is not updated until you move the mouse.
Ideally the object would completely update on screen as soon as you press the axis shortcut, giving the user a better idea of what is going to happen if they move the mouse.

* Axis lines stay visible after a transformation/edit. It may just be missing a call to `surface->update();` somewhere.

* Are there any good shortcuts available that we could set as defaults for these transform modes? I think these new immediate modes can be very useful, so I would consider having them enabled by default.
author: JFonS

body:
> Are there any good shortcuts available that we could set as defaults for these transform modes

I figured using anything other than the blender defaults of `G`rab `R`otate `S`cale would be unintuitive, but S already conflicts with the normal scale tool (and maybe R does too? IIRC QWER are all bound by default)

Thanks for giving it a try! I'll look into the other two bullets.
author: rcorre

body:
I did some more testing after the changes, here are some more small things I  found:
* If you select the scale tool (R shortcut) and then do a view transform (click and drag anywhere) the object translates instead of scaling.

* You can do `Shift+Axis` while rotating, but rotating an object over two axes is not really defined, so things just lock-up. I'm not sure what's the best way to handle this, probably just ignoring `Shit+Axis` shortcuts while rotating.

* The displayed axis when doing `Shift+Axis` transforms are wrong for `Shift+Y` (only shows X axis) and `Shift+Z` (shows all axes).

Some other things I noticed that can be improved in future PRs, not necessarily in this one:
* Axis highlighting on the gizmo handles is still active when doing instant transformations, which can be confusing. We could disable highlighting altogether, or even better, highlight the currently locked axes.

* Given the way we compute transformations (intersecting rays and planes) sometimes the current mouse position doesn't intersect the transform plane and moving the mouse does nothing. This means the user can be moving things around and, after changing the locked axis, they get no movement and no feedback as to why nothing is happening. #56946 should help alleviate this in rotations, but the other transforms can also fall into deadzones, so it would be interesting to look into those as well.

Again, great work on this PR. Once the three smaller items are fixed this should be good to merge :)

body:
Won't this make all windows focused by default when using multiple windows?

From a quick look at the Windows and macOS DisplayServers they don't seem to default to focused, it's only set when receiving appropriate events. Maybe we lack something that would get a focus in notification triggered on the main window after creation? CC @bruvzg
author: akien-mga

body:
> Won't this make all windows focused by default when using multiple windows?

Yes, but surely this should be expected.

> Maybe we lack something that would get a focus in notification triggered on the main window after creation?

Yes, a `FocusIn` event is not received by new windows, which is the only time this variable is set to `true`.

Note: When a window is created, all events that are not `ConfigureNotify` events are discarded here:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/a2a4ec7cb582650ed0cb70b33e9d954e288d1513/platform/linuxbsd/display_server_x11.cpp#L4647-L4653
So even if a `FocusIn` event is sent (which I'm not convinced it is, because I would expect only a `MapNotify` event to be sent) it will be discarded.

Note 2: It is set to `true` on initialization in 3.x:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8f17a4037bb302c8b8b0659e9bcdd4c06c288713/platform/x11/os_x11.cpp#L4138

author: bruvzg

body:
> Hm, can Godot maybe detect that editor or system language is one of CJK and suggest downloading optional fonts? This way, CJK speakers get improved looks, while all the others do NOT get unnecessary bloat...

I think that would be a pretty good way of approaching it. I'm trying to think of other examples of software which does this, but I'm sure I've seen precedent for this approach.

> > Not sure what's going on with the Linux build. Having trouble parsing the specific error, but I'll look into it a bit more later
> 
> A font header might be too big for GCC, I had a similar issue when I was trying to include a full ICU data file (~25 MB) as a header.

Hmm, that sounds about right. I wonder if that's a sign that this is the wrong approach and indeed, the correct way is prompting the user to download the fonts instead.
author: SaracenOne

body:
@SaracenOne I think you can try using one of the `NotoSansCJK<lang>-{Regular,Bold}.otf` fonts instead of the subset TTFs. Regular and Bold are about 32 MB in total (saves ~10 MB).

These fonts allow picking the appearance using `locl` OpenType feature. Different `<lang>` in the filename only decides the default appearance of glyphs.
author: timothyqiu

body: Cool, I'll look into this!
author: SaracenOne

body: > These fonts allow picking the appearance using locl OpenType feature. Different in the filename only decides the default appearance of glyphs. HarfBuzz have `locl` feature enabled by default, and it seems to be working fine with the fonts taken from https://github.com/googlefonts/noto-cjk/tree/main/Sans/OTF/Japanese. That's probably a better way to do it. > Hmm, that sounds about right. I wonder if that's a sign that this is the wrong approach and indeed, the correct way is prompting the user to download the fonts instead. I guess if we go with downloading, it should be full "Language Pack" not just font, and will save even more space since editor/class db translations are quite large (https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3421). > A font header might be too big for GCC, I had a similar issue when I was trying to include a full ICU data file (~25 MB) as a header. For the reference, adding 30 MB of fonts to test `locl` caused Apple clang to consume almost 6 GB of RAM, GCC is usually significantly more memory hungry, probably it's just running out of memory. I'm not sure how to solve it.
author: bruvzg

body: Can the newly added system fonts support improve this issue?
author: reduz

body:
> Can the newly added system fonts support improve this issue?

If you have a list of pre-installed CJK fonts for each system. Or if you are willing to load every single system font and check if it supports all required characters (font metadata is not fully reliable, so getting supported language list might be not enough).

Seems fine to me. Another option would be to add support for `\[` and `\]` to escape brackets, so this would become `\[b\]text\[/b\]`.

That's maybe more common/expected at least now that Markdown is so popular, but might also be more difficult to implement, so I'm fine with using `[lb]` and `[rb]` for literal brackets.

body:
This should maybe be added to `doc/tools/make_rst.py` too for the class reference XML+BBCode to RST converter. So far we've always enclosed non-tag brackets with `[code] [/code]` (which treats everything within as literals), and that seems to be good enough, but it probably makes sense to have this feature there too.

There are more of our BBCode tags which are supported in RichTextLabel but not in `make_rst.py`, we should probably document that for doc writers (but that's outside the scope of this PR).

body:
I have an idea for how to implement surface picking for setting the correct surface material. I would like some thoughts on it before I write any code. @reduz, @JFonS ?

Getting the correct surface can be done by getting the vertices for each mesh surface and doing ray-triangle intersection tests based on those vertices.

This could be pretty expensive on complex meshes however, so my thought was to index these surface triangles by their surface normal. This way I could do a raycast from the editor camera, get the collision normal and check the index against that. This would reduce the number of tested triangles.

Is this a valid approach?

EDIT: Now that I'm testing, I can't create any mesh that has more than 1 `surface_material_override`, I'm quite sure I have created a box mesh that had 6 overrides before. 🤔
author: gaudecker

body:
Regarding surface picking, this was something I'd really like to add, but couldn't think of a decent way of handling it, to the point that I was considering skipping it and simply using mouse release+key modifier to compensate.

There is another problem with surface picking though which is more broad though: skinned meshes, since the current gizmo selector only consider the unposed base mesh, not the current pose it's in when doing selection

**Regarding surface picking:**

I wrote a quick proof of concept implementation where I generate a `TriangleMesh` from each mesh surface and do ray-triangle intersection tests on its faces.

It seems to work well, but there will probably be performance issues with larger meshes. If someone knows a better way to do this, let me know.

author: akien-mga

body:
I think this should not put the material on the `GeometryInstance3D/material_override`. It should be on the actual mesh material slot (`Mesh/surface_X/material` or `CSG/material`), with an option to go on the `MeshInstance3D/surface_material_override/X`. Or the reverse, on the surface override with an option for the mesh.

Reasons:

* **For attaching it to the mesh (`Mesh/surface_X/material`):** The most common use case is setting up master assets, which are saved in scenes to be reused. When meshes are imported, materials are attached to the mesh. We don't import materials into override slots, so dropping materials on meshes should also default to this most common use case. That is a specific material for a specific mesh, tied to the actual mesh as a master material. Not just instances of the mesh.

* **For Mesh Instance override as a secondary option (`MeshInstance3D/surface_material_override/X`):** In some less common cases, I might have generic objects like rocks that can use multiple rock materials. In this case I'd want a blank or default rock material on the mesh, and a quick drag and drop to the **mesh instance** surface material override for a custom, **per instance** override of say a mossy or dirt covered variant.

* **Against using `GeometryInstance3D/material_override`:** besides the fact that it's way down in the inspector, the biggest reason against this is material_override only supports one slot. surface_material_override and mesh/surfaces support all material slots. If I have an object w/ 4 material slots, there's no reason I would want the same material in all four slots. If I wanted full coverage of one material, I wouldn't have taken the time to split the uvs into 4 groupings. I would have made a mesh with only one slot.

e.g. 4 material slots
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/632766/182034920-e3d3a6c1-d848-448e-ad4e-ce914d34426f.png)

I have removed the safe check now, which actually makes the freeing behavior consistent across other implementations for various textures.

The potential inconsistency can be fixed via a dedicated PR (in the `master` branch), not here, because this affects other texture implementations, which is obviously out of scope for this PR.

I'd like to remove `GradientTexture2D` in Goost (see goostengine/goost#170) which I and other [Goost users](https://github.com/goostengine/goost/discussions/136) have been using for the past year successfully, so that I can maintain this feature in Godot now. Without this fix, this introduces a regression in Goost when `GradientTexture2D` is used as a light source for `Light2D`, because the feature is already used in production.

Thanks for understanding.

author: ator-dev

body:
Sorry but I am not convinced by this approach, it maybe makes writing code faster but it is at the price of significantly slowing down any editor action.

Most of the time this is not a problem anyway, as storing the previous state of a property stays relatively simple. The real complex cases that UndoRedo has to handle are usually when you have modified sub-objects, which your implementation does not covers.
As a consequence, I don't think it is worth making UndoRedo slower considering the most common use case works fines with the current approach, and this new one does not help with more complex scenarios.
author: groud

body:
(I just noticed I commented in the proposal rather than here, so copying it here)

My feelings about this is that, in my experience, most of the time when dealing with undo and redo, you are not really dealing with properties or something that simply changes state. In most cases you are adding or removing things, so its not really possible to do different to the way it is now.

Even in many cases where you modify properties, its not as simple as replacing new value by existing value, as the existing value has to be cached at the beginning of editing (as in when you drag something and move it).

So, even though I understand this can be useful in some cases, it sounds like it would be a minimal amount of them, for which I am not entirely sure it's worth doing this change.
author: reduz

body:
Thank you very much for your time in checking this and pointing out potential issues! I really appreciate your understanding in dealing with my proposal and considering a differing point of view. Below I have given my responses (and explanations) to the points you brought up - if you have the time please take a moment to look at any relevant sections, as I have tried to lay out my argument clearly there in order to better describe how the code applies, and why I believe it genuinely to be a useful addition that could make integration of safe undo systems much simpler; don't feel you need to read everything as this is absurdly long. The code screenshots may be helpful, however, in summing up what I think are 'problem areas' with the current system.

A question I will pose to you first, though, is: Are you happy with the way undo-redo is currently handled, and the code that contributors are making in order to use the system correctly? Do you think the amount of effort required to maintain actions, and the reliability issues users are having with undo-redo, is acceptable for the editor? _(Note - I'm not making any judgement about whether it is or isn't, this is for you to decide.)_ If so, then I have no objection and the main undo methods can continue being used in the same way; I will start instead working on a possible improvement over global undo which many users seem to be clamouring for.

As I see it undo-redo is a chore that in many cases takes a lot of work to make function for a particular part of the editor (e.g. newly designed or redesigned by a contributor), and then can be badly affected by a change in one of the used methods or may lead to failure/discrepancy edge cases; and to me the resulting code looks difficult to understand, maintain, or change. This is only my opinion though - as I would like to emphasise, and I fully acknowledge my bias towards cumulative undo given that I designed it - and you may consider it in a different light; in which case I'd appreciate your thoughts on this. If there's a certain way you'd like contributors to be working with the main undo-redo methods that would work better and isn't always being followed, that would be good to know as well.

@groud 's points:

> at the price of significantly slowing down any editor action

This is a concern I had originally as well, and I can see why you don't like the idea of the system having to check where the changes happened in an object when an action is committed. I would argue though that compared to the price of, for example, rendering text or doing other background processes, this isn't hugely expensive and not something to worry about - all that happens is it checks a list of often less than 10, almost always less than 20 properties and logs any with a difference from the cached value. Personally, there has been no perceivable difference in performance for me; lag from introducing too many objects/keys/values occurs long before and is the same amount as with the normal methods. Also, since this happens once every time the user performs a significant action and has no effect on project performance, it is not too important in my opinion and many of the editor methods already do more complex looping operations, some of which, unlike cumulative undo, might not have linear time complexity.

> storing the previous state of a property stays relatively simple.

I agree completely that the main system works in an intuitive way, and it doesn't take a lot of effort to make a simple action work. However, I think it's very easy to make mistakes - especially where references/pointers are involved - and the resulting code is often hard to make sense of for when it needs to be changed or understood. There are lots of complex if/case statements that deal with undo actions, and the additional complication of needing dedicated variables and/or calls for rollback values can make code significantly less clear and so harder to maintain.

The current system results in strings of nested code, full of do/undo methods often unclear in their relation to each other. As I see it, the problem even greater than unreadable code is that these methods can change internally at any time; there is no restriction on which methods are used, including a mixture of internal processing, abstraction layers, and methods that are exposed to GDScript (or the main APIs).

The methods contain a large amount of their own complexity/processing, some of which could be completely unrelated to the needs of the undo system, and there is no guarantee that 'opposite' methods do the exact opposite thing (especially when there is no 'opposite' method available, so a combination of others must be used). Using the current system there will always be edge cases to fix, where the modified object (or other objects modified by the methods) gain a particular state unexpected by the undo system, leading potentially to a crash or data loss; just one incongruency has the potential to escalate with more undos/redos which the user may automatically try when they notice that something has gone wrong.

Below are some screenshots of code I believe highlights some of the issues particularly well; rollback values can be non-obvious as well as how methods relate, spaghetti code has been generated through having to call long strings of methods, some methods have been made specifically for undo-redo, and special cases (often extending a long way above and below) have to be given their own sets of similar but not quite identical undo/redo lines:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153290525-b76cbc57-4f5a-4dff-8eb1-aeae921b0924.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153290932-9122a268-e464-4797-8c26-c12a01693971.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153291091-9ae3e109-8d15-4a75-ae9e-2c28410534a1.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153291226-1fa939ce-3b88-4121-a993-5fb38b7364d1.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153291484-a8773200-acbd-48c9-8b63-ac494f39b9c3.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66553618/153291825-df690bf9-c946-4e05-ad0e-19d144a76f6b.png)

> when you have modified sub-objects, which your implementation does not covers.

> does not help with more complex scenarios.

My implementation does not follow object references automatically to avoid unneeded processing; however, making a nested action for a modified sub-object works as expected (as I tested when replacing all undo methods in the animation system). In my opinion this is a good solution, since it makes very clear what objects outside of the main target are being modified, and possibly discourages unnecessary state changes outside of the current object.

You are right that complex scenarios will not necessarily work with this system - however, I think there are [broadly] two main types of 'complex scenario' handled by undo-redo. The first involves creating and moving nodes or similar objects within a data structure like a SceneTree; this would not work very naturally with cumulative undo, and would probably take considerable effort. The second is complex because lots of method calls (`do` and `undo`) are made to an object progressing it to a particular desired state, often including calls that only do a very small amount of the processing but which must be accounted for nevertheless. This can be reduced. with cumulative undo`, to only a few lines of code - a state change is declared to have started, the state is changed with a few direct calls, and this is committed with precisely the previous state and the new state being recorded.

If something goes slightly wrong during the main undo methods (relatively likely in this second kind of complex situation) and the state is not returned to the exact expected state, this can leak out with more undos/redos and affect other parts of the object, but cumulative works almost as a backup that can fix the result of a complex operation not quite working (resulting otherwise in data loss or corruption). This could really help with users' trust in the engine and especially undo-redo, which at the moment is not great as demonstrated in [this poll](https://www.reddit.com/r/godot/comments/slwtkx/poll_how_do_you_use_godots_undo_system/?utm_source=share&utm_medium=web2x&context=3); when I have spoken with the community about it in general, they have expressed distrust and unhappiness with the system and it's fairly common to avoid doing more than one undo at a time.

@reduz 's points:

> you are not really dealing with properties or something that simply changes state. In most cases you are adding or removing things

It's true that not many deal with properties explicitly (call `add_do_method` rather than `..._property`), but underneath it is usually simple state that is changed as a result, which cumulative undo works well with. Any places where this system would work especially poorly it doesn't have to be implemented, but since it utilises the methods called when loading scenes/resources from files most things including adding/removing objects works automatically anyway. For example, when I tested replacing all animation-related undo methods with cumulative ones, this included creating and removing animation resources as well as other similar processes, and worked as expected.

> the existing value has to be cached at the beginning of editing

Could you give some more detail on this please? The system works essentially like this, by caching the value at the start of an edit (when `create_action_cumulative` is called) - I tried it with animation key editing [which works by dragging], and instead of breaking the system it fixed several significant issues with it where keys are deleted or moved incorrectly by the main undo system methods.

> even though I understand this can be useful in some cases, it sounds like it would be a minimal amount of them

Thank you for considering my idea and seeing its potential uses! I understand that it looks like it would be non-useful in the majority of situations, but (as mentioned in some of my points above) I have found it to work well in almost every case - the main ones I'm dubious about are the ones involving adding/removing nodes, which is probably best handled by the main undo methods. It functioned with no noticeable performance difference when all animation editor undo-calls were replaced, fixing several problems at the same time, and from looking at many of the other files I see no reason it couldn't be implemented to a large extent in other places as well.

By the way, to demonstrate where cumulative undo could be especially helpful, I tried it on a part of the animation editor which previously had no undo-redo implemented due to how complex it would be to get working: Optimize and Clean Up Anim. It enabled this to be done through adding 2 lines of readable code, and meant that as well as being able to safely cache a complicated action, this action would no longer cause the undo history stack to be deleted which is confusing and frustrating for users.
author: ator-dev

body:
> Are you happy with the way undo-redo is currently handled, and the code that contributors are making in order to use the system correctly? Do you think the amount of effort required to maintain actions, and the reliability issues users are having with undo-redo, is acceptable for the editor? 

Well, I never had to complain too much about it. The API is quite simple and straightforward to use. So yeah, I like it the way it is right now. We needed to add a few features here and there for some specific use cases (like, for example, the TileData's object's properties that depend on its owner TileSet's properties). But otherwise it works fine.

> I agree completely that the main system works in an intuitive way, and it doesn't take a lot of effort to make a simple action work. However, I think it's very easy to make mistakes - especially where references/pointers are involved - and the resulting code is often hard to make sense of for when it needs to be changed or understood.

Yeah, and the main problem is that your solution won't solve those problems anyway. The most complex cases are when your editing modifies several objects at the same time, you have visual/cache updates to trigger elsewhere, or optimizations to be done not to slow down the editor, etc... So even with an automatic way to detect which properties changed, then you would still have to do a lot of manual work to solve those complex cases. Your solution only make sense for small objects modifications, and basically transforms a handful of `add_do_*` and `add_undo_*` into a single function call.

For those cases, it might be interesting for some people to write a little bit less code, but in general in Godot, we favor simple APIs in place of more complex ones, even if it means the end user has to write less code. Thus, regarding that point, most of your screenshots show perfectly acceptable and understandable code to me.
author: groud

body:
Thank you for confirming this, and thanks again for your time. I personally prefer APIs that allow less code to be written, but I can understand why [in the Godot design philosophy] you prefer more simple ones that give more power and efficiency, and see that this may not be of interest to Godot developers and its design. I'll close the PR with the understanding that either this suggestion or a future one could still supersede the main undo system later on, but at least right now there is little interest in overhauling the current way of working as issues caused by it are acceptable.

Final answers to your points, for the record:

> The API is quite simple and straightforward to use.

I agree with this, but not fully, since it does result in contributors having to put a lot of effort into making a complex series of function calls (and reversed operations) work for undo-redo - I see this as difficult to understand and maintain. You're right that it's efficient and easy to understand/control when writing undo-redo code though, and it's true that in many cases it is the 'right' system.

> Your solution only make sense for small objects modifications, and basically transforms a handful of `add_do_*` and `add_undo_*` into a single function call.

Your summary is correct (though unless I misunderstand you're implying that only small changes to objects are covered, whereas I think my system works best for large, complex changes). It does still take some extra work to implement for changes to several objects, several cases (such as adding/removing nodes) aren't covered, and it does only combine do/undo calls. In my opinion it would still be helpful for improving maintainability and reliability of certain areas of the editor, but again the worth of this is for project lead developers such as you and reduz to decide, and I accept that Godot's philosophy doesn't necessarily integrate well with this.

body:
I've found very little difference in performance between the min for the setting and the max (8k) on an i3 10105f (3.7GHz) and the potato antiquity that is the Quadro K2000, so there's really not much of a reason to decrease the setting unless you're trying to hyper-optimize the game.

In fact it seems that setting the thing too low can have a negative performance impact.
at 32 i'm getting 260 FPS in an empty scene. with 8192 I'm getting 303, with default i'm getting 310

Personally I think just maxing out the setting makes sense, as in real life scenarios, the tiny hit that you're getting from maxing it out is nothing compared to what is actually going on in the scenes, while setting it too low will have a very clear impact on scenes when you go over the limit.

In a Scene with dozens of lights and 3m primitives at 1mpx/frame i'm getting the identical render times (~44.7ms) with the 8k and default settings tested.

body:
I like splitting stop and play into stop, pause, start, and resume, but this does leave a question. After this change, what will be the best way to replay an animation if it's currently finished, resume an animation if paused, switch to and start an animation if it's not the current one, and do nothing if it's already current and playing?

In 3.4's docs, it's unclear to me if `current_animation` is cleared when the animation pauses or is stopped (but I think it must be cleared when the animation ends, otherwise it would be the same as `assigned_animation`). `is_playing()` is also ambiguous in 3.4, but your doc updates show it means the animation is actively moving forward. Would it be...

```gdscript
var name = "animation_i_want"
if player.assigned_animation != name:
  # switch and start
  player.start(name)
elif player.is_playing():
  # currently playing the anim already, so do nothing
  pass
elif player.current_animation_position <= 0:
  # stopped, but already on the correct anim, so start it
  # but would this even work, or would current_animation_position be null since is_playing() is false?
  player.start(name)
else:
  # we're not assigned a different anim, not playing, and not at the start, so we must be paused on the correct anim
  player.resume()
```

I'm actually having a hard time figuring out how you would do this. There's got to be a better way that I'm missing.
author: goblinJoel

body:
> After this change, what will be the best way to replay an animation if it's currently finished, resume an animation if paused, switch to and start an animation if it's not the current one, and do nothing if it's already current and playing?

Desired Action | Command
-- | --
Replay an animation if it's currently finished  | `player.start()`
Resume an animation if paused | `player.resume()`
Switch to and start an animation if it's not the current one | `player.start("animation_i_want")`
Do nothing if it's already current and playing | `pass`

> In 3.4's docs, it's unclear to me if current_animation is cleared when the animation pauses or is stopped (but I think it must be cleared when the animation ends, otherwise it would be the same as assigned_animation). is_playing() is also ambiguous in 3.4, but your doc updates show it means the animation is actively moving forward.

`current_animation` and `assigned_animation` are not changed by this PR. `current_animation` is a runtime parameter that is controlled and used by the Godot engine. If an animation is playing, it contains the name of the animation. When the animation is playing, it has the same value as `assigned_animation`. Ignore this property. Use `assigned_animation` to control the current animation assigned to the player. `assigned_animation` is a user parameter that can be used to assign the desired animation without changing whether or not the player is currently playing. It's normally used in the editor to set which animation to play.

Note:
```
player.start("animation_i_want")
```
is the same as
```
player.assigned_animation = "animation_i_want"
player.start()
```

> Would it be...

I would suggest:
```
var name = "animation_i_want"
# Ensure player is not paused
player.resume()
if player.assigned_animation != name or not player.is_playing():
  # Start desired animation
  player.start(name)
```
author: madmiraal

body: Thanks for the thorough response! That technique you suggest doesn't seem too bad. Would you mind adding info to your docs about what happens if you pause an animation that is already paused or resume one that is already playing, never started, or finished?

body:
Updated the documentation for `pause` to:
> Pauses the currently playing animation. To resume the animation again use [method resume]. To stop and reset an animation use [method stop].
[b]Note:[/b] Has no effect if the animation is not playing. See [method is_playing]

and `resume` to:
> Resumes an animation that was paused. See [method pause].
[b]Note:[/b] Has no effect if the animation is not paused. It will not start an animation that was not playing or is finished. See [method start].
author: madmiraal

body:
I'd also be interested to hear the response to feedback that "you can now pause any node, so this may be excessive".

Specifically, what does this provide that node pausing does not?

If the node is paused, does it also freeze animation playback? (Do some forms of pausing cause animations to skip frames?)
author: lyuma

body: As more of a Godot novice, I would feel like using a generic node-pausing function (that I assume is like turning off processing?) is a hack for pausing an animation. I would expect that to have some sort of consequence besides pausing playback, since I'm not really thinking about pausing the player node so much as the animation it's currently playing. So having a pause method on the player to pause the animation is more reassuring. Plus I'm more likely to find it when looking through the animation player docs wondering how to pause.
author: goblinJoel

body:
> Plus I'm more likely to find it when looking through the animation player docs wondering how to pause.

This can simply be addressed in the description section of the node's documentation.
author: YuriSizov

body:
As for "I would expect that to have some sort of consequence besides pausing playback," if the docs specifically state that it doesn't, would that assuage your concern?

Is pausing recursive?
author: lyuma

body:
From the PR description, the original poster probably has some thoughts on that and has thought about it more. But as for myself...

I took a quick look at #46191, and it sounds like pausing in that context is basically the same as disabling processing. I didn't see at a glance whether pausing is applied to a single node (and any children, based on their process mode), or if it's global. I guess it must be per-node, otherwise the settings don't make much sense?

If the correct way to pause an animation was to stop processing on the AnimationPlayer (via the generic pause functionality), it would definitely have to be documented inside AnimationPlayer. Otherwise, why would one think that the right way to pause playback on an animation was to pause processing of the player and all its children (unless individually set not to inherit)?

If the docs specifically state that there aren't any consequences of pausing the AnimationPlayer node aside from pausing playback, and that that's the way you're supposed to do it, that would help my concern. But I do still think the concept of pausing an animation should be separate from pausing gameplay or node processing. It does sound like it's recursive by default, too, and whether that's a bug or a feature for someone who wants to pause an animation will vary.
author: goblinJoel

body:
We discussed this briefly in a PR review meeting.

We want to make sure we get this properly reviewed by all folks currently active on animation APIs (CC @godotengine/animation). There's also a lot of movement around AnimatedSprite right now from @Mickeon @dalexeev, and a lot of conflicting proposals for different parts of the various animation components.

I'm generally in favor of this proposal (splitting the current `play` and `stop` with boolean traps into four methods), but we need to make sure that we have a clear agreement and a clean API for 4.0 without remaining design issues that will lead to tons more proposal to change specific behaviors (e.g., without having looked at details, how does the new `resume()` work with backwards playing?).
author: akien-mga

body: Seems like https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/44345#issuecomment-809511102 is still valid here.
author: KoBeWi

body: Thanks for bringing this to my attention. Not a fan of changing `play()`'s behaviour, not a bit. Its current behaviour reduces code redundancy, by making sure the same animation isn't reset every time. It makes it much simpler and tidier to set up animation code, even if it's technically "improper".
author: Mickeon

body: I am rather in favor of implementing `pause()`. Perhaps a `stop()` after `pause()` would reset the playback position, which is the expected behavior, but I think we should make some mockup diagrams and do a poll on how to combine this with reverse playback.
author: TokageItLab

body:
`stop()` is a very strong verb, and typically encompasses `pause()` already. It should imply pausing **and** resetting the animation back to 0 outright, which is typically the more common behavior, too.

I am in favour of adding `pause()`, which would simply be a tidy equivalent of setting `playback_active` to **false** (like **CanvasItem**'s `hide()`). I do **not** see the point of introducing `resume()`. It makes things needlessly verbose, because as I've mentioned, I'm not in favour of changing `play()`'s current behavior, and if that were to be the case, with no parameter passed, it would behave exactly like a theoretical `resume()` would.

The last dilemma to figure out for me, and honestly this one surprises me, is the fact that AnimationPlayer's `play()` allows setting custom_speed, but why? `playback_speed` already exists, what is the necessity for it, is it a shorthand?
author: Mickeon

body:
![](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/47700418/191675596-1d7e0b64-79c6-4bac-bcf5-1aceb0717333.png)

I also think that `pause()` should be the equivalent of `playing = false`, which is point 1 in the picture. But there is room for discussion about `stop()`. Which of the options (2 or 3) should be the default?

`resume()` looks unnecessary since `play()` can be called with no arguments. But it seems to me that we need to either remove the `backwards` parameter altogether (since `speed_scale` can be negative), or split the method into `play()` and `play_backwards()` (like in `AnimationPlayer`), since calling `play()` with no arguments means calling with `backwards = false`.
author: dalexeev

body:
> But there is room for discussion about stop(). Which of the options (2 or 3) should be the default?

I don't see any room for discussion, myself. If I could go back in time, I would set it like option 3. There's no reason it should behave like option 2, and if there is, it feels like an exception to the norm.

Think of the way your typical playback software _(and hardware)_ works. There is **Play**/**Pause**, and then there is **Stop**:
- **Play** plays the playback;
- **Pause** pauses playback, if it is playing;
- **Stop** pauses playback and restarts it from the beginning.


Here's **Vegas Pro**'s GUI:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66727710/191689658-f84a6401-c4e9-4ff1-b60a-019604bace62.png)

Here's **FLStudio12**'s GUI _(and I just tried, it works **exactly** as I've described above)_.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66727710/191693956-4f364e44-e081-4110-9999-2e3b92a7bad3.png)

Here's a stock image from a generic remote: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66727710/191694162-58ee2032-bda8-43be-ab68-58e3d3a3e3f7.png)

Aseprite does something interesting: by default, it toggles between **Play**/**Pause**, but it is possible to enable toggling between **Play**/**Stop**, instead. So, even with a layout and naming mix-up, the behaviour is essentially the same.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66727710/191695090-df9d3c95-d173-4aa8-b878-48e096a7e456.png)


Mind everyone, it is fine to stray away from conventions, but the further you do, and the more spread these conventions are, the more difficult it becomes for users to adapt.
author: Mickeon

body:
What if we make:

1, 2: `pause(reset_frame_timeout: bool = false)`;
3: `stop()`?

It makes more sense to me.

author: timothyqiu

body: Shouldn't the oblique near plane be a `Plane` type instead of a normal and position?
author: aaronfranke

body:
> Shouldn't the oblique near plane be a `Plane` type instead of a normal and position?

In the `visual_server` this is true, and how it is implemented in the 'camera' representation there.
But in the editor it is more relevant to associate the plane as a normal and position as a traditional Plane has the value `d` along with a normal which is the distance of the plane from the origin, whereas for this camera its `global_transform` is the origin, and we need the global position of the plane to calculate the relevant camera-space `d` value.
author: qaptoR

body:
I've wanted to add this to the camera for a while now to do this very effect, so I'm glad someone's implementing this. Exposing it as oblique clipping planes seems like it should be sufficent enough for the actual effect, but yeah, seeing a demo of a working implementation of portals/mirrors would help sell this.

The only other thing that I would suggest should be considered as further features might be a way to manipulate the other clipping planes too. The reason you may want control over the other clipping planes too is that if you're looking through a mirror/portal, while you're essentially placing a flipped fake camera mirroring you own camera behind it, and the camera frustum is much wider than the confine of the portal, you actually want to be able to cull everything outside of the confines of the rectangle you're rendering through. This shouldn't affect the ability to actually create the effect itself, but being able to clip unwanted geometry would likely make the effect more optimized. I'm not sure, but it might even be necessary to give a way to handle the view and clipping frustum seperately. You may also want a way to override its position for other forms of occlusion culling too (the room/portal system, ect.), since the portal camera will be behind the portal, but as far as occlusion tests go, you would want to consider it aligned with the portal plane for the most efficent render.

Just wanted to my thoughts on this since too. I'm glad you're implementing this. A bunch of us really want to make use of this feature too.
author: SaracenOne
qaptoR

body: Hmm, yeah, that sounds about right. So, I'll suggest to continue the PR as is, and maybe one thing I could do is look a bit more into the idea of exposing the full camera matrix, but target it strictly as a future 4.x feature rather than encouraging broadening the scope of this PR too much. Figuring out the math for oblique projection is valuable either way.

I just want to point out the people who did the heavy lifting when it comes to this PR.
I mention them here with links to their work: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/2713#issuecomment-966076554

Igor Kosyanenko, who attempted this before me but got stuck when there was warping in the portal image. EDIT: The fix for that is the FOV of the skybox should be the same as the portal camera (which should be the same as the player camera); this is a short video showing the problem, and the solution: https://youtu.be/hchttF-iN7Y

Eric Lengyel, whose name you'll actually find in the `Camera_Matrix::set_oblique()` function, since it's his mathematical research which Igor founded his work on.

I'm merely carrying the torch by continuing the work and stitching it all together, because I needed it for my game project, and hopefully it will help others until such a time as there is a better solution.

body:
not sure what the next step is. I can see a button marked "ready for review".
However there is one function `'camera::set_oblique_plane_from_transform'` which was intended to be a straightforward call to generate the oblique plane for a given mesh, using the forward normal as the automatically chosen normal.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40372043/187094505-88cfb0fa-38d6-418d-951c-eb8e2b396055.png)
fig 1: plane-mesh, showing surface-perpendicular normal is upward (green) vector.

However, in this image, while using the XR demo of another contributor to test the camera for mirrors I realized that based on how a plane-mesh instantiates, it is actually the 'upward normal', or 'y component' of the basis which should be used as the default.

Looking into why I chose the forward normal, I can see that it was because for godot 3.4, a quad-mesh was essentially a plane-mesh, but instantiated 'upright' with the forward normal perpendicular to the surface, and so was the default choice of mesh for a portal/mirror.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40372043/187094782-0a746e63-09e4-472f-a9a7-4b3774824a25.png)
fig 2: Quad mesh in default instantiation pose

Since godot 4.0 has removed the quadmesh as an option (likely for redundancy), I propose the function in question be changed  for both 3.x and master PR (but I bring it up here since it really must be done in master regardless) to use the `basis.get_colum(1)` instead of `get_column(2)`.

In addition to this, it might be a good idea to change `Alt + F (Fold Line)` and `Alt + Ctrl + F (Go to Function)` default shortcuts to something else. `Alt + Letter` and `AltGr + Letter` (which is equivalent to `Alt + Ctrl` on Windows) input combinations are common, and probably should be avoided for the text editor.

author: bruvzg

body: Do you think we could do this in a way where we still have one function, even if it requires a boolean or extra field in InputEvent? I am afraid that having an extra function like this may be very confusing when looking at the API.
author: reduz

body:
> Do you think we could do this in a way where we still have one function,

Like calling `unhandled_key_input` twice (with an extra pass_nr argument) instead of adding new `unhandled_unicode_input`? Yes, this might be more clear variant.

With the single `unhandled_key_input` pass, I do not think so. The issue is in call order:

- CodeEdit receives `unhandled_key_input` first, we can't handle input here, since shortcuts are not processed (and there's no clear way to determine if there's any conflicting shortcuts that will trigger).
- Menu receives `unhandled_key_input` and processes shortcuts.
- Only after this, we can process remaining input, so another pass is necessary.

There's nothing special about `InputEvent` with the special character and one without it, just different Unicode values, so the extra field won't help. The only difference is there's a shortcut which is conflicting or not.
author: bruvzg

body: As discussed on IRC. I think if we want shortcuts to happen before, it seems like a cleaner solution to have a `shortcut_input()` call and then do `unhandled_key_input`. This would also help clean up that the later can receive shortcuts, which is pretty hacky.
author: reduz

body:
Updated as discussed. Now, unhandled input is processed in the following order:

- `shortcut_input` (`Key` or `Shortcut` events, handled shortcuts)
- `unhandled_input` (all events)
- `unhandled_key_input` (`Key` events, handled Unicode input with modifiers)

body:
Looks pretty good!

One thing we need to define style-wise is whether we want to enforce a trailing comma on multi-line list initializations, like we try to do for enum definitions and some other existing list initializations.

Personally I'm in favor of it as it makes things more consistent, and makes it easy to add new items when relevant. But I don't have a very strong opinion and I'm also fine with keeping this as is if others prefer it so.
author: akien-mga

body:
> One thing we need to define style-wise is whether we want to enforce a trailing comma on multi-line list initializations, like we try to do for enum definitions and some other existing list initializations.

I guess,we can leave it as it and enforce a definite style across everywhere once we have it.

body:
> For example: here,in a single file there is both trailing comma and not trailing comma.

Yes, because `_MAX` enum always mark the end of the enum, so there will never be any addition after that. But for other enums with `_MAX`, they should end with a trailing comma.

body:
tl;dr: see PR #56766

The issue comes from the fact the `editor` module is an empty shim given we only declare `editor.template_builders`. This is fine with Python>3.7, but Python 3.6 doesn't play along

With Python 3.6.8
```python
>>> import editor
>>> dir(editor)
['__doc__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', '__spec__']   # No template_builders field, this is normal
>>> import editor.template_builders   # import works
>>> editor.template_builders  # but access doesn't given it is resolved as `getattr(editor, 'template_builders')`
*** AttributeError: module 'editor' has no attribute 'template_builders'
>>> import editor.template_builders as tb  # this also doesn't work :(
*** AttributeError: module 'editor' has no attribute 'template_builders'
```

With Python3.7.9
```python
>>> import editor
>>> dir(editor)
['__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', '__spec__']
>>> import editor.template_builders
>>> editor.template_builders  # Same getattr as in Python 3.6
*** AttributeError: module 'editor' has no attribute 'template_builders'
>>> import editor.template_builders as tb  # This work given it is a lookup into `sys.modules`
>>> dir(tb)
['StringIO', '__builtins__', '__cached__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__spec__', 'make_templates', 'os', 'parse_template', 'subprocess_main']
```

A solution is to use the "from ... import ..." statement instead of the import:
so `import editor.template_builders as build_template_gd` becomes `from editor import template_builders as build_template_gd`
(this way Python find the module by doing a lookup with the `editor.template_builders` instead of potentially first finding `editor` module, then accessing it `template_builders` attribute)

However given nothing prevent from using the wrong import style, we could end up with this bug reappearing in the future :/

The even simpler solution is to consider Python 3.6 is end of life since 23/12/2021. So we could update to `EnsurePythonVersion(3.7)` at the start of `SConstruct` and be done with it.
But given this is end of life occurred fairly recently (and as @Calinou showed, ubuntu 18.04 - which is really common on CI - is still using it) I'm not sure it is the correct solution 😄 

Finally the more violent solution is to further improve the `_helper_module` method to create the parent modules (and update them so that they contain children modules as attributes) along the way. I've implemented this as PR #56766  ;-)
