body:
NoiseTexture3D is probably best done in another PR. 

Overall, I am torn about adding FastNoise ([despite the fact that I was the one who originally proposed it](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/22337)). 

I love the idea of having a noise library integrated into Godot. It is great for procedural systems, generative art, and for making cool VFX. 

At the same time, FastNoise exposes a ton of detailed handles which makes it very complex and daunting for end-users. One of Godot's goals is to keep things simple and easy to comprehend for the average user. As a result we often sacrifice control over minor details in order to keep things comprehensible. 

To me, FastNoise sits on the border of value and complexity. On the one hand, I think it is valuable, but on the other hand it introduces significant complexity (and another 3rd party dependency). 

After typing all this out, I am finding myself more supportive of merging than not. Especially if we could somehow replace OpenSimplexNoise entirely, then the benefits would more clearly outweigh the added complexity.
author: clayjohn

body:
If not merged into core, it should be an officially endorsed plugin (like what was proposed for VehicleBody some time back)

Even if it *does* add some complexity, it gives users a LOT a LOT of power and the ability to easily achieve/port effects seen in e.g. shadertoy.
author: Zireael07

body: I don't think Fast Noise is that complex, anyone that plays around with it a little will be able to quickly find their way around and it's not something where you can unintentionally be creating possible artifacts that wouldn't be clearly visible in the preview and that occur only in specific situations.
author: mrjustaguy

body:
@clayjohn 
> FastNoise exposes a ton of detailed handles which makes it very complex and daunting for end-users. One of Godot's goals is to keep things simple and easy to comprehend for the average user. As a result we often sacrifice control over minor details in order to keep things comprehensible.

GDScript is complex and daunting, as are C# and C++. Yet they are all natively supported languages. The capabilities do not detract from the stated goal. 

I would agree with you if the noise system was like, say Zylann's original voxel system where you had to get several variables set up properly to get it working. Otherwise it gave zero results. (It was fine later once we set it up with defaults and documentation.) But that's not the case in my PR, and I assume about this one. They work with default values as OpenSimplexNoise does.

Having a noise library with a bunch of options that all provide visual feedback does not require the user to understand and comprehend what everything does. They can push a bunch of buttons and get direct visual feedback as to how that changes the end result. That's far more user-friendly than half the things in the engine right now. 

e.g. Looking through the VisualServer, I can see it would not be merged if it required `simplicity` and `comprehensibility` for the average user. Same thing with Environment, or your PhysicalSky. Does the average user know what Mie, Turbidity, or Rayleigh are? A better metric is `useability` for the average user, which both noise PRs provide.

> Especially if we could somehow replace OpenSimplexNoise entirely, then the benefits would more clearly outweigh the added complexity.

Perlin and now simplex ([The patent expired 2022-01-08!](https://patents.google.com/patent/US6867776B2/en)) are suitable replacements for OSN. However due to class names and the like, you'll have to make a breaking decision to deprecate and remove OSN. 4.0 is a good time to do that.

> Overall, I am torn about adding FastNoise ([despite the fact that I was the one who originally proposed it]
...
> After typing all this out, I am finding myself more supportive of merging than not. 

Yes, so now at least three people have put in a lot of work to make your request possible. Us two and Zylann. And we haven't even received the courtesy of rejection, or rejection for reasons. Silence after completing the request is neither community-building nor contributor-encouraging.

So how about this. You were the original champion. I propose you take up that again and get a firm decision from the dev team on these questions:
* Do you want to support multiple noise libraries in any capacity? Right now OSN _is_ NoiseTexture. There is no abstract concept of Noise and NoiseTexture that can allow something other than OSN. There is zero support for 3rd party modules or plugins. To get a module to work, a gamedev has to hack up the engine. Case in point: https://github.com/tinmanjuggernaut/godot_fastnoise_simd#preparation
* Do you want fastnoise in core or only as a plugin or module?
* Do you want to replace OpenSimplexNoise since it is redundant with Fastnoise?

Then we could insert smaller, more digestible PRs such as the following.
1. Noise - abstract class to separate Noise from OpenSimplexNoise
2. FastNoise/Lite - to add to, or replace OSN
3. NoiseTexture3D

If the first is accepted, which allows support for modules and plugins, there are at least three additional noise modules available to users: fastnoise, fastnoise_simd, and fastnoise_lite, and room to convert them to plugins. OSN also has a new version with new algorithms one might be interested in implementing.
author: TokisanGames

body:
Really love the capabilities this PR seem to have. Incredible work, It looks epic! I really feel like it's something Godot has lacked, and I definitely think it should be in the core! 

@clayjohn. One possibility to make it easier for new users and simpler use cases would be to expose two variants of the noise as different resource types. One "simple" with a lot of the functionality hidden and set to default values basically filling the place of the old OpenSimplexNoise, but using the same code base and thus saving 2000 lines of code. Then expose a "flexible" variant with the full feature set and a lot of options for more demanding use cases.
author: albinaask

body:
> One possibility to make it easier for new users and simpler use cases would be to expose two variants of the noise as different resource types. One "simple" with a lot of the functionality hidden and set to default values basically filling the place of the old OpenSimplexNoise, but using the same code base and thus saving 2000 lines of code. Then expose a "flexible" variant with the full feature set and a lot of options for more demanding use cases.

This sounds like a good idea on paper. One issue is that to convert from "simple" to "advanced" noise without recreating the resource in the editor, it will require implementing a bespoke resource conversion tool. It is possible to make such a tool (see BaseMaterial3D's **Convert to ShaderMaterial** functionality in the inspector toolbox), but it isn't easy to discover for users.

~~Still, I see at least one way to condense the current selection of properties exposed to the user. The `seamless` and `seamless_blend_skirt` properties can be merged in one, automatically enabling seamless mode if `seamless_blend_skirt` is greater than 0.0. Then the `seamless` property can be removed.~~

**Edit:** The above is problematic for custom noise implementations (assuming OpenSimplexNoise is removed from core when this PR is merged). This is because custom noise implementations can have proper seamless support instead of relying on a blend skirt.
author: Calinou

body:
Updated this PR to fix some issues with properties, clean them up and find good default values which come close to OpenSimplexNoise defaults:

![grafik](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50084500/149430971-6a612ce8-1cee-4a74-9e76-db8c5f354f89.png)

@clayjohn I made sure that all properties that have no effect for a specific (fractal) noise type or with a specific combination are hidden, making it quite easy to use despite having lots of detailed handles. Give it a try :)

OpenSimplexNoise is now removed.
As discussed here and via chat, it makes sense to replace OpenSimplexNoise with FastNoiseLite. Other noise types can still be added via extending the Noise interface. The only feature missing in FastNoiseLite is generating 4-dimensional noise and therefore "true" seamless noise, but the new blending algorithm works very well in most cases. But for better tileable noise support (also in 3d) something like https://github.com/Xrayez/godot-anl could be used (ported to GDExtension).
author: Geometror

body:
@albinaask @Calinou I don't think it is necessary to introduce two resource types. As it was already said, it is not that complex to work with :) 
The `seamless_blend_skirt` property is only used when a custom noise resource does not implement seamless noise itself and relies on the fallback implementation. We could add a virtual method to the `Noise` interface to get that capability and hide the property accordingly.
author: Geometror
clayjohn

body:
@clayjohn I don't think this would make it less complex since domain warp can be used with every other noise type, fractal also together with cellular, etc. It might be possible, but they are simply not designed to be used in a seperate manner like you suggested.
Regarding the exposed noise types: 
Although Value cubic/Simplex Smooth give visually better results, they compute much slower than their simple counterparts. In my opinion the we should enable the enduser to make that decision.
However, we could think about renaming some properties: For example, maybe "distortion" is more descriptive than domain warp. "Value Fast" for "Value"/"Value Quality" or "Value Smooth" instead of "Value Cubic".

author: Geometror

body: @Geometror I think there should be a get_noise_3d() function because that enables the smooth evolution of a noise texture using time as the 3rd dimension.
author: npetrangelo

body: @npetrangelo This function is already present as part of the fastnoise_lite resource, but my question was rather about which operations a custom noise resource must implement mandatorily. Having a get_nosie_3d method in the noise interface would mean that every custom noise generator needs to implement it, even if it makes no sense/is not possible. But on the other hand, these noise generators could just project the 3d space to a 2d plane or implement some other fallback (this behavior could also be controlled via a property), which would make the interface more versatile. This, however, raises the question of where to draw the line: Should get_nosie_4d/5d/6d also be a part of the noise interface?
author: Geometror

body:
@Geometror for noise types that only support 2D, the 3D noise functions could just return the 2D noise value with z ignored. And then it should be noted in the docs which noise types do and don't support 3D noise.

I think having 4D noise would be good, especially if fastnoise lite already has it. It could be used for 3D noise + time. 5D and 6D noise seems unnecessary though, I wouldn't include them.
author: fire-forge
Geometror

body:
Completed the documentation, I am open for improvement suggestions as describing noise algorithms in an easy and understandable manner can be quite difficult :)

Unfortunately, adding a get_noise_4d() function to the interface would require either gluing some code on top of FastNoiseLite from the legacy FastNoise library (this would only be possible for simplex noise) or providing a dummy implementation which would just project the 4d space onto a 3d space and use the already present 3d function.
The first option involves quite some work since the library wasn't designed for 4d noise and the second one could be confusing for someone who expects true 4d noise, so I think we should stick to the get_noise_1d/2d/3d functions for now. Maybe 4d noise gets added to FastNoiseLite in the future.
author: Geometror

body: I included the suggestions and did some additional tweaks regarding the documentation.

author: akien-mga

body: It might be useful to have the ability to run FastNoise on a Thread instead of running on the Main Thread, so that games don't stall when used too much, and also so that Projects using Noise Materials don't take a bit to load when large noise textures are used in the project, but instead just load (even if without textures for a little bit)
author: mrjustaguy

body:
> It might be useful to have the ability to run FastNoise on a Thread instead of running on the Main Thread, so that games don't stall when used too much, and also so that Projects using Noise Materials don't take a bit to load when large noise textures are used in the project, but instead just load (even if without textures for a little bit)

Textures are generated fast enough to animate, though if you want a real dynamic noise texture it should be implemented as a shader. Otherwise if you're generating a noise texture so you can use it in a material or terrain, you can't do anything with it until the texture is generated, regardless of which thread it's rendered on. I don't think multithreaded is necessary, IMO.


@Geometror @clayjohn How about a version for 3.x? I had made a branch for both versions.
Edit: Godot 3 needs to maintain opensimplexnoise of course, but Noise and FNL can be added.
author: TokisanGames

body:
I didn't mean this for dynamic noise textures. The problem is with running on main thread, it freezes the project until generation of the noise is done, and there are MANY cases where you want to generate while the rest of the project is running, without causing stutters or freezes while it's generating... 
The thing is you can tell it to generate noise textures at start up, but not actually need them until much later, so it's stupid to force long loading at start or when texture is actually needed when you can hide it away by doing it on a thread.. and in games, if you dump someone into something too early, it's very much preferable that a texture isn't loaded compared to a freeze/stutter, so yes, allowing the option to run this on a Thread would significantly help the smoothness of applications using it.

Another option would be allowing conversion to an image texture and storing the final Noise texture for uses as materials in the end product... Really both would have their uses to improve the experience for the users (dev and end users) in different ways.
author: mrjustaguy

body:
@mrjustaguy The noise generation is already offloaded to its own thread when using `NoiseTexture`. If you want to use any noise generator directly you could create a thread yourself and just call it from there (I guess, but I am not too familiar with the Godot threading API).

@tinmanjuggernaut As you said, OpenSimplexNoise needs to be kept in Godot3 since FastNoiseLite does not provide 4D noise yet. As having several noise generators in core is not desired (decided in earlier discussions), the only option would be to backport the Noise interface, which might be doable without breaking compatibility.
author: Geometror

body: @Geometror upon further testing I see that that specifically isn't the issue, as a larger texture (that takes longer to make) stutters before applying (seemed like texture generation to me for the texture size I used in the original testing), so generation isn't the issue, it's moving it to VRAM in one go that stutters... so Texture Streaming is actually what would be needed for this to not cause a stutter/micro freeze...
author: mrjustaguy

body:
@mrjustaguy If you are stuttering when creating a noise texture that is not displaying on screen, perhaps you have properly identified the cause. But if you're creating a texture in a material and displaying it on screen, then the engine is halting while it compiles the shader. Blocking shader compilation is a rampant problem in 3.x. It has received a bandaid in 3.5 https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/53411, but isn't perfect.

@Geometror I wrote the noise interface for 3.2 and 4 so we could provide for many noise types. Since the devs decided they do want noise, at the least, the interface should be added IMO.
author: TokisanGames

body:
> @Geometror @clayjohn How about a version for 3.x? I had made a branch for both versions.
Edit: Godot 3 needs to maintain opensimplexnoise of course, but Noise and FNL can be added.

I don't have any issue with adding new noise types to 3.x so long as we can avoid any compatibility breakage.
author: clayjohn

body:
Ok, then here is the plan: I will do a subsequent PR with some improvements I had in mind, also incorporating the feedback we get in the following weeks.
Ideas/planned changes:
- Maybe bring back 4d noise and add it to the interface [I previously said this might require too much work but I will do a bit of experimenting, it might be not that bad]
- Supporting float texture formats (FORMAT_RF, FORMAT_RGBAF)
- Some code refactoring
After we got everything right, then we could decide whether or how backporting this is viable/desired.

author: clayjohn

body:
> It might be useful to have the ability to run FastNoise on a Thread instead of running on the Main Thread, so that games don't stall when used too much, and also so that Projects using Noise Materials don't take a bit to load when large noise textures are used in the project, but instead just load (even if without textures for a little bit)

@mrjustaguy IMO it should not be the job of the noise class to offload work to a thread. It should be done with a helper eventually, or a job system of some kind that can offload basically anything to said thread(s).

I also figured out that if I drop my own `FastNoiseLite` implementation for Godot's, and later move to GDExtension, the cost of calling it is going to go way up. The reason is, calls to `get_noise` from an extension have much more indirection, and cannot be inlined.
There usually one way to workaround slow calls (here pseudocode):
```
void generate_noise_series(const Vector3 *input_positions, int input_count, float *output_noise)
```
However, no Variant type allows to do this. `PackedVector` types use CoW so return by reference is not possible, and passing + returning just to avoid the allocation is a bit awkward (not to mention I need to preallocate the vector somewhere in my library exclusively for Godot, which probably can't be thread_local, contrary to what I usually use).
In addition, I was exposing domain warp as its own thing (allows to warp XYZ coordinates with just one noise call), but won't be able to do that. So I might have to keep and prefix my version...

Another thing, more on usability side, I have this in my implementation:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311555/161158596-1cd29679-d7f7-4994-9da8-4498ef3a780a.png)
My use case does not involve textures at all, so it is really useful to have a direct preview of the noise right above it. It's even fast enough to not need a thread. Apart from `FastNoiseLiteGradient`, it's directly usable for Godot: https://github.com/Zylann/godot_voxel/blob/master/editor/fast_noise_lite/fast_noise_lite_editor_plugin.cpp, although it requires the noise to properly emit the `changed` signal when properties are changed, or being told so by the inspector.

author: mrjustaguy

body:
> How does it look in motion?

In motion, SDFGI fade-in is quite noticeable indoors by default (but it's OK outdoors):

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/180032/149562178-7c63217c-f28a-4bec-a513-f3c2dc0b9751.mp4

However, you can improve the appearance of indoor SDFGI fade-in (at the cost of outdoor appearance) by exploiting the fact that [Environment ambient light settings can be adjusted independently of SDFGI ambient light settings](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/55104).

Here's an example with Sky Contribution decreased to 0.25. Indoor fade-in becomes much less noticeable, but outdoor appearance is worse:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/180032/149562285-0be4a33f-f282-48be-9bb3-83a8480ab8ab.mp4

In outdoor scenes, fog can be used to make SDFGI fade-in less noticeable. If you are OK with having coarser GI, you can also increase Min Cell Size to increase the area covered by SDFGI.

body:
This definitely ameliorates the issue to the point it's really subtle most of the time, but on very rare occasions the accumulated speed is way bigger than the actual mouse movement:

For example, worse case scenario, if the last reported `event.speed` was let's say ~200 pixels, I can still move the cursor 2 pixels and get a ~50 pixels reported distance, even after 0.3-0.5s of wait time.

Waiting anything longer than 3s fully resets the speed as expected.

--- 

IMO this is a good change and can be merged as is, the following is more like proposal material:

I think the issue can be fully solved by allowing users to not use accumulation at all, especially now that `event.speed` reports precise float values for Linux & Windows, and I believe it's there just for movement jitter compensation on platforms like Mac?

Maybe by exposing `min_ref_frame` and `max_ref_frame` and allow users to set them to 0 we could achieve a more accurate mouse tracking.

---

Many thanks for tackling this issue @madmiraal !
author: ghost

body:
I made a recording with a "fake cursor" trying to mimic the real's cursor movement with `event.speed`. Because of that it won't be "linear" like the OS cursor (`event.relative` would achieve that)... but it's good enough to visualize what I wrote in my previous comment.

Please ignore the recording artifacts, they're part of the Linux experience. :)
 
https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9743949/149637991-9b0abbe3-faee-45a2-b765-bc231aee654c.mp4

So here you can see:
1. When doing a circular motion, the resulting motion is polygonal instead. If I'm doing a very fast motion it looks like a rotating line. I think that's because of the 0.1s lag?
2. The residual speed is still there. I also think it only appears less worse than the actual recorded speed values (because of multiplying with `delta`). When visualizing the speed with a motion vector or logging the values, it is obvious that it's still way off.
3. Sometimes the movement barely reflects on the fake cursor. At first I thought this might be because I collect the speed from Input events, and then I update the cursor position inside `_process` (because I have to multiply by the frame `delta`) ...  but it doesn't happen when using `event.relative`, so I think it might be a valid bug report.
author: ghost

body:
I ran a test using `min_ref_frame=0.01` & `max_ref_frame=0.1`, since setting them to 0 would result in `event.speed` returning NaN values.
The tracking I got was pretty similar to `event.relative` and this reminded me that Godot doesn't actually read the mouse values as given by the sensor (and I'm not aware of any game engine doing that either), so I guess there's not much more that can be done here.
author: ghost

body:
@ceLoFaN See also godotengine/godot-proposals#3796 (and its implementation #56789). The mouse doesn't report its speed just its movement; so `event.speed` is just `Input.get_last_mouse_speed()`. Furthermore, `event.speed` may miss the last update, because it's within the 0.1 s. So (with this PR) it's better to call `Input.get_last_mouse_speed()` when you need it e.g. in `_process()`, which also allows the last movements to be applied and speed to be reset to `(0, 0)` within 0.2 s.

It's also worth noting that godotengine/godot-proposals#552 would allow users to more accurately calculate velocity, acceleration, etc. from `event.relative`.

body:
This PR is ready for review! There are a few gui/theme issues I can't figure out, but these aren't blocking issues and fixing it could increase the scope too much (for me at least), so I think it's better if they are be fixed later.

- Good way to implement padding (and other things) in AcceptDialog, see https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3832
- Labels in list view could be better, see: #56915
- On popup, sizing/positioning is sometimes wrong, probably related to some internal sizing override.
- Currently all arrow keys are redirected to the results container to allow keyboard selection in grid view. I think it's better that way, but perhaps there is a way to switch focus off of the label and only then redirect the input.

I also looked into making the dialog a singleton. It makes some sense given that we allocate all these nodes for the results on startup. However, that only works if only one object subscribed to the quick open signal at any moment.

- The place holding the main contents should be the dark area, but instead it's the bottom part.
- The icon for changing the view mode is at the bottom, when it would better placed to the right of the search bar.
- The text of the items in list mode seem to be truncated for no good reason.
- I think the bottom text should rather be a tooltip when hovering an item.
- Not really a big fan of windows resizing by themselves.
- There are no borders.

body:
> What's the status of this?

The new dialog is functional, although there are still some things I don't like in the code. I've already mentioned a few styling issues, and I don't know how to fix them. Any help with theming/UI issues, or code review would be appreciated.
author: stijn-h

body:
> The current looks of this dialog goes very against the overall visual style of the Godot editor:

To me, it's kind of clear that Godot's design might be lacking, which is why I redesigned this dialog in the first place. I designed it precisely without caring about what 'style' Godot has, only caring about what a Quick Open dialog needs.

> * The place holding the main contents should be the dark area, but instead it's the bottom part.

I'm okay with making the results part darker, but I think the bottom part should still be even darker. Here is what it looks like (ignore the white dialog border, I think it happened after rebasing, maybe a theme update?).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/166115628-43b9a6aa-fd48-45ae-a8b2-4f0b4d32ab0d.png)

> * The icon for changing the view mode is at the bottom, when it would better placed to the right of the search bar.

No, that would not be better. It would no longer be a simple search bar, and even on it's own, that place is not a better location. There is free space at the bottom, and putting it there also doesn't break the simple design of search bar + search results. 

> * The text of the items in list mode seem to be truncated for no good reason.

There is a good reason, as I've explained in the post above.

> * I think the bottom text should rather be a tooltip when hovering an item.

No.

> * Not really a big fan of windows resizing by themselves.

I don't think it works well with Godot's separate windows, but I have seen this style work in other software. It's up for debate whether we want to keep this or not. 

> * There are no borders.

So?
author: stijn-h

body:
> To me, it's kind of clear that Godot's design might be lacking, which is why I redesigned this dialog in the first place. I designed it precisely without caring about what 'style' Godot has, only caring about what a Quick Open dialog needs.

If you think Godot's overall UI design needs changes that's completely fine, but this sort of thing needs to be done as a whole and with proper discussion on what said changes should be (the [proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository would be the perfect place for that).

Having a section of the editor look and act differently from the rest just adds confusion to the user, who expects things to behave consistently across the editor.
author: YeldhamDev

body:
> If you think Godot's overall UI design needs changes that's completely fine, but this sort of thing needs to be done as a whole and with proper discussion on what said changes should be (the [proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository would be the perfect place for that).

Top down, bottom up. Both is possible, but I have no interest in getting bogged down in discussions over what 'design rules' Godot should follow. I think it's more effective to try to design a small isolated component, and maybe others can take that further. 

Actually, I think that because of this top down approach, Godot's style (which was supposed to have gotten a complete overhaul in 4.0 btw) has remained so stagnant, even when there is so much room for improvement.

author: realkotob

body:
>I could not reproduce the memory leak when using the new quick open dialog, can you send me a minimum reproduction project or tell me how to reproduce it?

Well, seems like I fixed it after all. Anyway, I should've been more specific. The CI build failed because asan detected memory leaks. You could see it in the list of checks.

<details>
  <summary>Click if you're interested about the issue and the solution</summary>
  
This is the old log: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/actions/runs/3818883371/jobs/6496060823

You can see in the logs that asan reports "direct leaks" on lines where a Label node is allocated in the QuickOpenResult item classes. 

The new dialog works by creating a bunch of results upfront, then hiding them if they are not necessary. But in the CI test, the dialog was never opened, and so the result nodes were never added as a child to either the list view or grid view, and thus none of them were freed. As a fix, I added a deconstructor that frees all the nodes. Still not sure why there were only direct leaks in the label nodes, but maybe Im misunderstanding the tool.
</details>

Anyway, to given an update on what the design currently looks like:
 
List view was changed because I could not get the path next to the filename to work the way I wanted to. This is probably easier to read anyway.
![lijst3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/210186969-49a7976c-e842-4588-9b7e-8a565f00cde5.png)

Grid view now uses FlowContainer, which makes it possible to resize the window and have the columns update automatically. I also removed redirecting the left right arrow key events to the result container, because it interfered with moving the cursor in the searchbox. 
![grid](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/210186970-b511042c-e977-4de0-8910-ae1e4092b227.png)

All the issues I had found myself are solved, and so are I think most other issues that were raised. The PR was already open, but now that the CI has succeeded I think it's possible to review (although it will have to wait for 4.1 I guess).

body:
> is this intended that the dialog is empty when there is no search text? It should just display everything

It will show all possible results at the start if the amount of possible results is below some threshold. If there is tons of files I feel like showing a bunch of them is just noise. We could do something with recently opened files here, but I don't consider that a must have feature.

> the names in grid view are tiny

I think you're using a different editor scale? I've tried to improve the scaling in the latest commit. I think it looks a bit bad at 75% now, so we could stop scaling down from 100%. The text is supposed to be tiny though in grid view (8px at 100%), since there is just not much room for text.

>"Search files by name" is a bit misleading, because it searches by the whole path

It still felt more natural to me than something like "Search files...".

>why the class was moved to an entirely new file?

I renamed the file because I renamed the dialog class to be more consistent with how other dialogs are named. If people think having the git history is more valuable, then I can change it back. I guess if we are going to make the names consistent, this would be a good point to do it, since the whole thing is rewritten.

> I wonder if include_addons shouldn't be toggleable with a button inside the dialog

I personally think this is only useful in edge case scenarios, and it also isn't a must-have feature. But I decided to try and implement in the current design to see how much it would affect the design and code. Maybe people like it this way, or they find it distracting.

body:
> Are you on top of some old version?

No, you can see it in your video. The split mode works like you said. But the icon in the filter files part, which actually has the exact same grid/list view logic as here in this dialog ,does not work that way.

> I just noticed that the new dialog no longer allows to open multiple files. Also opening something instantly makes it more prone to missclicks.

You want a quick open dialog to be fast, and distraction free. You have a file you want to open and want to get there the quickest way. Whether you want to edit a scene, or select a texture for a sprite. Having to click multiple times does the opposite. Look at other code editors and what they do. How realistic are your use cases, and are they worth compromising for?

> Keyboard navigation is broken i grid mode. Up/down move selection left/right, left/right keys have no effect.

It used to be there, I think I removed it after moving to flow container. But it's not broken, because if the grid eats up left/right keys you cant move the cursor in the searchbox anymore. That felt bad to me. Maybe you can do something with focus, but I don't know how to shift focus to the grid and back.

For long file paths, ellipsis (`…`) can be used, with only the end of the path showing. author: Calinou

body:
This PR is ready for review! There are a few gui/theme issues I can't figure out, but these aren't blocking issues and fixing it could increase the scope too much (for me at least), so I think it's better if they are be fixed later.

- Good way to implement padding (and other things) in AcceptDialog, see https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3832
- Labels in list view could be better, see: #56915
- On popup, sizing/positioning is sometimes wrong, probably related to some internal sizing override.
- Currently all arrow keys are redirected to the results container to allow keyboard selection in grid view. I think it's better that way, but perhaps there is a way to switch focus off of the label and only then redirect the input.

I also looked into making the dialog a singleton. It makes some sense given that we allocate all these nodes for the results on startup. However, that only works if only one object subscribed to the quick open signal at any moment.
author: stijn-h

body:
The current looks of this dialog goes very against the overall visual style of the Godot editor:

- The place holding the main contents should be the dark area, but instead it's the bottom part.
- The icon for changing the view mode is at the bottom, when it would better placed to the right of the search bar.
- The text of the items in list mode seem to be truncated for no good reason.
- I think the bottom text should rather be a tooltip when hovering an item.
- Not really a big fan of windows resizing by themselves.
- There are no borders.
author: YeldhamDev

body: What's the status of this? The remarks from the previous comment should be addressed. Also the PR needs rebase.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> What's the status of this?

The new dialog is functional, although there are still some things I don't like in the code. I've already mentioned a few styling issues, and I don't know how to fix them. Any help with theming/UI issues, or code review would be appreciated.
author: stijn-h

body:
> The current looks of this dialog goes very against the overall visual style of the Godot editor:

To me, it's kind of clear that Godot's design might be lacking, which is why I redesigned this dialog in the first place. I designed it precisely without caring about what 'style' Godot has, only caring about what a Quick Open dialog needs.

> * The place holding the main contents should be the dark area, but instead it's the bottom part.

I'm okay with making the results part darker, but I think the bottom part should still be even darker. Here is what it looks like (ignore the white dialog border, I think it happened after rebasing, maybe a theme update?).

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/166115628-43b9a6aa-fd48-45ae-a8b2-4f0b4d32ab0d.png)

> * The icon for changing the view mode is at the bottom, when it would better placed to the right of the search bar.

No, that would not be better. It would no longer be a simple search bar, and even on it's own, that place is not a better location. There is free space at the bottom, and putting it there also doesn't break the simple design of search bar + search results. 

> * The text of the items in list mode seem to be truncated for no good reason.

There is a good reason, as I've explained in the post above.

> * I think the bottom text should rather be a tooltip when hovering an item.

No.

> * Not really a big fan of windows resizing by themselves.

I don't think it works well with Godot's separate windows, but I have seen this style work in other software. It's up for debate whether we want to keep this or not. 

> * There are no borders.

So?
author: stijn-h

body:
> To me, it's kind of clear that Godot's design might be lacking, which is why I redesigned this dialog in the first place. I designed it precisely without caring about what 'style' Godot has, only caring about what a Quick Open dialog needs.

If you think Godot's overall UI design needs changes that's completely fine, but this sort of thing needs to be done as a whole and with proper discussion on what said changes should be (the [proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository would be the perfect place for that).

Having a section of the editor look and act differently from the rest just adds confusion to the user, who expects things to behave consistently across the editor.
author: YeldhamDev

body:
> If you think Godot's overall UI design needs changes that's completely fine, but this sort of thing needs to be done as a whole and with proper discussion on what said changes should be (the [proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository would be the perfect place for that).

Top down, bottom up. Both is possible, but I have no interest in getting bogged down in discussions over what 'design rules' Godot should follow. I think it's more effective to try to design a small isolated component, and maybe others can take that further. 

Actually, I think that because of this top down approach, Godot's style (which was supposed to have gotten a complete overhaul in 4.0 btw) has remained so stagnant, even when there is so much room for improvement.

author: realkotob

body:
>I could not reproduce the memory leak when using the new quick open dialog, can you send me a minimum reproduction project or tell me how to reproduce it?

Well, seems like I fixed it after all. Anyway, I should've been more specific. The CI build failed because asan detected memory leaks. You could see it in the list of checks.

<details>
  <summary>Click if you're interested about the issue and the solution</summary>
  
This is the old log: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/actions/runs/3818883371/jobs/6496060823

You can see in the logs that asan reports "direct leaks" on lines where a Label node is allocated in the QuickOpenResult item classes. 

The new dialog works by creating a bunch of results upfront, then hiding them if they are not necessary. But in the CI test, the dialog was never opened, and so the result nodes were never added as a child to either the list view or grid view, and thus none of them were freed. As a fix, I added a deconstructor that frees all the nodes. Still not sure why there were only direct leaks in the label nodes, but maybe Im misunderstanding the tool.
</details>

Anyway, to given an update on what the design currently looks like:
 
List view was changed because I could not get the path next to the filename to work the way I wanted to. This is probably easier to read anyway.
![lijst3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/210186969-49a7976c-e842-4588-9b7e-8a565f00cde5.png)

Grid view now uses FlowContainer, which makes it possible to resize the window and have the columns update automatically. I also removed redirecting the left right arrow key events to the result container, because it interfered with moving the cursor in the searchbox. 
![grid](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25907608/210186970-b511042c-e977-4de0-8910-ae1e4092b227.png)

All the issues I had found myself are solved, and so are I think most other issues that were raised. The PR was already open, but now that the CI has succeeded I think it's possible to review (although it will have to wait for 4.1 I guess).

author: realkotob

body:
> is this intended that the dialog is empty when there is no search text? It should just display everything

It will show all possible results at the start if the amount of possible results is below some threshold. If there is tons of files I feel like showing a bunch of them is just noise. We could do something with recently opened files here, but I don't consider that a must have feature.

> the names in grid view are tiny

I think you're using a different editor scale? I've tried to improve the scaling in the latest commit. I think it looks a bit bad at 75% now, so we could stop scaling down from 100%. The text is supposed to be tiny though in grid view (8px at 100%), since there is just not much room for text.

>"Search files by name" is a bit misleading, because it searches by the whole path

It still felt more natural to me than something like "Search files...".

>why the class was moved to an entirely new file?

I renamed the file because I renamed the dialog class to be more consistent with how other dialogs are named. If people think having the git history is more valuable, then I can change it back. I guess if we are going to make the names consistent, this would be a good point to do it, since the whole thing is rewritten.

> I wonder if include_addons shouldn't be toggleable with a button inside the dialog

I personally think this is only useful in edge case scenarios, and it also isn't a must-have feature. But I decided to try and implement in the current design to see how much it would affect the design and code. Maybe people like it this way, or they find it distracting.

Yes, forgot to fix it. I don't know if it is big enough, but I tried to make everything a bit bigger across the board. It does make the icons stand out even more, because they are so small (why don't we make them bigger?).

> Also the addons button should have text. Right now it gives false impression that it switches view, while they are 2 separate buttons. The text can be "Include Addons" to be shorter.

I see your point, but it also cuts away space for the file path details. So it would be a compromise either way. I added a separator between the buttons instead, maybe that is good enough.

> Also maybe the layout button should display the current layout mode, like in filesystem. It's more intuitive.

I think it makes more sense this way.

Aside from that I changed the base path in list view to include "res://" again. This creates a more consistent feel with files in the root folder. Otherwise, it felt like these results are not clearly separated, like you can see here: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/56772#issuecomment-1368566007 I haven't heard anyone complain about it, and it is a bit redundant, but I think I prefer it like this.

body:
> Are you on top of some old version?

No, you can see it in your video. The split mode works like you said. But the icon in the filter files part, which actually has the exact same grid/list view logic as here in this dialog ,does not work that way.

> I just noticed that the new dialog no longer allows to open multiple files. Also opening something instantly makes it more prone to missclicks.

You want a quick open dialog to be fast, and distraction free. You have a file you want to open and want to get there the quickest way. Whether you want to edit a scene, or select a texture for a sprite. Having to click multiple times does the opposite. Look at other code editors and what they do. How realistic are your use cases, and are they worth compromising for?

> Keyboard navigation is broken i grid mode. Up/down move selection left/right, left/right keys have no effect.

It used to be there, I think I removed it after moving to flow container. But it's not broken, because if the grid eats up left/right keys you cant move the cursor in the searchbox anymore. That felt bad to me. Maybe you can do something with focus, but I don't know how to shift focus to the grid and back.

Some updates

 - Added back support for page up/down and left right keys. I removed this after using the FlowContainer, but I found a way to do it. The one caveat (still) is that left/right keys are taken by the grid view, so you cant move the search box cursor in grid view. But I think the moving the selection index is preferable.

body:
Is this really needed @bruvzg ? The whole idea with GDExtension is that we can use the classes in Godot much better. For instance to obtain internal Vulkan data needed in extensions we use render device directly and have exposed the required resources there.
I would suggest the same should be done for access to the window handles, where our DisplayServer can be accessed from the extension.

body:
> I've also added a check to `AtlasTexture::set_atlas()` ensuring no cyclic dependency will be created between some `AtlasTexture`s.

I once went down that rabbit hole and there's still a lot of horrible ways to trigger cyclic dependencies by mixing and matching proxy texture formats, e.g.:
```
var a1 = AtlasTexture.new()
var p1 = ProxyTexture.new()
p1.base = a1
a1.atlas = p1
	
var sp = Sprite.new()
sp.texture = a1
add_child(sp)
```

Basically any `Texture` type that uses another `Texture` type internally can easily create such cyclic dependencies.
Also `MeshTexture`, and probably `AnimatedTexture`, etc.
author: akien-mga

body:
> > I've also added a check to `AtlasTexture::set_atlas()` ensuring no cyclic dependency will be created between some `AtlasTexture`s.
> 
> I once went down that rabbit hole and there's still a lot of horrible ways to trigger cyclic dependencies by mixing and matching proxy texture formats, e.g.:
> 
> ```
> var a1 = AtlasTexture.new()
> var p1 = ProxyTexture.new()
> p1.base = a1
> a1.atlas = p1
> 	
> var sp = Sprite.new()
> sp.texture = a1
> add_child(sp)
> ```
> 
> Basically any `Texture` type that uses another `Texture` type internally can easily create such cyclic dependencies. Also `MeshTexture`, and probably `AnimatedTexture`, etc.

Maybe adding a method like `virtual bool Texture::is_referenced_by(const Ref<Texture> &other_texture)` overridden accordingly by `AtlasTexture`, `ProxyTexture` etc. and used in `AtlasTexture::set_atlas`, `ProxyTexture::set_base` etc. would solve that?

This test demonstrates the bug. It walks two kinematic bodies towards two static body walls. One wall has 1 collision shape and the other has 4 shapes. On 3.4.2 with Bullet physics the kinematic body that collides with the 4 shape wall will jitter while the other wont.

body:
> The change reverses the lines in this commit which added compound collision support:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/6dd65c0d67960b0f0b26a24bd2f10fe8d54aa37a#diff-28f7bca21ebbe699cd16802c05d43f4e085331c7d0427c38da0ab3e6d14dc696L1186
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/6dd65c0d67960b0f0b26a24bd2f10fe8d54aa37a#diff-28f7bca21ebbe699cd16802c05d43f4e085331c7d0427c38da0ab3e6d14dc696L1217

This is worth highlight for review. This change was done in #27415 together with a lot of other fixes, but I don't see it discussed specifically. @madmiraal did identify this PR as the cause for some of the regressions linked below (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/34596#issuecomment-575632933).

> This hopefully fixes a lot of bullet physics jitter issues seen here (from @pouleyKetchoupp bullet issue tracker):
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/29392
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/34596

I tested the MRPs from those two issues respectively in 3.5 RC 1 and this PR and confirmed that this PR fixes them :+1: 

- #34596: Does seem to be a regression from 27415 which removed those two lines.
- #29392: Actually predates 27415 (merged in 3.2 and this is reported against 3.1), but there seems to have been a bug introduced in 3.1 that affected both GodotPhysics and Bullet. This was fixed in #40377 but it was found to only solve the bug for GodotPhysics, despite being implemented in KinematicBody itself - this was likely due to the 27415 change that is being reverted here.

This also seems to fix the following issues which are linked in the above issues or reference #27415:

- #28438.
- #35780.
- #35713.

There might be more, still testing. I'll update when I've reviewed everything I can find.

Some other related issues which do not seem fixed as of this PR (no change):

- #32182: Tested all 3 MRPs linked in that issue.

These might warrant further debugging and might not be related to the 3.1 and 3.2 regressions discussed above.

author: MarioLiebisch

body: CC @touilleMan - more fun with #56766. We can't hardcode module specific stuff in the `SConstruct`, so I think we should reconsider some of the other options as this is growing to be a complex workaround.
author: akien-mga

body:
@akien-mga `modules.mono.build_scripts.mono_reg_utils` package is not declared among the helper modules:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8958e1b35297baa8ef4f9e03bb4ad105500e3a0c/SConstruct#L45-L54

Given `modules.mono.build_scripts` is a Python package, adding `_helper_module("modules.mono.build_scripts", "modules/mono/build_scripts")` should do the trick

> We can't hardcode module specific stuff in the `SConstruct`

Can you elaborate ? I think as long as the helper scripts doesn't try to declare Scons rules at import time our current approach is fine.

> I think we should reconsider some of the other options

I'm fine with that. The primary goal of the current approach was to keep the complexity low by avoiding to have to move the helper scripts to a `scons_tools` folder. However using `scons_tools` has it own advantages (it is better integrated with how scons works so we can expect to avoid the kind of issues we currently have ^^)

Right now I feel like we need a bit more arguments to ditch the current approach, but if you have already made up your mind, feel free to revert the changes 😃
author: touilleMan

body:
>> We can't hardcode module specific stuff in the `SConstruct`
>
> Can you elaborate ? I think as long as the helper scripts doesn't try to declare Scons rules at import time our current approach is fine.

Modules are meant to be modular, and thus self-contained. If we have to add an instruction specific to a module in `SConstruct` we break encapsulation. This means that third-party modules would also need to have us merge instructions specific to their module to work out of the box.

Having to predeclare scripts for each subfolder also breaks encapsulation for core, editor, etc., But that's less problematic than modules which should really not require any code change outside their own folder.

> Right now I feel like we need a bit more arguments to ditch the current approach, but if you have already made up your mind, feel free to revert the changes 😃

I haven't made my mind, it's up for discussion. But yeah I don't really like the impact that this change is having on the codebase compared to the original issue which could be worked around ad hoc (though I'm aware it could still break in similar ways in the future if other common names get claimed as frequently installed python modules).

For example moving the template generation code to `methods.py` could be a way to share it between scripts. Or indeed embrace the `scons_site` workflow and move our helper scripts there.
author: akien-mga

body:
> Modules are meant to be modular, and thus self-contained. If we have to add an instruction specific to a module in SConstruct we break encapsulation.

I think we can expose the `_helper_module` function to the `SCsub`, this way any module could declare it own helpers

> But yeah I don't really like the impact that this change is having on the codebase compared to the original issue

Totally agree 😄 

> I haven't made my mind, it's up for discussion.

@garyo sorry to summon you like this, but I think your experience on Scons would be very valuable here 🤗 

To summon the current discussion:
- Godot build system is composed of 1) a root `SConstruct`, 2) multiples `SCsub` in sub folders, 3) multiple .py (and/or python packages) helper scripts
- the helper scripts are imported from the `SConscruct/SCsub` files the regular Python way ([example](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/71066cd63bcb3ce66064d1892b60fc1a2dfdc2b3/SConstruct#L15-L17)). This is fine most of the time, but it can create clashes (typically when a .py file is added in the root folder, see issue #56652)
- To solve #56652, we forced the declaration of the helper scripts (see #56698 and #56766). The idea was to eagerly load the helper scripts to prevent them from being shadowed, however this is not as simple as expected (hence this issue and PR #56766 to support Python < 3.7)

My guess is the "right" approach is to use the [site_scons](https://scons.org/doc/production/HTML/scons-user/ch17s08.html) feature so that our helper module would be first class citizen to Scons instead of being rogue python modules 😄 

Do you think it would be feasible to just move our helper modules into a `site_tools` folder, for instance:
- [godot/modules/mono/build_scripts/](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/tree/3.4.2-stable/modules/mono/build_scripts) would be renamed `godot/modules/mono/ site_scons/`. For importing, [import build_scripts.gen_cs_glue_version as gen_cs_glue_version](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/45eaa2daf1e3d2571a9f31a3421911262ec82f51/modules/mono/SCsub#L13) will be replaced by `import gen_cs_glue_version`
- [godot/methods.py] would be moved into `goto/ site_scons`, it imports would not be modified
author: touilleMan

body:
I can't comment on the helper modules stuff, as I don't know how that works.

Regarding the issues caused from supporting the mono module in vsproj gen, is this issue only on the `master` branch? If that's the case I suggest not bothering with it; soon it will not be needed. If it's causing errors right now, just remove support for the module temporarily.

I must also say I never liked the idea the vsproj gen needed to hard-code this module specific configuration in the first place. The compiledb generator doesn't need to do any of that, so I think vsproj gen should borrow some ideas from it.

body:
> BTW, the no way to do squashing from Github right?

We don't use GitHub's squash and merge feature on the main Godot repository, since it loses the merge commit in the process. We prefer keeping the merge commit to make the Git history more self-documented (even if that makes it longer when not using the `--no-merges` option).

body:
> Yes I agree it is not the clock time. But that does not matter for a cache buster. It does not have to be a valid time. It just needs to be unique within the second.

I'm not sure it's correct though, as the only realistic way to have two exports done in the same second is to do it from two separate processes (e.g. two command line exports on the same machine, or exports done from two different machines and ending at the same time). In this case, `get_ticks_msec` will be process specific and there's no guarantee than a bigger value actually identifies an export that is newer, it just means that the editor instance that did this export had more uptime.

I'd rename the variable to `ticks` though to make it clear that the code author understood that they're handle engine ticks (i.e. multiple seconds, possibly dozens of minutes) and not the milliseconds that would be expected after `date` and `time`.

Or maybe even `salt` because it's actually not the ticks but just using ticks as base to get a random identifier from 0 to 999. But it's not cryptography so it might be the wrong terminology.

body:
Sorry, I didn't have the time to fully investigate this yet, but I don't think we need it, at least not this way.
If your server is properly configured (via E-Tag, or "must-revalidate") you don't need a cache buster, additionally, this might even force re-downloading of some assets that hasn't changed.
If anything, we seem to have to opposite problem, i.e. that the PWA app does not cache enough (#56103 and there we might need to invalid cache see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/compare/3.x...Faless:js/3.x_pwa_prefer_cache which is still WIP )
author: Faless

body:
> If your server is properly configured (via E-Tag, or "must-revalidate") you don't need a cache buster, additionally, this might even force re-downloading of some assets that hasn't changed.

Unfortunately, those headers can't be overridden for all files on itch.io or GitHub Pages.
author: Calinou

body:
Yes I have also the problem that I can not override these headers, and you do not want to browser re-evaluate each time the page is loaded (costs to many requests), The only way a browser can see without a server request that a resources is changed is by changing it (so add a cache buster). And yes this can result in loading resources which where not changed because, this will happen not too often and is not in comparison with browser which always needs to check if resources are changed, this will delay loading the page.

body:
The best solution would be not to use one date time as cache buster but to determine the hash of each file and add the hash to the url. This way the file only gets downloaded when it is actually changed. This would be more work, and if we choose to do this a more experienced godot developer should pick this up. However a timestamp is small simple and will work just fine as short term solution which can be back ported to the 3.x because it is a small change.

A question about integration into our CD pipeline, since we serve our app under `/` and therefore index.html can't be renamed, perhaps we still need explicit rule in nginx to `expires -1` or a `Cache-control` header for the index, as it references the exported wasm and pck?

with

And I am pretty sure they both provide the proper cache headers.

As I mentioned, I don't think we don't need a cache buster.
If the cache headers from the server are wrong, the `html` page returned will be the old one, with the old hash, and cache will not be busted.
If the cache headers are correct, the `html` page will be revalidated (and the new one returned), but so will the other files.

body:
`If the cache headers from the server are wrong, the html page returned will be the old one, with the old hash, and cache will not be busted.`  This is exactly the problem this would be addressing.  We currently have this issue, and this sucks for cached clients because our game is multiplayer and our servers will not let old clients connect due to need to update.  

And I can confirm that unless the web request chain receives a very short expires time with 'must-validate' in cache-control header (which would revalidate and receive a 304), there is going to be problem.  This is also the case for our cluster's reverse proxy, as caching may be done at the proxy as well.  

Deployments aren't just limited to itch.io or GitHub Pages as some html5 gaming portals do not implement caching properly.  This is a partial reason why we are attempting to serve our app in an iframe on portals if it's an option, as we have more control there.

Servers can respond with wrong cache headers, which may or may not be out of your control and different exported URIs may help in those scenarios.  I'm not saying this really needs this to be merged, but there is a use-case here.
author: jordo

As I explained, I don't see how this PR addresses this problem, since if the cache headers are wrong, the `html` returned will have the old timestamp, making the whole process pointless.
author: Faless

body:
> > This is exactly the problem this would be addressing.
> 
> As I explained, I don't see how this PR addresses this problem, since if the cache headers are wrong, the `html` returned will have the old timestamp, making the whole process pointless.

Which is why I asked about the cache buster on the *.wasm and *.pck exports.    Here's a very real scenario that is happening right now:

*.html files served from a webserver with correct cache control (revalidates), while *.pck (unknown file extension to most webservers) are served with say "Cache-control: max-age=604800".  What happens in this case when re-exported projects now have a different .pck file with the same URI?  The web client in this case doesn't even make the request for a new pck, and just plucks it out of it's local cache.
author: jordo

body: If there is no understanding of the above user story I can not explain much more unfortunately.  It sounds like a few are already working around this anyways.
author: jordo

body: In general, the response to this PR seems to be that `as long as http servers are configured properly, and reverse proxies and clients are doing what they are supposed to, this PR is uncessary`.  But that is often not the case in many real-world scenarios.  We have seen de-sync plenty of times among our exported game specifically when *.pck resources are plucked from http client's local cache while the other http resources are re-validated and potentially updated.  A guaranteed different exported URI for each resource imho is a pretty good way to bust this up and guarantee client reload on re-export.  So I do support this although there are a couple small issues with the implementation.
author: jordo

body:
> In general, the response to this PR seems to be that `as long as http servers are configured properly, and reverse proxies and clients are doing what they are supposed to, this PR is uncessary`.

I think there is a misunderstanding here. Adding a cache buster when the servers are configured correctly **is detrimental**. This is the main reason why I am against this PR.

>  But that is often not the case in many real-world scenarios. We have seen de-sync plenty of times among our exported game specifically when *.pck resources are plucked from http client's local cache while the other http resources are re-validated and potentially updated. 

If you feel this can help a common scenario, I think we can add an **optional** cache buster, but I would still recommend fixing the proxy and servers configurations (again, the goal of CDNs and proxy caching is also to provide proper caching headers, if that doesn't happen, something is wrong in the infrastructure).

And again, I still don't think this will fix it in the end, and it will just trigger other problems.

So, in case we want this:
- Needs a rebase
- The cache buster should be added as a [config option](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/platform/javascript/js/engine/config.js#L109-L115) and not as a separate function.
- Should also be correctly added to the [localFile](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/platform/javascript/js/engine/config.js#L294-L307) function.
- Must be added as an export option (off by default).

author: tijmenvangulik

body: If you append the file contents hash to the exported filename (with the exception of the .html root) I'm not sure how this can be considered detrimental as you can actually be **more** aggressive on your webserver cache settings... Here is an example output from say our current export: ``` index.html index.audio.worklet.js index.js index.pck index.wasm ``` It could be: ``` index.html index-FILECONTENTSHASH.audio.worklet.js index-FILECONTENTSHASH.js index-FILECONTENTSHASH.pck index-FILECONTENTSHASH.wasm ``` Since all the other URI's are loaded via the first request to index.html, *.html is the only resource type that needs to be configured to revalidate. All other files under this directory you could even cache indefinitely without revalidation as it's guaranteed their contents will never change (and if their content does, it is exported as a different URI). From a caching perspective, this is actually the opposite of detrimental and more cache efficient because right now this is what happens if configured correctly: Every URI needs to be re-validated because the content MAY change at any time, which mean a bunch of additional round-trips and a bunch of 304's **if** configured properly ( and really annoying de-syncs across wasm, js, and pck files if not). If you implement exports like this: ``` index.html index-FILECONTENTSHASH.audio.worklet.js index-FILECONTENTSHASH.js index-FILECONTENTSHASH.pck index-FILECONTENTSHASH.wasm ``` you could configure your webserver to aggressively instruct clients to cache .js, .wasm, and .pck as their contents at their respective URI's are guaranteed to never change. In this scenario there is only a single request and single validation +304 response required for the initial request to index.html, as every other dependency is derived from that first request. At any rate, from a potential cache 'getting it wrong' perspective, I think the above export example is less error prone. I think it's going to be a good option for the engine to provide devs because it really removes a lot of potential issues whether it's considered a cache buster or not really. Definitely useful for devs who are deploying to environments that aren't under their direct control, or devs who are not familiar with what their actual server cache settings should be. However, I think in order for this to make it into main, I would want to the see a file-contents hash on the exported filename though which is definitely more work than what is included in this PR. The only problem / downside I can see to implementing this is local filesystem issues & confusion if you choose to export to the same output directory as a previous export. In which case I think there could/would be multiple copies of the same dependency with different hash depending on how implemented, but I can already think of a few different solutions for that.
author: jordo

body:
Hi,

Cache buster fix the caching problem for normal websites. For PWA’s you need more. I do not use the PWA version because the cache problem is even worse. You will also need to correct the pwa service worker for this which is in my opinion not correctly written for on and offline caching. I have given up on this change. I have simply modified the html template with extra cache busters and wrote a script to update it on every release.

Tijmen

body:
I don't know if this is the right place but I came here after a Google search. I am neither Godot nor a javascript/web developer. 

But when I am serving your wasm file, I realized that the etag information is deleted from client request and not sent to the server. If it hadn't been deleted, the server and client would have always instantly agreed that it was the exact same file and everything would have been resolved quickly with the 304 code. As far as I understand from the first comment, an aggressive method has been developed to prevent the classic cache mechanisms' problems like clients doesn't realises new files. But there is etag mechanism for it. If etags are not removed from requests, a much healthier and faster caching method can be followed.

body:
Hm I wonder if that's the best way to fix this. If an exit code was set manually, I would expect it to be respected so `SceneTree::quit()` shouldn't default to `EXIT_SUCCESS` but actually use `OS::get_singleton()->get_exit_code()`, no?
I.e. change default value to `-1` and use it as magic for "get exit code from `OS`" instead of the user provided one.

CC @touilleMan who implemented/refactored the exit code logic.

*Edit:* That being said the default `_exit_code` seems to be `EXIT_FAILURE` so if we change that, we'd have to update a lot of code to set the exit code manually. This was changed in #38929 to basically remove the logic I'm describing above.

There's only a few `SceneTree::quit()` calls which might need review to check if they actually respect a potentially not `EXIT_SUCCESS` error code that would be set ahead of quit.
```
$ rg '\->quit\('
editor/editor_node.cpp
3003:   get_tree()->quit();

editor/project_manager.cpp
1907:   // This method must be called before calling `get_tree()->quit()`.
1953:                   get_tree()->quit();
2110:   get_tree()->quit();
2356:   get_tree()->quit();

core/debugger/local_debugger.cpp
277:                    SceneTree::get_singleton()->quit();
```
author: akien-mga

body:
@akien-mga from what I see, `OS::get_singletion()->set_exit_code` get used by `SceneTree`, `EditorNode` (and also in main, but this is special case when the SceneTree is not instantiated)

The issue I see is `SceneTree` and `EditorNode` work at different levels and hence walk on each other toes.
I would say only `SceneTree` has the legitimacy to use `set_exit_code` (considering it itself expose the `quit` that should be used instead, and `set_exit_tree` is not exposed by ClassDB)

So `EditorNode` should be reworked to keep the error code as an internal attribute, and provide it a parameter when it calls `quit()`.

This fix seems pretty trivial given how close `set_exit_code()` is from `_exit_editor()` (which itself calls `quit()`)
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/045648d09166377500630c606a447857cd841fc0/editor/editor_node.cpp#L958-L962

@akien-mga tell me if you want me to submit a PR on this ;-)
author: touilleMan

body:
> So EditorNode should be reworked to keep the error code as an internal attribute, and provide it a parameter when it calls quit()

I agree with this approach, I considered something similar but went with this due to the simplicity.

> There's only a few SceneTree::quit() calls which might need review to check if they actually respect a potentially not EXIT_SUCCESS error code that would be set ahead of quit

I had a brief look when I made this change and found no other obvious cases at least, but it was late. :P

I had to change `detail/sample_max_error` default to `5` so that vertices computed by recast are aligned with the voxels grid much better. This way we're avoiding problems even on very complex meshes while sacrificing some navmesh details.

In practice (flat meshes or meshes with some gentle hills) this makes no difference while this way we're able to avoid potential problems.

The grid alignment algorithm will have to be re-done anyway, but IMO this commit at least polishes what's already there.

author: Calinou

body:
Updated the document for now, clarifying that "SkeletonRetarget is for Skeleton3D" in the class reference.

I don't have much knowledge about Skeleton2D. If it has the concept of rest/pose, then I think a similar implementation of SkeletonRetarget is possible. However, it may not possible to implement a retargeting track for Skeleton2D in AnimationPlayer. The reason is that Skeleton2D does not have its own track type for transform.

In Skeleton3D, only Pos/Ros/Scl3DTracks are allowed for bone transform. But, Skeleton2D bone transforms are shared with normal properties, and there are two types of tracks: Bezier Tracks and Property Tracks. This makes retarget tracks for Skeleton2D difficult to implement.

Although, I think 2D assets don't have much concept of stance, unlike 3D assets, so I guess there is not much demand for implement them for Skeleton2D. In other words, there are few models that have the "same stance" and different bone naming conventions and rest as an asset; It's means, there are few animation packages. So I guess it is rare case that need to retarget for 2D, since multiple models in one product may be created by the same person.

I also wait for others' opinions.
