author: vmedea

body:
> Being able to run the editor on this laptop helped me find a new issue: keypad cursor/pgup/pgdn/etc keys don't work. When numlock is on, it generates numbers, with numlock off, the numpad keys don't seem to do anything. This is with wayland only, with X(emulation) they work.

Thanks for the bug report! I'll add it to my TODO list and update you when I find something.
author: deralmas

body:
> Thanks for the bug report! I'll add it to my TODO list and update you when I find something.

I think I found the cause. I printed the `keycode` and `physical_keycode` for the left arrow key on the keypad: 
```
       NumLock  keycode    physical_keycode
X11    Off      KEY_LEFT   KEY_KP_4
X11    On       KEY_KP_4   KEY_KP_4
WL     Off      KEY_KP_4   KEY_KP_4
WL     On       KEY_KP_4   KEY_KP_4
```
So for X11, the 'keycode' depends on the numlock status but for wayland it doesn't. Not sure *why*, as you do pass in the xkb_state for translation. Maybe a matter of a change in `_xkb_keysym_to_keycode`?

Edit: yes this does it:
```patch
diff --git a/platform/linuxbsd/wayland/key_mapping_xkb.cpp b/platform/linuxbsd/wayland/key_mapping_xkb.cpp
index 0e23b9ba391dc6f831b6a0177375d1803877da34..c02ce939ea3bf1612938f94a7cb7347729798205 100644
--- a/platform/linuxbsd/wayland/key_mapping_xkb.cpp
+++ b/platform/linuxbsd/wayland/key_mapping_xkb.cpp
@@ -93,7 +93,6 @@ static _XKBTranslatePair _xkb_keysym_to_keycode[] = {
        //{ XKB_KEY_KP_Equal,                Key::EQUAL   },
        //{ XKB_KEY_KP_Separator,            Key::COMMA   },
        { XKB_KEY_KP_Decimal, Key::KP_PERIOD },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Delete, Key::KP_PERIOD },
        { XKB_KEY_KP_Multiply, Key::KP_MULTIPLY },
        { XKB_KEY_KP_Divide, Key::KP_DIVIDE },
        { XKB_KEY_KP_Subtract, Key::KP_SUBTRACT },
@@ -109,17 +108,19 @@ static _XKBTranslatePair _xkb_keysym_to_keycode[] = {
        { XKB_KEY_KP_8, Key::KP_8 },
        { XKB_KEY_KP_9, Key::KP_9 },

-       // same but with numlock
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Insert, Key::KP_0 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_End, Key::KP_1 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Down, Key::KP_2 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Page_Down, Key::KP_3 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Left, Key::KP_4 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Begin, Key::KP_5 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Right, Key::KP_6 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Home, Key::KP_7 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Up, Key::KP_8 },
-       { XKB_KEY_KP_Page_Up, Key::KP_9 },
+       // same but without numlock
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Insert, Key::INSERT },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Delete, Key::KEY_DELETE },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_End, Key::END },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Down, Key::DOWN },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Page_Down, Key::PAGEDOWN },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Left, Key::LEFT },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Begin, Key::CLEAR },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Right, Key::RIGHT },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Home, Key::HOME },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Up, Key::UP },
+       { XKB_KEY_KP_Page_Up, Key::PAGEUP },
+
        { XKB_KEY_F1, Key::F1 },
        { XKB_KEY_F2, Key::F2 },
        { XKB_KEY_F3, Key::F3 },
```
author: vmedea

body: @vmedea I can confirm that your patch works! Thanks a lot for your help!

Looks like `Key::*` was changed in regard to SUPER / HYPER (#70052):
- `Key::HYPER_L` and `Key::HYPER_R` have been replaced with `Key::HYPER` (causing a compile issue)
- `Key::SUPER_L` and `Key::SUPER_R` were removed from the documentation and binding (no replacement) *but* they're still in `core/os/keyboard.h`

I'm not sure this makes a lot of sense, "super" is the windows key right? I don't know what "hyper" is. Not something one'd often use in a game but i dunno…

Edit: just reported it there
Edit.2: see also #72032, "SUPER" is "META" now, and no L/R distinction anymore is on purpose

body: I noticed that I could drop my local patch to comment out `.axis_value120=…`, I think this was solved by embedding the WL headers.
author: vmedea

body: @vmedea tbf I thought about that for a while and couldn't understand why this is happening in the first place. The "main" headers don't mention `axis_value120` at all. All wrappers (including the one containing `axis_value120`) are generated at build time from the protocol descriptions. Perhaps it's formatted in such a way that makes it incompatible with older `wayland-scanner`? I doubt it. Perhaps it could've been due to some older cached protocol wrappers?
author: deralmas

body:
@Riteo I've checked: the `/usr/share/wayland/wayland.xml` in `libwayland-dev` package on Ubuntu 22.04 (which I assume wayland-scanner uses to generate header files) doesn't define the `axis_value120` event slot. It defines `wl_pointer` version 7 instead of 8.

> Perhaps it's formatted in such a way that makes it incompatible with older wayland-scanner? I doubt it. Perhaps it could've been due to some older cached protocol wrappers?

I'm not sure it's wayland-scanner at fault or it was just using different input data than expected.

Edit: no, it works fine when doing it manually. This gives the expected output:
```
$ wayland-scanner client-header /usr/share/wayland/wayland.xml test1.h
$ wayland-scanner client-header thirdparty/wayland/protocol/wayland.xml test2.h
$ diff -du test1.h test2.h
…
+       void (*axis_value120)(void *data,
+                             struct wl_pointer *wl_pointer,
+                             uint32_t axis,
+                             int32_t value120);
…
```
So I think Godot's build was using `/usr/include/wayland-client-protocol.h` (which unfortunately, Debian installs to such a global path) instead of the generated one!
(but this is solved now)
author: vmedea

body:
> So I think Godot's build was using /usr/include/wayland-client-protocol.h (which unfortunately, Debian installs to such a global path) instead of the generated one!

The issue came from a generated wrapper that doesn't include it, so I deeply doubt that this was the case.

> (but this is solved now)

I've never touched directly anything with the generator, other than a small preprocessing step that made them include the dynamic wrapper, so if it was indirectly fixed by something it could be broken any time from now, but oh well, at least it works now.

body: I'm wondering why it says "amdgpu" under display. I'm using nvidia drm kernel modules.
author: poiati

body:
> I'm wondering why it says "amdgpu" under display. I'm using nvidia drm kernel modules.

You have two graphics devices, one Nvidia RTX 3060M, and one AMD Radeon Vega which is part of your CPU. OpenGL applications would typically use the AMD one (less resource usage) unless you specify `DRI_PRIME=1` I believe. And Vulkan apps may choose one or the other depending on various factors (Godot should default to the more powerful Nvidia GPU normally, provided that it does report better metrics than the AMD one).

author: poiati

body:
@poiati

> I always had stutter issues when running with X11, all is gone with Wayland, it runs smoothly!

Great, expect a way better integration with the rest of your system! The only downside is that multiple windows can't be trivially implemented with the current `DisplayServerAPI` but in this phase I'm working on chipping away extra goodies, which should make the tradeoff worth it, such as touchpad gesture support (already implemented), tablet support (only pressure pretty much for now), touch specific stuff (hard to do without a proper big touchscreen but still planned), stuff like virtual keyboard support (trivial but not done yet) and IME (looks easy but yet to be done).

Also note that, as written in the PR, HiDPI/fractional scaling isn't trivial at all and will require a bunch of changes in the core from what I understand.

> Do we have any idea when we are merging this? Maybe for 4.1? Tks!

Who knows, it's in the 4.x milestone, and the [faster releases planned](https://godotengine.org/article/release-management-4-0-and-beyond/) could mean that it might even get in 4.2 for all we know. Let's just be patient :)

Actually while this PR might've not been merged in time for 4.0, there have been lots of improvements, already merged or planned to be merged, that pave the way for this PR, such as: 

- #68182, which moved the X11 code in a separate folder, so that the Wayland code wouldn't mash with X11's.

- #69449, #68372 and #68586 which allowed the X11 dependency to be dynamically uncoupled from the main binary, allowing it to be run from Wayland-only systems.

- #67620, which fixed a graphical problem on Vulkan that happened (AFAIR) only on Wayland due to it having transparency by default.

- #72004 and #72102, which allows this backend to share the XKB dependency handling code with X11.

- #68944 or indirectly through #72831 which will add an EGL driver for OpenGL support on Wayland.

I'm also tracking other unrelated PRs to make this backend consistent with the rest of them.

So, while it might've not be merged yet, be sure that me and people like Akien (which I won't ping as he's ultra-busy, but he was immensely helpful pinging me on lots of stuff that might've influenced this PR) are doing our best to make this PR succeed, so just wait :)
author: deralmas

body:
Uh, so, time for an update:

I'm still in a semi-paused state with this thing because I wanted to focus for a bit on other things such as relaxing a bit, working on the italian translation and a few dumb projects of mine but as usual I keep just juggling them instead of actually focusing.

Oh well.

Anyways, I've not forgot this and this isn't abandoned. I'm still helping people willing to test and any bug report is always appreciated.

But this isn't the sole reason I'm writing this. The premultiplied cursor issue just popped into my mind randomly, so I looked around a bit and look what I found, the `wl_buffer` specification says:

> If the buffer uses a format that has an alpha channel, the alpha channel is assumed to be premultiplied in the color channels unless otherwise specified.

(taken from https://wayland.app/protocols/wayland#wl_buffer)

For more info there's also this wayland-devel thread: https://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2021-September/041990.html

So, mistery solved I guess. I was wondering for a while whether I had to "manually" premultiply the texture and the answer is yes. It also looks that other projects are doing this, including SDL with PR https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/pull/4864 (in case anyone wonders, I haven't looked at the code, just the PR's title and description if that even matters).

A fix is coming soon-ish.

author: deralmas

body:
It might be better to use reverse-dns names; they make it easier to differentiate between community builds of Godot and official ones, as well as having less of a chance of conflicts between different window IDs.

OBS seems to do this for its windows, and Flatpak does too for seeing what folders it should access and what dbus names it should own. I don't think promoting old names like `godot-editor` would be good.
author: orowith2os

body:
> It might be better to use reverse-dns names

yes fwiw the [xdg spec](https://wayland.app/protocols/xdg-shell#xdg_toplevel:request:set_app_id) recommends using app ids that match the base name of the application's `.desktop` name. These are usually reverse-dns names. in case of godot editor `org.godotengine.Godot`

im not sure how to handle variations in this case though, maybe "subdomains" under the common prefix.
author: vmedea

body:
I'm aware of this but didn't give it much importance as stuff like firefox, foot and mpv use "simple" ids. Plus there's also the issue about the fact that projects can't declare package names so I had to use X11's approach of blindly setting the app id as the whole unadulterated project name.

@orowith2os

> they make it easier to differentiate between community builds of Godot and official ones

that's a fair point.

> as well as having less of a chance of conflicts between different window IDs

@vmedea
> im not sure how to handle variations in this case though, maybe "subdomains" under the common prefix.

Mhh, I'm not sure either. Perhaps we could treat the editor, project manager and the generic engine as different programs, a bit like Android does, so: `org.godotengine.Editor`, `org.godotengine.ProjectManager` and `org.godotengine.Godot`. xdg-shell's specification talks about dbus activation but I know nothing about it.

Anyways, I'm not against at all making the app id use the reverse-dns style, I just dislike a bit the fact that games don't provide one themselves, which is the reason I stuck to a simpler approach.
author: deralmas

body:
Ok, I found some more info about what's the matter with D-Bus:
 - https://specifications.freedesktop.org/desktop-entry-spec/latest/ar01s08.html
 - https://wiki.gnome.org/HowDoI/DBusApplicationLaunching

Basically, we should be able to use the fancy reverse-dns `org.godotengine` "subdomains" _but_ in order to be extra fancy™, there must be extra packaging stuff (that's missing in the first place, so whatever), such as specific desktop files for them all (excluding obv the messy project name app ids). I think that this might warrant some attention on both X11 and Wayland if it's worth in the first place to have D-Bus activation support.

I still wonder why firefox, foot, mpv and mtpaint (80% of my graphical programs excluding godot BTW :P) don't use the fancy™ app id. Eh, whatever, I'll make a new commit and see what happens.
author: deralmas

body: There, now the values for the project manager, editor and "plain engine" are: `org.godotengine.ProjectManager`, `org.godotengine.Editor` and `org.godotengine.Godot` (the last one applies only if there's no project name setup)

author: deralmas

body:
Ok, time for a status update.

I went to the sprint and finally discussed out some of the biggest issues left, namely HiDPI and multi-window support.

Regarding HiDPI, the plan is, in simple terms, to completely ignore the weird Wayland thing by convincing the whole engine that it's an unscaled window and doing all the scaling ourselves. I already hacked it in locally and can confirm that this approach works.

Regarding multi-windowing instead I proposed to make the specific subclasses such as `Popup` signal the `DisplayServer` about some predefined classes through an enum of sorts. I've still not tested this approach so it might not be good enough still.

I'm also refactoring the whole thing to finally try and put as much annoying Wayland stuff into a separate class since we now have also to convert between scaled and unscaled coordinates with the whole HiDPI thing. I'm finally giving this approach a shot and will report whether it will be worth it in terms of maintainability and stuff like that.

Tell me if you'd like to test the hacked in scaling branch. You'll have to set e.g. 200% scaling yourself through the editor and there's no handling for the cursor but it'd be nice having confirmation that it looks as it should on HiDPI stuff.

Edit: the branch has also some other issues but oh well, I said it was hacked in :shrug:

author: Slabity

body:
Time to talk about the new stuff I've been working on.

In short, I'm making an experiment to refactor most code and make it more maintainable as I've never felt really satisfied with it under that aspect.

I've based it on the HiDPI branch I had locally and improved that feature too, so if you want to test it, go to my `wayland-thread-refactor` branch over at https://github.com/Riteo/godot/tree/wayland-thread-refactor

For the technical people, please keep reading for a rationale and some notes. As usual, I also write some notes in my commits so that you can hopefully always keep track of whole thing if you want.

Edit: in the end I'm breaking lots of stuff right now lmao, input handling's really messy.

## The technical part

As soon as I came back from the sprint I started thinking on what would be the next steps to do and settled on two big, somehow related, things: HiDPI support and making the code more maintainable.

I say that they're related because at the sprint we decided to do the whole scaling manually, which in short requires us to keep track of _both_ the logical and scaled window sizes (naming might differ in code, I haven't still settled on it), which is a mess and requires to "translate" lots of position data backwards and forwards.

I never liked the code a lot because we passed a huge `WaylandState` struct between all objects and, while this approach allows a very fast iteration cycle, it binds everything together. I never used the API before this PR so that was definitely a great advantage but now that I have more experience I'm sure I can untangle the code a lot.

The HiDPI implementation was the nail in the coffin for me, so, based on the hacked in HiDPI branch I've begun a big refactoring stemming from two important observations:
 1. We can avoid most uses of the `WaylandState` struct by using Wayland object references in a more clever way: we can fetch directly and _anywhere_ (not only in the event handlers) the custom data  we assign to the objects (such as `WindowData` for `wl_surface`s). This will also avoid useless loops in which we looked for data with an associated object as we can do the opposite instantly and have the same result. We can also tag objects to make sure that we're actually working with Godot Wayland objects and make sure that the object's actually initialized correctly (a thing I had no idea was possible before working on HiDPI, thank libdecor for that).
 2. The Wayland event handlers do pretty much only a few things really, such as "async" stuff (e.g. instantly resizing the window wayland-side, responding to xdg-shell pings, attaching the cursor as per-spec), translating data (e.g. input events, scaled window data) and sending it to the main thread through our dedicated `InputEvent`-like event queue. In short, the interface leans heavily into being almost mono-directional, with most main-thread side stuff being simple Wayland-related requests through the native Wayland queue (_not_ our custom unrelated one). 

So, I've decided to give a shot to an approach I've always been skeptical about (and of which I still am to an extent): moving all Wayland stuff into a `WaylandThread` class. The main ways of interfacing with Wayland will be its own methods, so that we can move all the "translation" stuff into a nice place, and all the incoming data from the thread will be available through an event queue. The usage feels almost like the Vulkan driver with lots of low-level, yet Godot-aware, requests.

The idea is that `DisplayServerWayland` shouldn't care about _most_ Wayland technicalities (that's the point I'm a bit skeptical about, hence the "most") but instead only comb through the right data and do the right requests through `WaylandThread`, which will do all the annoying stuff.

Input handling is very messy too (I mean, this is a messy problem by nature) and I'm still not sure on how to fit it into the equation but I'm starting to become optimistic. I have some ideas though.

In theory we should also be able to move locking only into the Wayland-Godot event queue operations, which should theoretically reduce lock contention if that's even an issue. Not really a priority/goal of this refactoring but it would be a nice side effect.

Keep in mind that this is all an experiment and at the time of writing there are still a lot of transitional hacks to migrate the code slowly without breaking or rewriting everything from scratch in one go.

That said, I would really like to know your opinions on this experiment.
author: deralmas

body:
The refactoring is done and it was a success!

It went mostly as planned, so most of the huge message above should still apply IIRC.

But, most importantly:

**We now have HiDPI support!**
*Note: the cursor is still blurry (needs special logic and isn't high priority) for now ~~and auto scale doesn't work yet (may need some changes outside the Wayland backend code).~~* **edit:** autoscale now works! See below.

| Before, scale 2 | After, scale 2 and manual 200% UI |
|--------|--------|
| ![a pixel mess](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/assets/31065808/2ce1452a-7303-482a-8159-20f811352670) | ![noice](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/assets/31065808/4b8321a3-9436-46b5-af26-26fa7c8c6ee7) | 

(you might have to click on the images to taste the sweet sweet pixels)

## In short

- Most of the Wayland-specific code and the polling thread got put into a class named (fittingly) `WaylandThread`, which handles most initialization, the message queue and scaling in a way that shouldn't hurt one's brain too much (especially due to scaling) while reading the whole thing.

- `WaylandState`, a "jack of all trades" struct got yeeted away in favor of getting the data associated with Wayland objects and `WaylandThread`'s methods, along other stuff described in the comment before.

- Luckily very little things are a dumb wrapper and the rest makes it 100% worth it, scale is handled automatically and _just works_.

## Extra notes

- Along the properties in the comment before, I've explicitly made `WaylandState` as less stateful as possible (outside of Wayland object handling of course). It still needs to have some state but the idea is that `DisplayServerWayland` will still have to keep track of stuff it touches, which avoids lots of issues and has actually improved stuff like cursor handling and the (hacky) pointer warping logic.

- There are still lots of `TODO`s and rough edges but I think everything got refactored in one way or another so I'm calling it good enough. You'll see further improvements over time. A notable rough edge is input handling, which actually works in scaled coordinates because of a few annoying things. I'm planning to scale input events while stuffing them into the Wayland->Godot queue but we should be fine for now.

- `WaylandThread` has some unused window id arguments because I have an idea that I'm pretty sure will work for multiple windows, stay tuned. 

- Borderless windows don't work (at least on sway) for some reason. I'm aware of this.

- I haven't tested tablets at all. I'm not even sure if they work and I'm sure I commented out something. I'm in a pretty weird situation with them as I have only a very cheap and dumb Wacom tablet.

body:
@TuxTheAstronaut While testing other stuff I stumbled on the crash you're getting and I think I finally figured out why.

In simple words, it looks like that if the primary selection extension is announced later than the "seats" (mouse, keyboard, that stuff), it sets it up in a different part of the code which gives the primary selection event handler bad data, thus crashing when it tries to access it normally.

I haven't actually confirmed if it fixes the issue because I've reverted to the old version of sway I was using for other reasons, so please tell me if it actually does (I'm pretty sure it should).

I pushed a very simple fix and, while at it, I merged the latest changes from master, because why not?

body:
# All right, time for a new status update!

You might've noticed that this PR hasn't had updates in a while.

While I'm, as usual, doing other stuff, there's actually another big reason for my inactivity here: I've been doing some mad experiments lately (read: since the 5th of July) which sadly didn't really pan out as I hoped but taught me some cool stuff that's actually useful.

## First, the bad news
Yes, this was still a multiwindow attempt.

I'm aware at how much time this feature is sinking from me, but I genuinely don't like the idea of forcing single-window mode on users. I might have to live with it for now though.

Toplevels work fine, the problematic part is popup handling. In theory we could make floating editors and docks work fine, but since we have no clear API separation on what is a popup and what is not we have no practical way of adding special cases for either. Also, `WINDOW_FLAG_POPUP` doesn't count, as tooltips don't use it because they don't want input to be redirected to them. This makes that flag more of a `PopupMenu` flag than an actual "floating borderless window" flag.

I had an idea of tacking on windows a "class" enum, distinguishing them between `WINDOW_CLASS_TOPLEVEL`, `WINDOW_CLASS_POPUP` and `WINDOW_CLASS_POPUP_MENU` or something like that, but the almost-but-not-quite redundancy with `WINDOW_FLAG_POPUP` made everything ugly and the lines between the classes blurred too much for my taste.

This is a distinction that also X11 needs (tiling WMs beg for proper popup tagging), mind you, but finding a way that keeps compatibility and is not terribly redundant is hard. I really don't have the time nor the motivation to work on this as of now, plus this has to be discussed with the other platform maintainers, which is even harder, so this will have to be postponed once again.

The bad news don't end though: even if we changed all the APIs (I did some PoCs with the above idea), wlroots has a pretty nasty timing bug for us separate-event-thread users: https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/wlroots/wlroots/-/issues/3662

Luckily with some light testing it looks like KDE doesn't have it, but still, wlroots compositor users aren't few. Admittedly I've not talked about this with the wlroots people yet. There are some patches floating around which _should_ fix the problem but they're all WIP and so on.

I really want this to work one day though, I've not given up yet!

## (Finally) the good news

This was sort of planned. While I hoped that it would yield something usable, I knew this would've been a throwaway experiment, especially since I allowed myself to play with the core willy-nilly. It was a proof of concept after all.

I've not actually worked non-stop for more than a month. While I got a bit frustrated due to the asynchronous hard-to-debug nature of this sort of stuff and the compositor bugs, I've actually worked on this very sparsely. This was mostly due to personal factors such as tiredness unrelated to Godot.

Also, this resulted in some actually very useful code patches that I'll be applying soon™ here!

Since a main showstopper for multiwindowing was the ugly occlusion bug, I've actually found a really nice, pretty much "proper" workaround! It's a bit shoehorned in Godot's architecture and _might_ still give some weird problems, but it's our best bet I've not had any huge issues with it. I'm talking about handling the frame event manually, explained very clearly here: https://emersion.fr/blog/2018/wayland-rendering-loop/

(it's some annoying stuff so don't worry if you don't understand it right away)

Thanks to this fix, coming soon™, you'll be able to hide the window and have the game still tick!

Also there were issues with the event loop, since it could panic _before_ having the time to read (and print) the Wayland error message. Now most (all?) of the "Wayland error XX" messages should be accompanied with an actual textual reason on why the compositor disconnected us. Thanks to kennylevinsen over at #wayland at OFTC for pointing this out!

Another other thing I found out was, from what I can understand, that the actual thing closer to Godot's focus on Wayland is the keyboard focus, not the pointer focus like I thought. I haven't actually thoroughly investigated it but I recall talking about this on IRC. I think that makes more sense, so I'll probably bring that over here and see what happens.

Honestly, considering the stuff above and ignoring the big disappointment of not being able to pull this off even as a PoC, I'm actually very happy on the stuff I've learned, especially for the occlusion fix since now the usability improved considerably!

Note that the branch hasn't been uploaded online because it's a dumpsterfire of workarounds and attempts, with a metric ton of debug `print_line`s and failsafes attempting to figure out what's wrong with the complex asynchronous dance needed for multiwindowing.

I'll soon rebase this PR, fish out the good stuff and fix the few things that got broken by the refactoring.

Thanks for you patience!
author: deralmas

body: There, the occlusion "workaround" is up! Please, tell me if it works for you.
author: deralmas

body:
Sorry for all of these messages, but I swear, this is worth it:

Cursors are now good! I added HiDPI support for them and best of all... :unicorn: :rainbow:  :sparkles: **_animated cursors_** :sparkles: :rainbow: :unicorn: 

I took some pics and recorded a video of the feature but it was actually pretty boring. I'm not really sure why I'm that excited about cursors above all but... Yeah! Cursors!

As usual the technical stuff is in the code comments and the commit descriptions but, in short, the theme is as big as the most scaled screen to avoid regenerating it a lot of times and it makes the code a lot simpler. It looks right everywhere, we just avoid potentially reloading the theme a lot.

Pls test kthxbai

author: cg9999

body:
@cg9999 thanks a lot for testing and debugging!

> WARNING: Entering a window while a window is hovered should never happen in DisplayServer.

I've noticed that too. It's new, probably coming from some new change I've not yet chased, like #80279. Sounds like more sanity checking to me, which never hurts and is honestly great to see. 

I'm not sure how to replicate it but chasing that PR is in my TODO list, which should fix it.

> Icon shown in Waybar is wrong when in project manager:

I'm not really sure how to handle this. There's no icon setting API. From what I understand compositors and Waybar rely on a `.desktop` file for the icon and whatnot. I'm not sure why it's that way but I hope that it will be fixed with some proper "packaging".

> After selecting project, also name is wrong. I'm not sure why KiCad was shown instead. KiCad is not running either.

I can only suppose that waybar is "dumb" and doesn't show the actual name of the window, instead relying, still, on some `.desktop` file. Then hopefully this will be eventually fixed at the same time with the icon issue.

> Sometimes, when selecting the project from the project list, the project manager disappears, but the project won't start loading until a very long time. (10-30 minutes maybe?).

This is probably the weirdest. Judging from the backtrace you gave it's just a roundtrip message, which means that we send a message and wait for a reply to make sure that every message we queued up got read by the compositor. It's just a synchronization method... That's very weird.

For the record I'm pretty sure that `try_set_mode` is pretty hacky (IIRC we can't really depend on some assumptions did there regarding window mode setting but I'm not sure) but not that much to break a simple roundtrip. I'm seriously confused.

> I've had one crash when selecting project from the manager.

That's a bit weird. First of all, the thread cleanup error is my fault, I forgot to clean stuff up on a late init error like that. I've put this in my TODO list.

I could actually replicate similar issues over time myself on sway. For some reason certain globals just... Disappear. I'm not sure if they "pop in" at different times or something like that. If you ever manage to replicate this somewhat consistently, a log with `WAYLAND_DEBUG=1` would help, but don't feel forced to do so, this stuff's annoying exactly because it's inconsistent.

This will probably become less of an issue with time though as I eventually plan to reduce to the minimum the amount of needed globals as an effort to increase compatibility.

Could you give me some info regarding your setup such as GPU, distro and the compositor/DE used? That would be useful.

Again, thanks a lot for testing!

author: cg9999

body:
@cg9999

> Yeah, this one is weird. The first time it happened, I had several processes stuck in the same state and they actually all recovered simultaneously maybe 10-30 min after they had been started.

Uh. That sounds like a compositor issue tbh.

> Ok, had another crash with WAYLAND_DEBUG=1 the signature is bit different on this.

I know this one well. For some reason it crashes while pushing a mouse motion message in the Wayland->Godot queue. I'm really not sure why.

That events gets sent _a lot_ so perhaps there's some cap we're hitting? The crashing code is inside some allocation stuff and I suppose that perhaps there could be some failed allocation causing a null dereference.

This one in particular is really annoying me and I think I'll take a very close look to it because it's very important if the Wayland thread can't talk with the main thread properly.

>  Here's a log where the process hung and continued again after I started typing in firefox. It hung on line 4539 (I added annotation in the file)

Uh, the weird thing is that it stalled right on the `wl_callback::done` event, which is the server reply. This isn't normal. It's stalling in a libwayland-client method, where it spinlocks dispatching new events and checking whether a reply flag is set.

I have a very strong feeling that this might be a compositor/libwayland-client issue since it also happens with other clients and for this particular issue I'd see if this happens too another compositor like sway or kwin too while at it.

Edit: yup, something is weird with `List::push_back`. `_data` is `nullptr`.
author: deralmas

body:
@cg9999 I think I figured the reason for the weird mouse motion crash and I should've fixed it (I couldn't replicate the issue after some light testing). Please tell me if that's the case.

In short, while fixing the missing wayland error message issue I also added a second unconditional event dispatch which the docs recommended. The thing is that I very dumbly forgot to lock the main mutex there, so race conditions happened.
author: deralmas

body:
Ok folks, new important thing: I implemented proper fractional scaling. Godot's automatic scaling won't recognize it due to differences with Wayland but if your compositor supports the fractional scale protocol it should now look less blurry when you have everything scaled to 1.5x.

The other important thing is that now, when resizing, **everything will get squashed around until the next repaint.** I'm not really sure how I could bring the old behaviour back, especially with fractional scaling on which is the reason of this weird change, but tbh I kinda think that it might be an improvement since stuff doesn't overflow from the decoration, albeit it feels a bit less snappy.

As usual, please tell me if anything weird pops up while scaling, especially now that this change is up.

author: Domeee

body:
@Domeee thanks a lot for the log!

All the outputs report their size properly so that's not the issue. I have an idea on why this might be happening: since integer output-derived scale can always change, I built a very simple method (too much actually) to get the scale of the last output the window entered, *fallbacking to 1 if there's no info*.

Once the screen cycles its power, it probably gets registered as a new output and for some reason the window doesn't receive a "enter output event", thus having no idea of the scale of the output it's on.

A quick way of confirming this would be to move the window across all the screens and see if anything changes, if everything else works properly that is.

We unfortunately need some more logging in there to further confirm this, so I've pushed a new commit which adds some new verbose logging entries: "window entered output", "window left output" and "window has no output associated, returning scale of 1".

Thanks for your help!

body:
@Domeee thanks for your help, we're finally onto something!

My theory was right, we're indeed hitting that fallback case where we have no idea about what output we're on.

I'm still not really sure about the details, but one thing that stands out is that it enters a new output without any scale info (defaults to 1) and yet complains about the window not having one associated...

Anyways, while it enters this weird output, by what I can see it looks like the window is on a good output also, so the obvious solution is to improve the scale getting algorithm by looking at all the outputs the window's on, a thing that was already much overdue.

Regarding the weird output... I'm not really sure what's going on and might as well be some weird edge case related with suspend and perhaps even the compositor itself. I think that it might be better to not think too much about it for now.
author: deralmas

body:
All right sorry for the double post, and I indeed thought about the new output too much.

It's libdecor once again. The fix is simple: just label outputs too as our own.

Let's see if I can pull this one off quickly as my time is little.

Edit: There, the fix should be up. I'll also improve buffer scale calculation so that the issue will not happen either way, thanks for all your help and patience!

author: stalkerg

body:
Hi @stalkerg, thanks for testing!

> cursor not show real position and in logs I can see...

The icon warning is normal, Wayland doesn't have a window icon API.

The cursor thing is weird though.

A full verbose log with `WAYLAND_DEBUG=1` would help. There's something wrong with cursor theme loading probably.

> also UI scale too big (looks like 2x but should be 1.3)

That's a known limitation as written in the PR description. Godot asks for the scale of all screens, which are limited to integers, so we're at the mercy of the compositor, which apparently rounds to the next biggest integer.

This could be fixed with a `DisplayServer` API change but this would have to be discussed outside of this PR and would take time, so I'm leaving the situation like this for now to work on other more important stuff when I have time.

No biggie though, this just influences the automatic scale. You can set a proper fractional scale in the editor settings and it should bring over to the project manager IIRC.

If you're not seeing blurry pixels, fractional scaling is doing its thing.

> With verbose I can see more information's

The "not all arguments converted..." means that I wrongly wrote a debug statement :sweat_smile: 

> seems like it try to scale up again and again.

The fact that the scaling message gets repeated is normal, but the fact that the size keeps growing is indeed weird, especially as it seems to come from the compositor (that "XDG toplevel..." message should report 1:1 what it gets).

I also notice that you're not using libdecor, which probably explains this last weirdness. You see, since not all Wayland compositors support server-side decorations (letting the compositor draw the window frame), I've had to add an optional, dynamic hook for this library that wraps the whole window API and add extra branches for it.

Admittedly the "plain" code branch you've running has been tested very little lately, so I wouldn't be surprised if you stumbled on some issues, sorry.

I'll put some extra love for libdecor-less setups in my TODO list especially now that we got more people with them.

>  I will try to fix it.

Thanks for your willingness to help and don't hesitate to contact me if you need help understanding the codebase!

Please though tell me on what you're exactly working on so that we can avoid working on the same thing; this PR is still WIP (I defined it in the description as beta quality) and the code is everchanging, so I wouldn't want to waste your time by changing something somewhere while you were touching that part.

author: stalkerg

body:
> can we use this API https://wayland.app/protocols/fractional-scale-v1 ?

We're already using it, hence it being not blurry. Wayland-side, scaling is just letting us have control of the size of the image so that it's not blurry, putting it in simple terms.

The UI size issue is related to how Godot is built.

> Yes, as I mentioned I am using KDE (KWIn), which provides SSD.

That's why libdecor is optional. I just didn't have time to test the non-libdecor branch since it's less requested.

> But my interest is mostly to seamless work under KWin Wayland session.

Seamless how? That's a bit generic. Is there some other issue?

> yes, we should use a special KDE protocol for that (it's trivial over Dbus).

Wait, is it a Wayland protocol or a DBus one? I'd be interested but this would also probably be lowish priority since there's bigger stuff to implement still.

> I installed libdecor and window behaviour became much more stable. DPI is still bigger but not so much. SSD is also used properly. Maybe libdecor should be a hard dependency for Godot?

I don't think so, primarily because I'd much prefer to add native CSDs to godot. This library has some issues I have to report, is a whole extra dependency which not everybody has and as you've seen there's no need to have it for SSD enabled compositors. Seeing a game refuse to boot because it can't draw CSDs is a bit silly IMO.

In other words it's just a stopgap solution to keep the GNOME people happy. The issues you've seen with a "pure" setup are not intended and should be fixed.

> one moment, does it mean Godot doesn't support fractional scale?

It does. It's just that it calculates the automatic _UI_ scale from the display scale, which on Wayland is always an integer. Fractional scaling is a per-window extension.
author: deralmas

body:
> I see, godot for some crazy reason uses the next int number for scaling. Why not just round? Or directly proper number. I have only one screen.

Godot's UI scale is not dynamic (yet), so the next best thing is to query each screen and look for the biggest UI size to use from the beginning as we can't change it.

On Wayland, screens are only integer-scaled so that's what Godot gets. The fractional part is just a per-window extension, not per-screen.
author: deralmas

body:
Thank you. Yes, I read the source code carefully and found it as well. 
Can we instead `wayland_thread.screen_get_data(p_screen).scale;` in  `screen_get_scale` start using `fractional_scale` from `main_window`? I believe in most cases, it will be much more accurate. 
Also, it seems like we got `fractional_scale` before Godot asked for a screen scale, and we have an option to just calculate the scale by ourselves from DPI and physical_width/physical_height .
But I suppose the first way will be better until we support only one window now. 

For app icon (yes not windows icon) we can generate .desktop file and put icon in the default folder if it does not exist.

author: stalkerg

body:
One more issue:
the cursor is too small. 
UPDATE:
In  `wl_surface_set_buffer_scale(p_ss->cursor_surface, scale);`  scale should be 1 because the closest number to my screen scale 1.2 is 1. 

From docs:
> The purpose of this request is to allow clients to supply higher resolution buffer data for use on high resolution outputs. It is intended that you pick **the same buffer scale as the scale of the output** that the surface is displayed on. This means the compositor can avoid scaling when rendering the surface on that output.

UPDATE v2:
These changes helped me:
```patch
-               wl_surface_set_buffer_scale(p_ss->cursor_surface, scale);
+
+               float fractional_scale = p_ss->wayland_thread->main_window.fractional_scale;
+               if (fractional_scale > 0) {
+                       wl_surface_set_buffer_scale(p_ss->cursor_surface, round(fractional_scale));
+               } else {
+                       wl_surface_set_buffer_scale(p_ss->cursor_surface, scale);
+               }
+
```

but maybe we should do it in `_update_scale` but at this time the `fractional_scale` not yet ready.

UPDATE v3:
I also have any errors like:
```
ERROR: Parameter "wl_cursor" is null.
   at: seat_state_update_cursor (platform/linuxbsd/wayland/wayland_thread.cpp:2761)
```

it's happened on the borders of panels, I suppose in that state cursor should be changed to "drag" but it's breaking something.
author: stalkerg

body:
@stalkerg

> Can we instead wayland_thread.screen_get_data(p_screen).scale; in screen_get_scale start using fractional_scale from main_window? I believe in most cases, it will be much more accurate.

Mh, that feels a bit like a hack and I fear that it might give issues on users with multiple monitors. The fractional scale can change between monitors since it's from the PoV of a window, so if you have a scale 2 screen somewhere and you're running the UI on an x1.2 screen it will be undersized for the scale 2 screen, which is what the current core logic is trying to avoid.

I'm not really sure that it would be worth it to change the behaviour of a method which is also one of the few we can actually fully implement (see a lot of the stubbed "not supported" methods) just to workaround a flaw in the core instead of fixing it directly.

> I am using SSD and with libdecor currently, everything is fine.

Actually libdecor has some weird bugs when it comes to toggling SSDs. You can't make a window borderless for some reason. I plan to report them eventually.

Implementing two paths is not that big of a burden since the API is similar and it's worth it considering that it would otherwise not boot.

To be completely honest, I didn't test that path in a long while and the fact that it booted up at all shows what I mean well :sweat_smile: 

>  the cursor is too small.

Oh right, I forgot to add fractional scaling to the cursor! The proper fix is a bit more complex; I'll put this in my TODO list.

> In wl_surface_set_buffer_scale(p_ss->cursor_surface, scale); scale should be 1 because the closest number to my screen scale 1.2 is 1.

Actually, your screen scale is 2. Again, I'm not sure if this is a protocol thing or compositor policy but your compositor is returning 2 when asked about the screen size, so we're doing what the spec asks.

As said above the fix is a bit more complex (implementing proper fractional scale handling in the cursor handling logic).

With the fix you've supplied your cursor is actually _smaller_ than intended, since it should be scaled by 1.2.

>  I also have any errors like:
> [snip]
> it's happened on the borders of panels, I suppose in that state cursor should be changed to "drag" but it's breaking something.

Yup, it can't find a dragging cursor from your cursor theme. The fix for the error spam should be trivial, but I'd be interested in what cursor theme you're using so perhaps we can add some better logic for fetching all cursors.
author: deralmas

body:
> Yup, it can't find a dragging cursor from your cursor theme. The fix for the error spam should be trivial, but I'd be interested in what cursor theme you're using so perhaps we can add some better logic for fetching all cursors.

It's the default KDE theme, and it 100% includes drag icons.

> Mh, that feels a bit like a hack and I fear that it might give issues on users with multiple monitors. The fractional scale can change between monitors since it's from the PoV of a window, so if you have a scale 2 screen somewhere and you're running the UI on an x1.2 screen it will be undersized for the scale 2 screen, which is what the current core logic is trying to avoid.

> Actually, your screen scale is 2. Again, I'm not sure if this is a protocol thing or compositor policy but your compositor is returning 2 when asked about the screen size, so we're doing what the spec asks.

Honestly, all this is a big hack. In wayland we can support, without hacks, only retina like 2x/3x HiDPI on the screens level or fractional scale on windows level. Because we have already used some heuristics (it's a better word here instead of hack), I suppose my heuristic is much better. 
1. 90% of users use one monitor.
2. Most existing monitors for PCs currently have a fractional scale (they have no double DPI). You can check web statistics. 
3. and current heuristic is not working correctly for most users. At the same time, my way will work fine even for 2x/3x. 
4. for the multi-monitor system, using the current display results is also a good solution, in KDE/Gnome, you set the main screen and a new application will be open on such a screen, and I believe we should set it as the main for a reason. Anyway, getting the current main window fractional scale, even for multi-monitors systems good enough and will be mostly expected. 

Shortly: the solution should first covered most popular devices and configurations. 
About 2 from compositor: it's just stupid Wayland behaviour, **if we start using fractional scale, we can't look into int scale anymore!**

> The proper fix is a bit more complex; 

for example? Seems like we can scale the cursor by ourselves and avoid `wl_surface_set_buffer_scale`.
author: stalkerg

body:
@stalkerg I'll go a bit out of order in responding to ease up writing as this became a bit heavy for some reason. I hope I haven't missed anything.

Uh, also I missed this question for some reason:

> For app icon (yes not windows icon) we can generate .desktop file and put icon in the default folder if it does not exist.

We already have one, and I'm pretty sure that this is packaging related. Generating a .desktop would mean that the executable is static, and at that point it becomes responsibility of the distros' package manager, which already put it in the right place.

> It's the default KDE theme, and it 100% includes drag icons.

All right, I'll look the names it uses. Our current cursor name list comes from the X11 backend as the themes have the same format but it looks like this is not enough.

> for example? Seems like we can scale the cursor by ourselves and avoid wl_surface_set_buffer_scale.

For example implementing the fractional scaling protocol as I wrote. That's exactly how it works.

The cursor is just a surface, just like a toplevel, so for doing what we want we have to tell the compositor that we know what we're doing, otherwise it will scale everything down by itself and blur everything in the process.

> Honestly, all this is a big hack. In wayland we can support, without hacks, only retina like 2x/3x HiDPI on the screens level or fractional scale on windows level. Because we have already used some heuristics (it's a better word here instead of hack), I suppose my heuristic is much better.

I'm not sure what heuristics you're talking about. The method clearly says, in both the name and the documentation, that it returns the _screen scale_.

The fact that an internal, hack-ish (once UI scaling is dynamic the issue will go away) core behaviour depends on this method doesn't justify IMO going out of doc.

Again, we can discuss with the other platform people to see if there's a better solution.

Even if we implemented your approach, it still wouldn't cover the case where the application is started in a scale 1 display (which would still return a "fractional" scale actually) and then moved in a 1.2 display. It'd still be flawed.

> and current heuristic is not working correctly for most users. At the same time, my way will work fine even for 2x/3x.

There's no heuristic. What's done is core-side which doesn't mesh well with Wayland as said in this message.

> for the multi-monitor system, using the current display results is also a good solution, in KDE/Gnome, you set the main screen and a new application will be open on such a screen, and I believe we should set it as the main for a reason. Anyway, getting the current main window fractional scale, even for multi-monitors systems good enough and will be mostly expected.

That's in KDE/Gnome, but the whole point of standardizing every protocol is to depend as little as possible on a specific compositor. We can't just ask people to open their window in the scale they want. It's just simpler to set it themselves.

> About 2 from compositor: it's just stupid Wayland behaviour, if we start using fractional scale, we can't look into int scale anymore!

The closest thing we have to fractional per-screen info is... integer scale info. That's it.

This is a Godot API limitation. Just as the window API doesn't mesh well with Wayland's, the scale handling doesn't. No biggie, we can talk about it with the other maintainers.

We'll figure it out, just like with multi windowing.

> Shortly: the solution should first covered most popular devices and configurations.

This would break every other setup, which is the whole reason "Wayland" is taking so much time to have all the features people want.

Wayland people can already run the engine, in _multi window mode_ through XWayland. The single window limitation is already going to be a blow to public perception.

It doesn't make sense to rush through this small detail with such consequences, it'd make people doubt of the stability of this technology and people are already very emotional about it for some reason.

Really, please, let's go slow and steady; this is a new, very different display server and as such there's lots of work to make Godot's API embrace it too. Making a method return funky values just because of an internal detail is reckless IMO.

This isn't the only detail I had to discuss with the other maintainers to fit nicely into the code base. We don't have deadlines, there's no bureaucracy to go through to talk to other teams, we can do it as good as possible.

Also note that the same scaling API we're discussing was explicitly and mostly made for MacOS, so this is not the only platform requiring some rethinking to work properly.

I genuinely appreciate your effort and willingness to improve this backend but I don't think that this is the right approach.
author: deralmas

body:
> Wayland people can already run the engine, in multi window mode through XWayland. The single window limitation is already going to be a blow to public perception.

it's not working for me, this is why I come here. Godot 4.1 is broken for KDE Wayland session (all windows shrinking automatically). 

> I'm not sure what heuristics you're talking about. The method clearly says, in both the name and the documentation, that it returns the screen scale.
> The closest thing we have to fractional per-screen info is... integer scale info. That's it.

I believe this is a key misunderstanding point. The screen scale and fractional window scale are two different mechanisms for two different cases. If you try to use "fractional window" you can't start looking into the screen scale. I understand, what "screen scale" it's what Godot expected in the first place but if we already decided to use "fractional window" we should just create two completely different paths (screen scale and window scale with fractional support).

> Even if we implemented your approach, it still wouldn't cover the case where the application is started in a scale 1 display (which would still return a "fractional" scale actually) and then moved in a 1.2 display. It'd still be flawed.

Honestly, it's already a case, and if you want to keep the original Godot DPI you should normalize by 120 (1.2). 

> This would break every other setup, which is the whole reason "Wayland" is taking so much time to have all the features people want.

no at all, I did change only for the Wayland display server. If you are about some multi-monitor setup - it's fine, after all, 90% of users will work correctly out of the box. 

> It doesn't make sense to rush through this small detail with such consequences, it'd make people doubt of the stability of this technology and people are already very emotional about it for some reason.

hmm I see. Sorry seems like I misunderstood this PR. From my point of view, if we can make people happy right now by a few lines of code we should do it. All corner cases (multi-monitor setup) we can solve after.
author: stalkerg

body:
I understand you are trying to keep this PR as PoC to find a better architecture and help other display server implementations. 
I want to propose a more practical approach. Wayland here for more than 6 years and most distributors use Wayland by default. I don't think XWayland is a good solution, it's from time to time broken. 
Even experimental support which can cover most Wayland users will be better than nothing. 
If you suppose we can find a better way before the 4.2 release I am okay with it, but otherwise, I want to propose a more practical approach. 
Anyway, thank you for your work and your responses. Also, I apologize if I rush and push too much.
author: stalkerg

body:
@stalkerg all right. I understand. Now everything makes more sense.

Just to clear things up, _this is not a PoC_. This is voluntary work so we (usually) have the luxury of doing things slow and "right", which is especially important for something of this scale, which when not done properly can break in subtle ways.

Wayland's design doesn't help since it's very different from other platforms and we have very little information to work with in the first place, so this means that we either do things properly or we don't at all.

Frame throttling for example, an essential feature needed to avoid stalling while the window is hidden, took a painful amount of time to figure out to do both "properly" and without hacking `main/main.cpp`. It's that annoying.

But, now that you told me the reason I finally understand why you felt everything was going too slow.

I'm aware at how bad XWayland support is (probably due how little love has the X11 backend got), so much that I had to make a tracker over at #78581. I seriously hoped it would've been better despite feeling the pressure but I can't really go faster than this without burning out, so, we're going to change plans.

I discussed with the rest of the team and, since this PR has been usable for a while and the XWayland situation is going so awfully, instead of implementing every feature possible I'm going to stop doing that and wrap up this PR, doing everything in my TODO list, documenting and so on.

Sadly I realized this too late, and so, considering that feature freeze is close it wouldn't be practical to merge this PR so that it lands in 4.2.  Even just the review times would take quite a lot and it would leave me with mere days to wrap this up which isn't doable. Sorry.

Don't worry though. Unless something goes extremely wrong, this will land, experimental or not, into 4.3.

That said, now the priorities change and the issue we've been discussing will be fixed later in the wrapping up process. If we can't find a satisfactory solution in time I agree then than a workaround is needed akin to what you've proposed.

But yeah. In short, my plans were to do it slow and steady but it's not becoming practical as you pointed out due to the growing pains with XWayland.

I ask you a little bit more patience. I'm doing my best.

Thank you for your interest in this feature and letting me realize how bad things were out there.
author: deralmas

body:
> I discussed with the rest of the team and, since this PR has been usable for a while and the XWayland situation is going so awfully, instead of implementing every feature possible I'm going to stop doing that and wrap up this PR, doing everything in my TODO list, documenting and so on.

Good! Thank you for your response, I suppose it's a good strategy. If you have something from your TODO that I can implement (maybe something more isolated) I can try it. Also if you have IRC, discord, telegram etc for more quick discussions I want to join if it's possible.

author: TuxTheAstronaut

body:
@TuxTheAstronaut all right I've investigated further and it actually happens on both drivers. The fact that it works in the first place sometimes is just some blind timing luck.

The issue is kinda complex, so I'll try to simplify the issue as much as possible.

In short: due to the fact that Wayland tells us when we can draw, we have to treat (render-side) the window as almost always hidden.

That means that, just as when we don't have any visible window, the main loop goes faster.

The main loop is faster so we have a smaller delta time, which isn't really a problem, _but_ this smaller delta time is also passed to the shaders, which get called seldom since we're skipping drawing a lot. So the shader think that passed a lot less time than actually did since the last drawing.

The solution (I think) would be to pass to the shaders the actual time that passed since the last _actual_ draw.

I'll discuss this with the rendering people, thanks for the report!

body:
I know this PR won't be implementing multi window, but it may be worth looking at the proposed protocol that would (hopefully) fix issues with multi window applications, but there might be more that i'm missing here:
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/wayland/wayland-protocols/-/merge_requests/247
author: gerblesh

body:
@gerblesh hi!

Window positioning isn't the issue and instead something more intrinsic to the current Godot windowing API.

The issue is that there's no unambiguous way to know the class of the window we're going to create or, in other words, we have no sure way of knowing whether we're going to create an `xdg_toplevel` or an `xdg_popup`.

X11 actually has some similar issues since it has its own concept of a class (messing up stuff like tiling WMs) but its WMs are more lenient as it's pretty much tacked on, while on Wayland it's mandatory that we figure the right window type from the start, otherwise we get a really unusable mess (e.g. a tooltip registered as a toplevel would be catastrophic).

Fixing this, especially while preserving compatibility and not introducing duplicate or messy APIs would require _a lot_ of effort, and currently this PR as is is already a great improvement over the Wayland experience, especially for tiling compositor users like myself.

I'm really sorry that multi-windowing won't be implemented but I fear that a proper fix will require way more work outside of the Wayland backend itself.

That said, I want to note that, given the current constraints of the Wayland protocol, _it's more than doable_. We just have to figure out how to clear things up from our side, which I think will probably also make the life better for the users of other backends too in the long term.

author: afk-mario

body:
Hi @afk-mario, I don't think that is needed for multi window support. The proposal you linked allows to run multiple godot instances with one project, while we want to instantiate multiple windows within the same instance. 

The main blocker we have right now is the fact that we have no way of properly detecting whether a window is a popup or a toplevel. That should be fixed in the first place as also X11 is suffering a bit from it (think of all the tiling WMs issues it has), but it's a bit more leninent so it wasn't that obvious.

That can be fixed but it will require some elbow grease and communication between the various maintainers involved. It should be more than doable but I'd like to tackle this after we ship out a working Wayland backend as to not delay the whole thing further.

author: Domeee

body:
@Domeee uh. That's _very_ weird. The whole point of `WaylandThread` is to be independent of the main thread as far as possible, since locking the Wayland loop up can fill up the internal event queue and crash (what seems to be happening to you).

Are you doing something funky with `DisplayServer`? The only points of failure that come to mind are the `DisplayServer` taking up the wayland mutex too long and a very very long list of queued inputs (which are sent from the thread to the DS).

author: TuxTheAstronaut

body:
@TuxTheAstronaut that's due to vsync emulation. As you noticed is not a true value.

In short, our window is constantly not drawable (the compositor has to tell us) and so we process with the low processor usage mode time, which I had to override to 2000 FPS (otherwise we might miss the draw message too often, sorry)

**Note that you're not drawing at 2000 FPS, just `_process`ing.**

The FPS counter currently tracks plain loops, drawn or not, so it's just reporting the tickrate. It could (should?) be fixed to only track renderer calls but that's for another PR.

author: orowith2os

body:
@orowith2os

> When that's fixed, would it be possible to change the behavior here to properly report the FPS of the screen the game is currently located on? Wayland should be able to provide you with enough information somewhere for that.

In short: it's doable but not exactly how you think and it will require some other API changes to the `DisplayServer`.

Basically, while we can know a window's refresh rate based on the outputs it covers, we can't get its "main" output index, which Godot wants. This is actually a bigger issue with the DS API: Wayland is designed to be a mostly window-centric API (where you "ask" a window further info), while Godot is almost entirely output-centric. Another example of this issue can be seen with auto scale on "fractional screens": the fractional scale factor is actually only exposed to the individual windows, making `DisplayServer::screen_get_scale`, and thus auto-scale, inaccurate.

I think that this will be post-merge material, as I'll have to discuss the issues in depth and that will probably take a bit once it happens.

author: deralmas

body:
I was wondering if the actual rendering of the game, and disconnecting it from the game's logic tick speed, would be possible, which you answered, I think.

If your API reports 2000 FPS, does the game actually render at 2000 FPS, or does it say that and in reality render at a sane speed, with the internal logic running at that spoofed speed? That is what I was asking.
author: orowith2os

body:
@orowith2os in short, it is indeed only ticking at 2000 Hz and rendering at a regular pace.

Actually, the whole reason we tick this fast in the first place is exactly to allow the game to render at a consistent enough pace, as due to various (and actually kinda annoying) reasons we have to manually poll for a `wl_surface::frame` event and handle one ourselves.

If you're curious, the full story is pretty complicated. I can go into further technical detail if you'd like, but in short, we actually disable vsync (still no tearing, think of MAILBOX) and pace the frames based on when the compositor tells us to draw. This is needed because otherwise we'd stall every time the window's occluded as the rendering is on the same thread of the main loop. A pretty good explanation is here: https://emersion.fr/blog/2018/wayland-rendering-loop/ 

So, yeah, the game doesn't actually render at 2000 FPS, the counter is wrong for the reasons explained to tux above. It just ticks at that rate as you asked. :)

Edit: BTW don't hesitate to ask further questions if something is a bit unclear or you're more curious :)
author: deralmas

body: @Riteo just to be clear, like in the blog post, this means no busy loop? I really don't like it when frame rates go beyond what makes a difference and into heat-up-the-room territory :P
author: ndren

body:
@ndren kinda. The _process loop_ is hard-capped at 2000Hz as we still have to poll the wayland frame event, but _rendering_ is indeed only run at vsync rates.

I think that this could be even more efficient (and most importantly have a better UX), but this looks like a good compromise and definitely doesn't increase my pretty modest laptop's clock.

author: nathanfranke

body:
@nathanfranke Your report clearly shows me that there are some major UX issues. Thanks a lot for testing and pointing them out! 

> My first launch failed due to missing libdecor. I had to install it with pacman -S libdecor.

Uh oh. That's not normal. Libdecor is simply a stopgap solution to implement CSDs for compositors that enforce it. It should be _never_ mandatory though. I'll look into it.

> Very suspiciously, project fps is locked to 2060 on my 60Hz screen and 2240 on my 240Hz screen, exactly 2000 too much in both cases... 🤔

Your suspicion is correct, something's going on. Very confusingly, though, this is (mostly) intended. I talked about it at lengths in this PR, so I'll make it extremely short: due to various limitations and the way Wayland is built, we have to manually poll for when we can draw with a sort of workaround (forcing the "low processor usage mode" time to 2000Hz).

The result of this is that the process loop goes very fast (2000Hz) to have decent frame times, with actually only a few of its iterations drawing, depending on what the compositor tells us. 

Thing is, the FPS counter actually counts _any_ process loop, independently of whether they're drawn or not. There's also a bug in the way that the delta time is passed for a similar reason: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/82222

Not sure why it's doesn't report exactly 2000 FPS though.

> Log also says: "The requested V-Sync mode Disabled is not available. Falling back to V-Sync mode Enabled."

That's normal. That's compositor dependent. Apparently mutter does not yet support tearing: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/mutter/-/issues/2517
author: deralmas

body:
@nathanfranke oopsie missed some points:

> Launching project is always maximized, no matter what I set to display/window/size/mode.

That's weird. I'll look into that too.

> Editor and project don't have application icons. Log says: "Icon not supported by this display server."

That's expected unfortunately. Wayland by design doesn't allow setting arbitrary icons, instead they have to be set by their corresponding `.desktop` somehow. I'm not sure of the current state of the UX of this workflow but I don't expect it to be that great.

author: deralmas

body:
So I tested this a bit more / skimmed through the source code.

- Compose events with deadkeys are missing. I added them [here](https://github.com/arlo-phoenix/godot/commit/5b0a669f38df78d54b0afe6739a553192808f0d4), but there is a lot of copy paste going on (I kept the old code and just added compose handling even though events are constructed the same). 
- Window Size is wrong and just stays the size defined in the editor. E.g. when you have the window as 1280:720 and make it full screen on a 1920:1080 monitor, the window size stays the same. This also messes with control positioning and causes them to be incorrect to the anchors
- there seems to be no way to get fractional scale (I thought it would be in content scale, but that stayed at 1)

I also noticed that some pointers to xkb structs are in the wrong spot (tables can be shared between windows), didn't move them in my commit, but this will likely bring trouble when adding multi window support. 


> Due to the urgency of this PR, this list is purely informative and should not be treated as an hard roadmap for merging.

Regarding the urgency: 
This PR already offers basic Wayland support. But we already have basic Wayland support at home: XWayland.
I recently retested XWayland with Godot (last time I did was last year) and it's quite usable right now, at least didn't run into any major issues compared to before. Also with multi window support missing anyway / fractional scaling not fully working I don't think this will be in a state better than just using XWayland for 4.3 without major efforts. Maybe I'm overestimating the required stuff, but imo if this gets merged, wayland should on default be **disabled** at compile time and not actually be in the released binaries. Like for just using the editor and development this is already looking great and I'll continue daily driving this, thanks for your work! But seeing as you still have unknown crashes in Known Issues and there being lots of small issues for development, but big for actual releases (e.g. no icon, seems hard on wayland, xwayland has it) this would likely bring trouble to the people actually releasing games on Linux.
