Then again I'd say probably less than half a percent of gamers run wayland and even less people release games there with latest version so wouldn't be the end of the world.

Maybe I'm exaggerating small problems here, but I just saw "Due by March 03, 2024" on the 4.3 milestone and that doesn't seem possible without major efforts still this month which I haven't seen yet. Imo this should be merged before an Alpha for a long enough testing phase if it gets enabled on default (or does that due not mean actual release, if it's later, let's say May, the remaining issues should be fixable).

Alternatively since this will likely be a major part of 4.3, at least advertise it correctly and only call it basic support and include the list of remaining issues below (that's always done anyways, but I mean separate wayland list)


> About the fractional scale: Maybe you're looking for the setting `Display Scale` in the editor settings?

Thanks for the tip, but I mean the scaling used within the window (in WaylandThread window_state_get_scale_factor, I at least think that's the correct one, confused cause WindowSize isn't actual WindowSize so couldn't test it). I have a workaround, just annoyed me earlier.
author: arlo-phoenix

body:
@arlo-phoenix thanks a lot for taking a look! You brought out some great points!

> Compose events with deadkeys are missing. I added them [here](https://github.com/arlo-phoenix/godot/commit/5b0a669f38df78d54b0afe6739a553192808f0d4), but there is a lot of copy paste going on (I kept the old code and just added compose handling even though events are constructed the same).

Thanks a lot for bringing this issue up along with a patch! Yeah, the event setup logic is very... Weird. I could definitely take a look at refactoring it properly, perhaps adding your patch in the process if you'd like (with proper attribution of course :) ).

> Window Size is wrong and just stays the size defined in the editor. E.g. when you have the window as 1280:720 and make it full screen on a 1920:1080 monitor, the window size stays the same. This also messes with control positioning and causes them to be incorrect to the anchors

That's weird. We definitely call the rect change callback IIRC. Perhaps it just doesn't get sent while changing to fullscreen? It might also be related to "no-op size updates", some conditions set up to avoid useless rescaling/resize events. I'll take a closer look when I'll have the time.

> there seems to be no way to get fractional scale (I thought it would be in content scale, but that stayed at 1)

Indeed, that's a known issue. This is mostly a Godot plumbing thing, as Wayland exposes this property only per-window and the only API we have in `DisplayServer` is per-screen. Now that we're finally out of feature freeze I'll definitely make a PR with a `DisplayServer::window_get_scale` method and see where it goes.

> I also noticed that some pointers to xkb structs are in the wrong spot (tables can be shared between windows), didn't move them in my commit, but this will likely bring trouble when adding multi window support.

Thanks for pointing this out! As we're still in singlewin this isn't really of high priority, so I'll put this into my TODO list next to the stuff required for multiwin.

> This PR already offers basic Wayland support. But we already have basic Wayland support at home: XWayland.
I recently retested XWayland with Godot (last time I did was last year) and it's quite usable right now, at least didn't run into any major issues compared to before.

I thought (and hoped) the same, but XWayland has a lot of subtle issues which are making people's life pretty miserable depending on your setup, which also layer up with the regular X11 issues. I made a tracker some time ago as people got confused during the Wayland transition of some major distros: #78581.

> Also with multi window support missing anyway / fractional scaling not fully working I don't think this will be in a state better than just using XWayland for 4.3 without major efforts.

I actually asked around and it looks like multi-windowing is not considered essential for merging and the work to retrofit Godot's API to talk in something that Wayland can understand is not trivial (would be much harder with an unmerged branch floating around) so it's not that important that we don't have it yet (I tried _a lot_ though and it just can't work with the current API :( ) 

Fractional scale is fully working; Godot is just not "aware" about it so the auto-scale factor is not accurate, but that's it, the content renders correctly. You can simply set a manual scale factor in the editor UI settings for the time being.

> Maybe I'm overestimating the required stuff, but imo if this gets merged, Wayland should on default be disabled at compile time and not actually be in the released binaries.

It can and does coexist with the X11 driver without issues. If there are major issues for the time being we can simply default to the X11 driver and fallback to the Wayland driver, perhaps labeling it as "experimental".

Letting more people test it in an easier way would help greatly at detecting issues and knowing what's needed for some good compatibility all around (most globals could have a lot more compatibility code for older/newer versions).

> Like for just using the editor and development this is already looking great and I'll continue daily driving this, thanks for your work!

I'm glad you're liking this, thanks a lot for using it! <3

> But seeing as you still have unknown crashes in Known Issues and there being lots of small issues for development, but big for actual releases (e.g. no icon, seems hard on wayland, xwayland has it) this would likely bring trouble to the people actually releasing games on Linux.

Indeed, until we get proper 100% support I think that the plan is to label this as experimental or anyways add some disclaimer that it still doesn't have 100% feature parity with X11. There's definitely lots of work to do to improve the UX on that front.

BTW, regarding that mysterious crash, it's pretty high priority and I'm planning to take a look as soon as I have enough time. I think that it's probably a Wayland event queue overflow, which is a very annoying thing that the `WaylandThread` class already avoids greatly, so _I hope_ that it should not take too much time to identify and fix it, but you never know what exotic bugs might lurk out there :P

---

Uh, there have been some further comments while I was writing this wall of text. Let's see what I can pick without repeating myself:

> There already is --display-driver x11 so maybe we could make it so that if you want to use wayland you need to add --display-driver wayland.

That's already the case! We just run Wayland first to avoid running into XWayland. Takes nothing to change the loading order X11 first :)

> imo it's unusable just because of little things like the icon

The icon will _never_ work with the traditional API, unless someone adds a protocol for that (but from what I can tell there's no intention about going that route). Wayland only supports the "FreeDesktop" approach of associating an app id with a `.desktop` file which in turn points to an icon.

We can definitely improve the UX a lot regarding this, but this is one of those things that will require to adapt Godot's API for Wayland and not the opposite sadly.

> Alternatively since this will likely be a major part of 4.3, at least advertise it correctly and only call it basic support and include the list of remaining issues below (that's always done anyways, but I mean separate wayland list)

I already said this above but I'll repeat myself to make sure that the point goes across: **this backend does not have by _any_ means absolute feature parity with X11 and I don't plan to advertise it in any way like that.**

Despite this having taken a lot of work this was just the first step. I did everything in my power to make Wayland "talk" like Godot, but some stuff is simply impossible and will require some "compromises" the other way around, which will be much easier with this PR merged.

The biggest example of this dynamic is multiwin support, which I tried to implement in _a lot_ of different ways for _an awful awful long time_ and failed. I'm still very sorry that this is "shipping" like this but it gets exponentially harder to polish in this state :( I truly think that merging it singlewin only is for the better at this point.

Don't let the phrase "first step" mislead you though. This is already pretty stable (that weird crash is actually not that common under normal usage) and there are already quite a lot of goodies, with a bunch more which should be pretty easily doable given the right software, hardware and a bit of time (IME, touch screen, fancy tablets, virtual keyboards are all good examples). It's a first step, yes, but a pretty big one if you ask me :P

To conclude, this is not an easy feat. There is a lot of work that has and will have to be done to integrate this fully. Sadly most of this work is invisible (e.g., the EGL driver, figuring out how to tick while occluded, X11 dynamic loading, etc.) but trust me, we're getting there.

---

Phew, that was a mouthful! Hope you didn't get bored with this wall of text and that all your questions got answered. If something isn't clear or I missed something, please let me know and I'll try to help you the best I can :)

@Riteo Thanks for the in depth reply! 

> > Window Size is wrong and just stays the size defined in the editor. E.g. when you have the window as 1280:720 and make it full screen on a 1920:1080 monitor, the window size stays the same. This also messes with control positioning and causes them to be incorrect to the anchors
> 
> That's weird. We definitely call the rect change callback IIRC. Perhaps it just doesn't get sent while changing to fullscreen? It might also be related to "no-op size updates", some conditions set up to avoid useless rescaling/resize events. I'll take a closer look when I'll have the time.

I plan to test this myself again as well. Weird issue.

> > There already is --display-driver x11 so maybe we could make it so that if you want to use wayland you need to add --display-driver wayland.
> 
> That's already the case! We just run Wayland first to avoid running into XWayland. Takes nothing to change the loading order X11 first :)

I meant reversing the setting (without setting any cmd-line args on wayland it will start wayland right now), but looking back that'd be pretty confusing as well so forget that. 

> Wayland only supports the "FreeDesktop" approach of associating an app id with a `.desktop` file which in turn points to an icon.

My bad then, I was sure that it was a portal topic and didn't recheck. Only major issue for me then is the window size.

> Yeah, the event setup logic is very... Weird. I could definitely take a look at refactoring it properly

I meant my own code lol. But what did confuse me is that input handling looked so different compared to the X11 server. X11 does use xkb (and some other stuff) just with different headers. Otherwise it looked fine, it's not like a complete copy is possible with WaylandThread being separate so I don't think major refactoring is needed there (at least for initial merge). I would recommend some refactoring before multi window though.

author: deralmas

body:
> @arlo-phoenix I got reason to believe that the weird fullscreen size issue you had might be related to the one that @Domeee got with scaling.
> 
> Could you see if you can still replicate it with the latest commit?

@Riteo Doesn't happen anymore, thanks!

- My issue wasn't the exact same, but this is the solveable part. I misunderstood wayland and thought I could immediately get the window size after setting it to fullscreen which imo should be possible at least for a project setting, but that'd would require extra logic or false setting it. So if you want to get the window size you need to use the `size_changed` callback which is better practice anyways so my bad. What was confusing is that the callback gets called 2-3 times instead of once with sizes along the way of scaling, but that's probably just wayland as well and it's not like that breaks anything. 

- Fractional scaling between monitors doesn't seem to work (that's what I meant before by fractional scaling not working among other things). Like if I go beyond the boundary of my monitor so more of the window is on my second monitor with a smaller fractional scale the window scaling shoots up. I didn't check in the code, I plan to debug this myself this week or at least attach an example so it's clearer what I mean. (this happens both pre 32128e00d6d3a774246159df352c234894b132e6 and after, just tested it again)

- How should events / how do they currently handle the fractional scaling. If I query mouse position I get the mouse position usable within the Godot window and I could create a control which constantly follows the mouse and it would work as expected. Now if you listen to InputEventMouseMotion on a `_gui_input()` you get the positional data that would need to be multiplied by the fractional scale or it doesn't actually fit to the Godot coordinate system. Should that data be already scaled by the current scaling or is this a topic for the wayland implementation of #82800 ? Also topic for me personally cause of the passthrough thingy, in it's current form I scale inside the implementation, since imo it's expected that you can e.g. just call a get_rect on a control in Godot and use it for passthrough instead of having to do any scaling as a user

- If you aren't easily able to trigger the input event queue overflow crash: A good test is importing a large glb/gltf file and being impatient like me / clicking way too times (not even that often, but some imports take 10s and you can easily crash it there, crash doesn't happen without any clicking so it's very likely the overflow). I say this will be a pretty common way that would trigger this crash anyways so could be a good test

- 
@stalkerg 
> > there seems to be no way to get fractional scale (I thought it would be in content scale, but that stayed at 1)
> 
> a few extra words - we have one heuristic that will work for all single monitor configurations. Unfortunately, we want to support all cases, and because @Riteo wants to implement it properly on the Godot level, we have not introduced it. But if something need urgent I still think it will be a good option.

Not doing anything production related rn so I'm fine with just my hardcoded constants :), thanks for the info though. I think if the points of fractional scaling above are addressed it is not necessary for 99.9% of people to get fractional scaling. Couldn't find much, but what does windows do? There's fractional scale there as well and if there's no method there, we don't need it either (or am I missing something). The only thing I found about this topic with a quick search is https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/2661, where someone suggests that you can kinda get it through the DPI changed callback.

---

Overall I did exaggerate my issues in my last comments, I still haven't found out how in the world I broke my control positions/sizes which is why I didn't comment, but clicking enough always breaks something (I messed with project settings so maybe there?) I'm pretty certain it is **not**  an issue related to this or wayland so forget that. I also wanna say I don't want this to become an issue list for this PR, imo this still should be merged very early alpha so more people test this. The only thing that actually matters imo is that crash and if there are any others hidden, all others have workarounds or are just nice to haves (e.g. window between screens). Just using this for the editor works great and the only time I crashed was the gltf import. It's already looking quite amazing in stability from my experience!

author: reduz

body:
> I will give it a check soon. From what I remember this checked whether resources that were part of sub-resources forced the main one to be saved.

Nearly right. With my PR the code is more aligned to the actual saving algorithm. While the saving algorithm will also save all sub resources, so e.g. an Array in a `.tres` file. But the before called `_find_edited_resources` does not check whether sub resouces are edited and therefore should be saved later on. 
Actually, my code can be further improved to also check nested resources of a nested resource and so on. Currently is does only check the first sub resource without going deeper, e.g. Array in Array won't be found with this.
That is probably more work given the current structure.

author: akien-mga

body:
A big warning here is that this PR is just addressing the specific issue with 3D textures, and doesn't fix all related issues, for example if a user tries to compress a 256x1 texture. After this PR is merged, when compressing a 256x1 texture, S3TC will revert to the old behavior and produce invalid textures in these cases when using VRAM compression. So there's still a bug here, but hopefully slightly less likely to be encountered by users.

One slightly more permanent fix which would be to have the Image class manage the multiple-of-4 restriction, or move that restriction over to the importer itself (that is to say, fail the import upfront if the size is wrong, or adjust the rules for "Size Limit" to detect VRAM compression and enforce additional rules. Then, the resizing logic would be moved out of etcpak code, and either validation is moved out of import, or there could be an internal flag for Image objects which are allowed to violate the Nx4 rule. That or just fail the import and warn the user that their images are about to get blurry and messed up, or disable vram compress

Doing it at a higher level would also alleviate my concerns about the fact that ETC and BPTC textures are probably broken the same way that S3TC was, but we just haven't noticed it yet because it wasn't default

I have my own qualms about the fact that .glb files skip the import system for their embedded textures. In other words, that moving the resize/fail logic into the import system might cause errors to crop up in places that run their own compression. But such places should probably be aware of the block size anyway.

body:
> Fixes #53390

This PR doesn't really fix that, it only allows to force the `AtlasTexture`s generated during the import to have zero `margin` (to not trim transparent border pixels). And the cause of #53390 is [`AtlasTexture::get_data()` returning unexpected/incorrect data](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/53390#issuecomment-933687528) in case of non-zero `margin`, which this PR doesn't change whatsoever.
I mean this PR is fine on its own. If someone wants these transparent pixels to be included in the generated atlas then sure, let them have them. But that's only a way to omit / not face the #53390, not an actual fix.
author: kleonc

body:
> This PR doesn't really fix that

I understand your point. Maybe "fix" isnt the right word. But they are related and this PR implements that user's suggestion: "I suggest is godot trims transparent pixels from texture, need option to generate atlas as is with transparent pixels."

Also, would checking 'crop_to_region' also avoid the error that that user pointed out?
author: jasonwinterpixel

body:
>I understand your point. Maybe "fix" isnt the right word. But they are related and this PR implements that user's suggestion: "I suggest is godot trims transparent pixels from texture, need option to generate atlas as is with transparent pixels."

I just meant #53390 shouldn't be auto-closed by merging this PR (currently it will be [because you wrote "Fixes #53390"](https://docs.github.com/en/issues/tracking-your-work-with-issues/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue)) as it doesn't fix the issue with the current state of things. But it's not a big deal, I guess another more specific issue could be open if that one gets closed (actually maybe I'll open one later anyway as there are more problems with the current implementation of `AtlasTexture::get_data()` than just non-zero `margin` case).

> Also, would checking 'crop_to_region' also avoid the error that that user pointed out?

Yes, as with that option generated `AtlasTexture`s have zero `margin`. But it would of course result in the generated `AtlasTexture`s being smaller if there's actually some transparent border pixels originally.

Actually, didn't think about it earlier but with your addition the `crop_to_region` might be not working as expected anymore. When `trim_alpha_border_from_region` is `false` then the region which `crop_to_region` crops against will include transparent border pixels, and thus cropping to such region won't really crop transparent borders. Meaning `crop_to_region` has no effect when `trim_alpha_border_from_region` is `false`.
The thing is `trim_alpha_border_from_region` being `false` and `crop_to_region` being `true` are kinda mutually exclusive, so these options should probably be somehow merged into a single enum option instead? :thinking:
author: kleonc

body:
Okay. I updated the comment to not say Fixes.

Whats the point of Crop To Region? Should it just be removed? What's the use case for having it checked and unchecked?

How does Crop To Region differ between Region and Mesh type import?
author: jasonwinterpixel

body:
I mean I can gather as much info as you, have to look at the relevant PRs / proposals / code. :wink:




> Whats the point of Crop To Region? Should it just be removed? What's the use case for having it checked and unchecked?

Take a look at https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1661
It should generate `AtlasTexture`s without the transparent borders, like there was no transparent borders in the first place. Meaning when cropping only "the center part" is saved in the atlas and `margin` is zero. By default (when not cropping) also only "the center part" is saved in the atlas but `margin` might be non-zero (so transparent border is saved as info about what it's like, pixels aren't stored directly as that's kinda wasteful). 


> How does Crop To Region differ between Region and Mesh type import?

[Looking at the code](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/894e2fddda029247e6e614f4a4e11d4b7fd8176d/editor/import/resource_importer_texture_atlas.cpp#L321-L390), it's used only in Region mode.

body:
> This PR creates an option for AtlasTexture importers to allow developers to enable the trim on transparent pixels on the borders. It defaults to Off, which I think is better, more expected behaviour. [ Edit - now it default to On to maintain compatibility ]

I highly support this suggestion, as it can alleviate the user-defined code's overhead in retrieving the images by considering **region** and **margin**. While the current default setting of stripping out the transparent pixels seems to have a very low optimization effect on memory, it can be still an option. Overall I think one of the main usability problems with the Atlas Texture is that the user has very small control on the very step of creating one (just a few options when importing selected images as Atlas Texture), while it generates a lot of unnecessary data later on, hard to maintain and work with.

author: Calinou

body:
Doesn't disabling alpha trimming negate most of the benefits of using a texture atlas in the first place? As I understand it, alpha trimming is the main way to reduce memory usage when using a texture atlas (along with reducing fillrate usage). While desktop devices have plenty of memory to spare now, this isn't necessarily the case on low-end mobile devices.

> This PR creates an option for AtlasTexture importers to allow developers to enable the trim on transparent pixels on the borders. It defaults to Off, which I think is better, more expected behaviour.

While changing the default value in `master` is acceptable, the default value should be left to On on `3.x` to avoid breaking compatibility with existing projects. This is because [we now follow a strict compatibility policy within a major version.](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/about/release_policy.html)
author: Calinou

body:
> Doesn't disabling alpha trimming negate most of the benefits of using a texture atlas in the first place? As I understand it, alpha trimming is the main way to reduce memory usage when using a texture atlas (along with reducing fillrate usage).

For us, we're using it for reducing draw calls. Binding the same texture for dozens of canvasitems lets them all draw in one batch.

body:
Codewise it looks ok to me, so there is mainly just any performance implications to look at, I saw the ObjectDB is using a hashmap and don't know how fast it is. I guess it would be good to check nothing here is getting hit in a hot loop in e.g. physics, as there might be an alternate strategy if that were the case.

`shape_owner_get_transform()` doesn't seem to be used outside the editor from core (but is bound).
`shape_owner_get_owner()` is used from

`KinematicCollision2D::get_local_shape`
`KinematicCollision2D::get_collider_shape`

`KinematicCollision::get_local_shape`
`KinematicCollision::get_collider_shape`

I'm not absolutely sure on this, it looks like they are just bound so probably ok (maybe a physics guy could confirm this, or at least a 2nd pair of eyes).

I'm also having no idea how fast Hashmap is, if it is O (1) then then should be fine. I'm kind of not sure why it is a Hashmap rather than a Handle Pool, maybe the IDs of Godot objects increasing is required for some reason.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> I'm also having no idea how fast Hashmap is, if it is O (1) then then should be fine.

`Hashmap` is O(1) if there is no hash collision. When there is, it'll go through a linear search among entries that have the same hash value.

body:
FYI, I still see "iPhone Developer" and "iPhone Distribution" when I'm trying to get the "Automatic" provisioning style. Worked around with this as a wrapper for `xcodebuild` (changed PATH before invoking Godot)

```
#!/bin/bash
PBX=out/ios/MyAmazingGame.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj

# undo godot's v3.5.2 wrong take here

sed 's/iPhone Developer//' < $PBX > $PBX.fix
mv $PBX.fix $PBX
sed 's/iPhone Distribution//' < $PBX > $PBX.fix
mv $PBX.fix $PBX

exec /usr/bin/xcodebuild "$@"
```

> Second, helper methods in Tree, whose sole purpose was to cast arguments to `TreeItem *`, are no longer necessary.

author: neikeq

body:
> > Second, helper methods in Tree, whose sole purpose was to cast arguments to `TreeItem *`, are no longer necessary.
> 
> Why were they needed? Is it because of DEFVALs?

Because you could not bind methods with `Object` derivatives as arguments/return value with ClassDB before this PR. So we use `Object` everywhere even if there is a way narrower type expected, and then cast and check inside of every method. I guess whoever made `Tree` just added a wrapper to save a few lines.
author: YuriSizov

body: Yeah, see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/57205/files#diff-73e583f1bfffebc5e30a925726bf40f32d72daaea940bf229e71479e98f8d1f7R339 - prior to this PR the only types that could be used in bindings were `Object *`, `Control *` and `Node *` because they had dedicated `operator`s implemented in `Variant` (and this made `Variant` depend on `node.h` and `control.h`).
author: akien-mga

body:
Ah, I see. I never realized we were using only those 3 object types in method signatures.
This looks fine then. Both [`PtrToArg`](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/46053a1be9cc1bc4957280cc76602aead421884c/core/variant/method_ptrcall.h#L155-L177) (for ptrcall) and [`GetTypeInfo`](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/46053a1be9cc1bc4957280cc76602aead421884c/core/variant/type_info.h#L235-L242) (for bindings generators) already handle this.
author: neikeq

body:
> There might be quite a few other occurrences of bindings where return types or arguments have been limited to `Object *`, `Node *` or `Control *` when they could be more specific (I'd suggest searching for them with regex on the class reference XML)

So I had a look at occurrences for `type="Object"` in the class reference XML (`rg -g'*.xml' 'type="Object"'`), here's what I found.

## Normal method bindings

Some cases that could be improved easily with the new feature:

- #57351
- #57355
- There might be more in physics code specifically, I didn't look in depth but there's a number of getters returning `Object` and some might be actually always handling `CollisionObject2D`/`3D` (albeit via their `RID`).

## Virtual methods

Virtual methods are bound with `GDVIRTUAL*` macros and those don't seem to support types more specific than `Object *`. I don't know if this can be improved. Here's an example:
```
scene/gui/control.h
301:    GDVIRTUAL1RC(Object *, _make_custom_tooltip, String)
```
(This could return `Control *` in theory.)

## PropertyInfo

I found a few under-specified object types for some signals declared like this:
```
scene/gui/base_button.cpp
506:    ADD_SIGNAL(MethodInfo("pressed", PropertyInfo(Variant::OBJECT, "button")));
```

This can be worked around via a stringly typed hint in `PropertyInfo` like this:
```
ADD_SIGNAL(MethodInfo("pressed", PropertyInfo(Variant::OBJECT, "button", PROPERTY_HINT_RESOURCE_TYPE, "BaseButton")));
```
(Even though it's not a Resource type but a Node here.)

This is used throughout the engine for signals and properties to specific their actual type in the bindings.
Might be worth looking into to see if a solution similar to the one used here could make it possible to detect these types properly without having to rely on manually specified strings.

*Edit:* This seems only needed for signals, for properties the type is inferred from the setter/getter.
*Edit 2:* For now #57358 fixes the few occurrences where `PROPERTY_HINT_RESOURCE_TYPE` was not used but could be.

body:
This looks fine to me, but I am worried about the performance impact, especially when using the circular bokeh mode. 

Can you test circular bokeh on both low res, HD, and 4K?

Or maybe @Calinou is able to test on HD and 4K?
author: clayjohn

body:
@clayjohn I tested with Circular and High Quality Bokeh.

With this patch:
| Scaling                              | FPS       |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------- |
| 0.5 scaling (960x540)       | 480 |
| 1.0 scaling (1920x1080)   | 195 |
| 2.0 scaling (3840x2160):  | 18   |

Without this patch:
| Scaling                              | FPS       |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------- |
| 0.5 scaling (960x540)       | 395 |
| 1.0 scaling (1920x1080)   | 195 |
| 2.0 scaling (3840x2160):  | 55   |

Picking a higher reference resolution would most likely improve the situation for 4k as well, but I'm not sure that's the solution or if one is required. Any suggestions?
author: ghost

body:
> Picking a higher reference resolution would most likely improve the situation for 4k as well, but I'm not sure that's the solution or if one is required. Any suggestions?

Picking a higher reference resolution is the same as decreasing the fixed ``64.0`` multiplier or reducing the default bokeh size (which was likely chosen for artistic reasons rather than performance reasons).

I see two solutions and I am torn between the two:
1. Go with this PR and have a warning in the documentation that performance will scale with resolution, so at high resolutions, you may want to reduce bokeh size proportionately.
2. Don't merge this PR and have a warning saying that Bokeh size is fixed at 64 pixels * user_specified_size and users should scale this with resolution if they want consistent results. 

Number 1 is nice, because we want the default to be "just works". That being said the performance cost is pretty hard to swallow. The end result would be "it just works*" (* but runs too slow to use).

Number 2 is better for performance and for users who don't care about blur amount as much. 

Can you do another table like the above, but for Box blur and hexagon blur? They both should scale much better with resolution. If the difference isn't as bad with them, then I lean towards 1.
author: clayjohn

body:
> Can you do another table like the above, but for Box blur and hexagon blur? They both should scale much better with resolution. If the difference isn't as bad with them, then I lean towards 1.

I feel we should change the circle bokeh shape to **not** have performance that depends on DoF amount, even if this means it'll look worse at higher resolutions. This amount-dependent caveat is just too bothersome for game developers to handle, especially if they want to expose circle bokeh as an opt-in setting for those with fast GPUs (and keep box/hexagon shape by default). It can also cause unexpected performance dips when animating the DoF amount at run-time.

I remember discussing this with @lyuma in `#rendering` on the Godot Contributors Chat, but I can't find the messages in question right now. They posted a code sample which made circle DoF have the same performance regardless of the DoF amount (at the cost of visual quality at high amounts).

I would really prefer not to have resolution-dependent DoF as the default, as many developers don't test their games on high-resolution displays (since they don't own the required hardware in the first place). Also, resolution-dependent DoF can give some players an advantage if the blur effect is intended to obstruct something the player shouldn't see.

If anyone wants to work on this, I made a testing project: [test_depth_of_field_circle.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/7937714/test_depth_of_field_circle.zip)
author: Calinou

body:
I wrote a little Python function, in order to understand how many iterations are being made for the circular bokeh inside [this for loop](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/583b6a594ab88208d1e78c94e70a2957c316cf64/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/bokeh_dof_raster.glsl#L204):
```python
def iterations(blur_scale, blur_size):
  radius = blur_scale
  i = 0
  while radius < blur_size:
    radius += blur_scale / radius
    # print(radius)
    i += 1
  return i
```

`blur_scale` = one of `[8.0, 4.0, 1.0, 0.5]` - it represents the [blur quality setting](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/58324f4df822c7416278cda5bf41662e979f9e31/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/effects_rd.cpp#L1141), from `very low quality` to `high quality`

`blur_size` = `dof_blur_amount (from the camera)` * `64.0 (hardcoded multiplier)` * `resolution_factor (from this PR)`

----

And here's some results:

| blur_scale | blur_size | iterations |
| ------------ | ----------- | --------------|
| 8 (VLQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 1.0 | 6 |
| 4 (LQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 1.0 | 18 |
| 1 (MQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 1.0 | 80 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 1.0 | 162 |

| blur_scale | blur_size | iterations |
| ------------ | ----------- | --------------|
| 8 (VLQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 2.0 | 37 |
| 4 (LQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 2.0 | 79 |
| 1 (MQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 2.0 | 326 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 2.0 | 653 |

| blur_scale | blur_size | iterations |
| ------------ | ----------- | --------------|
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.1 * 64 * 1.0 | 39 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.2 * 64 * 1.0 | 162 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.3 * 64 * 1.0 | 367 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.4 * 64 * 1.0 | 653 |
| 0.5 (HQ)      | 0.5 * 64 * 1.0 | 1022 |

The increase in iterations looks pretty steep when cycling between DoF amount and qualities.
As far as I can tell these numbers (also) don't depend on the viewport's resolution, and maybe they should?
author: ghost

body:
> The increase in iterations looks pretty steep when cycling between DoF amount and qualities.
> As far as I can tell these numbers (also) don't depend on the viewport's resolution, and maybe they should?

Your resolution multiplier would make this dependent on resolution. You can see the default setting is to blur over 64 pixels * dof_amount. 

If we wanted to keep the cost the same as screen size changed we could scale both the ``blur_scale`` and ``blur_size`` This would keep the size and performance constant, but quality would suffer with a change in resolution.
author: clayjohn

body:
> Your resolution multiplier would make this dependent on resolution. You can see the default setting is to blur over 64 pixels * dof_amount.

Yes, I was thinking about `blur_scale`, but wasn't obvious from what I wrote.

> If we wanted to keep the cost the same as screen size changed we could scale both the blur_scale and blur_size This would keep the size and performance constant, but quality would suffer with a change in resolution.

I'll try to find a sweet spot where quality doesn't visibly chang, but performance improves.
author: ghost

body:
As promised, I've been working on improving the performance of the circle blur. Here's some screenshots to compare performance and looks:
| master | optimised |
| --------- | ------------- |
| ![dof_master_circle_hq_09](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9743949/151670569-9102dc0a-dfeb-4769-a4bc-8ef1c1294097.png) | ![dof_branchv6_circle_hq_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9743949/151670570-ee1c80e9-c616-4e4b-998b-ebdcc63f5926.png) |

I've still got a few things to sort out, like improving the quality and performance of the near blur, maybe see if I can make the whole thing resemble the unoptimised effect better... and tidy up the code, although it's mostly just some small changes inside the shader code.

I'm thinking of introducing these tweaks as a new DoF mode and call it "Circle Optimised" (?). So whoever wants to use the high quality, uncompromising classic "Circle" mode can still do so, but I think that one is more suitable for offline rendering as it stands.

---

Even if the optimized DoF performs well regardless of `blur_size`, scaling up the resolution increases both the pixel count and the `blur_size` (as of this PR)... so performance will degrade quicker when scaling, no matter how optimized the effect is.

That means I might still have to add a setting, for a finer control of the decrease in quality as resolution goes up.

I could also just pick a default resolution-quality factor that's good enough, and call it a day.

---
Here's a recording as well:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9743949/151672454-a93b4bea-1f9b-45d4-a375-975ef66cf43a.mp4
author: ghost

body:
The new circle mode looks great! Nice work :slightly_smiling_face: 

> So whoever wants to use the high quality, uncompromising classic "Circle" mode can still do so, but I think that one is more suitable for offline rendering as it stands.

I think your new mode can replace the existing Circle mode, as offline rendering with Godot is considered to be a corner case. It probably doesn't warrant having entire rendering modes on its own. Pushing up all other settings (such as shadow quality and DoF quality will usually make rendering look good enough (and be slow enough) already.

Some numbers might need more tweaking to get the before advertised FPS, but the good news is I fixed 2 bugs that resulted in visual artifacts at various DOF settings (one was an [implementation mistake](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/compare/master...ceLoFaN:improve-dof?expand=1#diff-e0e450877001549f08683f98506fbd1a943d20c96417a72573cc51bc4ba9c203R201), and one was a [scaling mistake](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/compare/master...ceLoFaN:improve-dof?expand=1#diff-e0e450877001549f08683f98506fbd1a943d20c96417a72573cc51bc4ba9c203R108) that happened on odd numbered resolution, as in 1919x1079), and it resembles the original DOF better.

Also, `servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/bokeh_dof_raster.glsl` is not updated with the changes.

Also, the other DOF variants (box, hex...) might need to be adjusted because of the scaling bug-fix... not sure haven't tested.

Unfortunately, I don't think I'll be able to resume work on this for the forseable future, but I pushed what I had at the time on my working branch.

At this point, I don't think we should decouple resolution from DoF until we have a good handle on the resulting performance issues. So this is 4.x work.

body:
We've discussed this in the Input PR meeting and basically the result of the discussion is:

* There are cases where you want it scaled even if captured (example, dragging a slider with the control scaled).
* There are cases where you don't want it scaled even if captured (example, rotating a cube on the viewport by dragging).

And in both you need the GUI context, so you can't use the raw input.

So we think the best solution would be to add a _global_relative_ property (similar to _global_position_) that is unscaled, and you use the one that best works for your needs.
author: reduz

body:
I tend to agree with reduz's opinion here. There isn't a single good behavior for relative mouse movement that covers all use cases. This also allows implementing this feature/fix without having to break compatibility in any way, now that 4.0 is released.

That said, `global_relative` may be a bit confusing (it sounds like it has to do with coordinate systems), so I'd suggest `unscaled_relative` instead.
author: Calinou

body: This is still an issue, and a clear simple solution has been given over 19 months ago. You have to apply a hacky workaround for this in client code in order to "unscale" it (which is the value that you will always want when using raw input). When you interact with UI stuff you are not using raw input because raw input does not contain the mouse pointer ballistics. So I don't understand what situation you mentioned in your meeting.
author: crashfort

body:
I'm not sure I have fully wrapped my head around every possible use case in a 2D / UI scenario, but at least in a 3D game scenario you never want to scale this value. It is not a minor issue either, if careless developers default to the scaling behaviour for mouse input, it will be perceived as very broken by players and this could easily tarnish the reputation of the engine if it becomes a common issue for games made with Godot.

Even if there are cases where the scaled behaviour is desired, I believe that defaulting to the scaling behaviour violates the Godot philosophy of making the common case easy and the rare case possible. Adding a work-around to apply scaling is a lot more intuitive than to "undo" the scaling, likewise it seems strange to me to have a default property that scales and a more specialized property that doesn't.

If you absolutely must keep the behaviour of the current property, then I would suggest to simply call the new unscaled property "delta" or "raw_delta" and make it clear in the comments for this and the "relative" property what the differences are and when you want to use which.

All that aside I do agree that "unscaled_relative" would be a lot less potentially confusing than "global_relative", but neither is really good IMO for something that is the expected default behaviour.
author: danielborgmann

body:
I've tried to add `relative_unscaled` and `velocity_unscaled` properties to InputEventMouseMotion and InputEventScreenDrag: https://github.com/Calinou/godot/tree/inputevent-add-relative-velocity-unscaled

However, it doesn't seem to work for some reason. The values are always `0` even when they shouldn't be. This occurs both with input accumulation enabled and disabled. What's strange is that if I print the value in the C++ implementations of `set_relative_unscaled()` and `get_relative_unscaled()`, I can see the value being set correctly in the member variable.

**Testing project for the above branch:** [test_unscaled_relative_input.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/14058461/test_unscaled_relative_input.zip)

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/assets/180032/c2ffca6d-4ed8-4da9-8f61-64f6e929317a
author: Calinou

body:
@Calinou I believe I see the reason why you are seeing zeros.

<details>
<summary>Collapsed to avoid noise</summary>

`Viewport::push_input` with `p_local_coords = false` (the default) calls `make_input_local` which calls `xformed_by` on the InputEventMouseMotion.

`xformed_by` creates a *new* InputEventMouseMotion instance. You left a comment saying that they are intentionally not transformed, but they are never set to anything, so when `push_input` replaces the InputEvent with the one returned by `xformed_by`, it has no unscaled values.

If I add the lines
```c++
	mm->set_relative_unscaled(get_relative_unscaled());
	mm->set_velocity_unscaled(get_velocity_unscaled());
```

to `xformed_by`, then I get values in the editor (they start out the same, but when I stretch the window they have different values):

![image](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/assets/61520531/f50ab82e-15f9-48d1-8884-0c9fa14c4f2f)


</details>

body: I'm wondering if a better approach might be to treat the filesystem like an optional cache, if the first scan isn't complete. What I mean by that is, when a resource is being loaded, check to see if it's been imported, but if it hasn't, and the first scan isn't complete, then try to import it. I don't believe this would cause any more slowdown than the approach currently here, since all assets used by editor plugins would have to be imported first anyways.
author: TechnoPorg

body: Should this be done to `move_and_slide` as well? It doesn't make sense to have some functions automatically multiply delta and some not. I often forget which variables I should and shouldn't multiply by delta when writing code, so I sometimes end up applying delta twice which isn't good. Removing any automatic delta multiplication would make this much easier to use.

body:
It seems that both `Basis::get_orthogonal_index` (already exposed) and `Basis::set_orthogonal_index` are both used only in the GridMap editor and very specific to GridMap.

I feel like they should likely be moved to be helper methods in the GridMap module instead of being member methods of `Basis` which are not relevant in the core API.

Something like:
```cpp
Basis GridMapEditor::get_basis_with_orthogonal_index(int p_index);
int GridMapEditor::get_orthogonal_index_from_basis(const Basis &p_basis);
```

> This is my first pull request here and I not know much C++ so I am not sure where the unit tests are, nor how to write them in this project.

Unit tests are located in the top-level `tests/` folder, but I don't think there are any for OpenSimplexNoise yet ([which may be replaced by FastNoiseLite in the future](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/56718) anyway).

> I also do not know what else may be necessary to expose this function to GDScript and the other supported scripting languages.

The `ClassDB::bind_method()` exposes the method you added to the scripting API. However, you need to update the class reference to add documentation for the newly exposed method. This can be done by running `bin/path/to/compiled.godot.binary --doctool` on a compiled Godot binary and editing `modules/opensimplex/doc_classes/OpenSimplexNoise.xml`.

body:
> When selecting other refresh rates with xrandr, I get respectively:

Should we round the returned values to the nearest `0.01` to prevent getting values with lots of decimals? As I understand it, the smallest *meaningful* granularity in refresh rate is `0.01`. (This is why we have `59.94` and `60` for instance.)
author: Calinou

body:
> > When selecting other refresh rates with xrandr, I get respectively:
> 
> Should we round the returned values to the nearest `0.01` to prevent this? As I understand it, the smallest *meaningful* granularity in refresh rate is `0.01`. (This is why we have `59.94` and `60` for instance.)
> 

I think that would be a good choice to keep the output similar to the output on other devices. The Linux implementation calculates the refresh rate instead of getting it straight from the display, so it naturally has more precision.

I was about to open a proposal for exclusive fullscreen on Windows, as [Windows 11's *opt-in* windowed gaming optimizations only apply to games using Direct3D 10-12](https://www.pcgamer.com/uk/windows-11-improves-windowed-gaming-experience-for-cursed-gamers-who-shun-fullscreen/), not OpenGL or Vulkan. But my error was that I overwrote the min width in column properties, which works when I immediately reused it for calculations, but breaks on the next resize where min width is used for remainder calculations. @Watunder I can't figure out which node type allows instantiation of ImmediateMesh and the latest docs do not seem to say either. Any idea? (alpha 8)

`Script inherits from native type 'ImmediateMesh', so it can't be instantiated in object of type: 'MeshInstance3D'`
author: gerrywastaken

body:
@gerrywastaken This is a pull request that was opened by mistake. See https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/50014 for the actual change.

ImmediateMesh is a *resource*, not a node. Therefore, you can't add a script to it. However, you can assign an ImmediateMesh resource to a MeshInstance3D node, and add a script to the MeshInstance3D node as usual.

`1.0.0` is not a default value, it's a placeholder. So it would still make sense IMO to check if any of those field is not empty (that's what the field is when it's only showing the `1.0.0` placeholder).

Moreover the warning should be shown in the editor UI. A `WARN_PRINT` is better than nothing but it would be better if this was visible directly in the export dialog, like done already for various types of configuration issues.

> `1.0.0` is not a default value, it's a placeholder. So it would still make sense IMO to check if any of those field is not empty (that's what the field is when it's only showing the `1.0.0` placeholder).
> 
> Moreover the warning should be shown in the editor UI. A `WARN_PRINT` is better than nothing but it would be better if this was visible directly in the export dialog, like done already for various types of configuration issues.

However, if rcedit is not configured this function will return immediately. Should I still need to check those fields before checking the rcedit path? Thank you for your help and patience. :smile:

body:
> Maybe a 4096x4096 makes more sense? (since GradientTexture1D using that limit)

4096×4096 is much, much larger than a 4096×1 texture :slightly_smiling_face: 
It's pretty slow to generate, and a single 4K RGBA8 texture requires 67 MB of VRAM on its own.

This is also why the default size of a GradientTexture2D is only 64×64, whereas GradientTexture1D is 2048×1 (or 256×1 with https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/57375).

body:
Honestly, I feel that the purpose of #57223 is to implement a hacky way of synchronizing multiple animations by abusing the fact that AnimationTree uses the string name as a reference to the animation. A reference to an animation resource should be unique.

What is the use case of #57223? Isn't "extra one more AnimationPlayer with AnimationPlaybackTrack, and only one AnimationTree with AnimationStateMachine" enough for that?
author: TokageItLab

body:
I'll admit it's hacky, and I wasn't aware that one animation player could call another without relying on method tracks. That could be used to implement what I wanted, thank you very much! Not sure if this fix is of much use as I couldn't find anybody else trying to use animation trees this way. PR can be closed if you agree.

My intention was to have different animation players play "component"-like animations, which could be used together with scene inheritance. It's was supposed to be way of implementing something similar to godot-proposals/issues/3858 without actually changing the animation code, other than this "bug". However, I think the `animtree -> specific_animplayer -> base_anim_player` method will work nicely and this is no longer needed(for me at least).

Thanks again for the idea!

Edit: After some testing it seems the animation tracks don't obey the BlendTree TimeScale speed. This doesn't affect my project as I don't intend to use TimeScale for more than debugging. Out of curiosity, though, is this intended?

> Overall looks ok, but this much of code makes me think that we should have a single method in EditorNode that initializes a ColorPicker passed as argument and connect it to about_to_popup of every ColorPickerButton used in the editor. Could be done in another PR, but it would invalidate the code added here, so idk.

Alternatively, there's a proposal for using only one ColorPicker for the entire editor: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3857. The settings could be set for this ColorPicker and then they would be respected everywhere because they all use the same one. This would also fix the issue of color presets often being out of sync.

It would be a lot more work to do this than to just fix the issues in these few places, but it would prevent it from happening again in the future. And it would make it a lot easier for plugins to use the default settings as well.

--------------------------

body:
I've drafted a part of the solution for #57148

Basically, the idea is to export the static `create_mesh_array` function of each primitive mesh and leverage that when converting static colliders to raw mesh data. This way we don't need temporary mesh-es which are uploaded to the `VisualServer` and then accessed from it (which is very slow due to mutexes involved as `VisualServer` holds each mutex for a long interval).

Tested it on my reproduction project (1 big mesh and hundreds of static box colliders) and I got 100x speedup so basically, there is no problem with baking on thread anymore.

@AndreaCatania @akien-mga or @BastiaanOlij do you think this approach is ok?

body: I think unshaded is unshaded, it should ignore emission.
author: reduz

body:
I've found an use case for allowing emission to be displayed and configured on unshaded materials: better performance for global illumination. Unshaded materials are much faster to render compared to shaded materials. This can make a significant difference for billboards and the like, especially if they get close to the camera at any point in time.

Unshaded albedo *does* not contribute to global illumination like a shaded material with emission does. This can be desired for lighting up the world using VoxelGI, SDFGI or LightmapGI.

On the left, an unshaded yellow box is used. On the right, a shaded black box with yellow emission is used (as I want only emissive lighting to be displayed). Both look exactly the same, yet only the shaded emissive box will actually affect VoxelGI lighting:

![2022-09-06_22 52 48](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/180032/188736195-a555d620-ce7c-4f03-9199-d88c8aa8b2cc.png)

However, from an user perspective, you'd expect the box on the left to also affect VoxelGI in the same way. This would be possible if you could edit emission to be any color you wish on an unshaded material.

**Testing project:** [test_voxelgi_unshaded.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/9500655/test_voxelgi_unshaded.zip)
author: Calinou

body:
I wonder whether 16 bit / 24 bit may be quite scene dependent on whether it makes a visual difference, depending on the near and far planes of the shadow map "camera" and the relative size of the polys. I guess it might affect things like shadow acne.

Worth mentioning another option is just to have a global 16 bit setting, rather than overridable per viewport. That way you could probably do the PR with one line of code or so, just change `storage->config.depth_buffer_internalformat` on startup according to a project setting. I guess having it per viewport is more adaptable though.

I have to say I'm not totally convinced on having it 16 bit by default, especially at this late stage in 3.x. Quite apart from the risk that it may make some types of scenes look worse, we are also assuming here that all GPUs will always run faster with 16 bit than 24 bit, we'd need a GPU guy to confirm this (there are all kinds of counter intuitive things that can go on). Maybe some support 32 bit `GL_OES_depth32` I don't know whether we are able to take advantage of this currently, just throwing that out there.

Also it would be helpful to know what kind of performance differences to expect, have you got any test results for different platforms? Especially mobile that supports the 24 bit extension. Obviously if there is a large difference it is more convincing to change the default, but if it is marginal, maybe it would be less worth it.

body:
This seems to have broken template build with MinGW-GCC at least:
```
platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp: In function 'int widechar_main(int, wchar_t**)':
platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp:145:22: error: invalid conversion from 'const char*' to 'char*' [-fpermissive]
  145 |  char *dummy_guard = dummy;
      |                      ^~~~~
      |                      |
      |                      const char*
```

This can either be fixed with:
```diff
diff --git a/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp b/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
index c4926209c7..506c903c35 100644
--- a/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
+++ b/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
@@ -41,7 +41,7 @@
 #pragma section("pck", read)
 __declspec(allocate("pck")) static char dummy[8] = { 0 };
 #elif defined __GNUC__
-static const char dummy[8] __attribute__((section("pck"), used)) = { 0 };
+static char dummy[8] __attribute__((section("pck"), used)) = { 0 };
 #endif
 #endif
 ```

or:
```diff
diff --git a/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp b/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
index c4926209c7..906403c592 100644
--- a/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
+++ b/platform/windows/godot_windows.cpp
@@ -142,7 +142,7 @@ int widechar_main(int argc, wchar_t **argv) {
 
 #ifndef TOOLS_ENABLED
 	// Workaround to prevent LTCG (MSVC LTO) from removing "pck" section
-	char *dummy_guard = dummy;
+	char *dummy_guard = (char *)dummy;
 #endif
 
 	char **argv_utf8 = new char *[argc];
```

Not sure which is best / preserves the compiler magic that keeps this section in place.
author: akien-mga

body: Hmm, I think that it would be better to just wrap this part in `#if defined _MSC_VER` as this is MSVC-only workaround. Sorry I didn't think about it earlier (I missed the difference between `const char*` for GCC and `char *` for MSVC).

body:
Just to note that this exclusive definition is the usual way of handling integer bounds in my experience. It is a matter of definition of course, but it's the same as an integer range in a for loop being:
```
for (int x=0; x<10; x++)
```
includes 0 to 9, but not 10.

One fun thing to mention with the plus one in the `expand_to` function, is that if you are doing this in any tight loops with thousands of points, it is possible to optimize by using the non plus one version, then adding one to the size at the end as a one off, rather than for every point.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> One fun thing to mention with the plus one in the `expand_to` function, is that if you are doing this in any tight loops with thousands of points, it is possible to optimize by using the non plus one version, then adding one to the size at the end as a one off, rather than for every point.

In order to optimize this, iterate over all points while setting minx, miny, maxx, maxy and afterwards call `expand_to` twice. That way you don't calculate begin, end, position and size multiple times.

author: dalexeev

body:
I think constructors are for basic type conversions (and their variations), and static methods are for additional ones.

```
Basis Basis(axis: Vector3, phi: float)
Basis from_euler(euler: Vector3, order: int = 2) static
```

`int` and `float` are too similar, so there is a "vector + number" main constructor and an additional "vector + number" static method with a specific name (`from_euler`, not `from_vector_and_number`).

Similarly with the `Color` constructors and the `from_hsv` static method:

```
Color Color(r: float, g: float, b: float)
Color Color(r: float, g: float, b: float, a: float)
Color from_hsv(h: float, s: float, v: float, alpha: float = 1.0) static
```

The main way to convert 3(4) `float`s to `Color` is to treat them as rgb(a) components. hsv(a) is an alternative/additional way to convert 3(4) `float`s to `Color`. And the method is named `from_hsv`, not `from_floats`.

And in the case of `Color.from_string`, we don't have multiple ways to convert `String` to `Color`. And even the name of this method says that it should be a constructor.
author: dalexeev

body: We've discussed  this in a PR review meeting and would like @vnen's opinion here. @reduz thinks that it's better to introduce a GDScript-specific way to define colors (maybe with color literals?)
author: YuriSizov

> We've discussed this in a PR review meeting and would like @vnen's opinion here. @reduz thinks that it's better to introduce a GDScript-specific way to define colors (maybe with color literals?)

I think this PR has nothing to do with GDScript, it's just replacing a static method with a constructor (I added this method in #35505 because I don't know C++ very well and because at that point the similar constructor didn't work with named colors until #45030 has not been merged).

Personally, I don't think GDScript needs color literals, but that question is out of scope for this PR anyway.

If there are no plans to leave 1-2 of the most basic constructors per type, then my arguments still work.

I agree with reduz. This PR makes code like this valid:
```GDScript
modulate = "00ff00"

I recently had a bug in my project, because I unknowingly assigned int to a color (because `Color(int)` exists).

Constructors are ok, but we should make sure they don't allow implicit conversions, which seems impossible right now.

author: Mickeon

body: In that case, can you suggest a more descriptive name for the `from_string` method?
author: dalexeev

body:
Looking at the implementation, I have to say that unfortunately `from_string()` is the most appropriate, because it tries to gather the color both from **HTML** code and from the **name**. However, what a weird thing. It's just the `Color(String)` constructor but with an optional parameter...
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/c4c35ab77a4090b0b8f813bce71fdf3053f65a05/core/math/color.cpp#L458-L464
 I feel like it shouldn't exist. At worst, it should just be `from_name()` and do only one thing.
author: Mickeon

body:
> Looking at the implementation, I have to say that unfortunately `from_string()` is the most appropriate, because it tries to gather the color both from **HTML** code and from the **name**. However, what a weird thing.

This method is mostly used in `RichTextLabel` where you can use both hex and named colors. In addition, the main `Color(String)` constructor also supports both hex and names (`Color("red")` and `Color("f00")`). The only difference between `from_string` (proposed `Color(String, Color)`) and `Color(String)` is that it doesn't print an error for invalid code/names, but returns the default color. That is why I wanted to replace the static method with a constructor.
author: dalexeev

body: Whatever the case for `from_string()`, the method feels closer to calling `get()` from a Dictionary than a constructor, hence it still does not feel appropriate to morph it into being one. It could lead to something new that other Variant types could "take inspiration" from, or it could lead to more unreadable code.
author: Mickeon

body: PR review meeting: we prefer the more explicit method name over an possible implicit conversion happening using a constructor.

body:
Ah I forgot to mention, but while I am at it, maybe we should remove the `release_focus()` and `get_focus_owner()` from Control?

For `release_focus()`, I can't really find a use case where you would need to release the focus from a specific node, so I think the more generic `Viewport.gui_release_focus()` covers the most most common use case. With the PR, `Control.release_focus()` could be easily replaced by this snippet:
```
if has_focus():
    Viewport.gui_release_focus()
```
But I don't really see a use case for it TBH.


For `gui_focus_owner()`, it's more like it's not something that really belong to the Control node API. It might be a helper, but `Viewport.get_focus_owner()` seems more adequate in general. I feel like `Control.gui_focus_owner()` makes it look like the focus owner is kind of dependent on the Control node itself, while it's something global to the Viewport.
author: groud

body: As discussed on RC, I agree that removing those may make sense, having them on Control is a bit weird.
author: mhilbrunner

body: They may be awkward, but I don't think they are harmful. You would normally need them in a script that extends Control anyway, so it saves a few keystrokes. I wouldn't support adding them, but since they are already here, I don't think it's too important to remove them.
author: YuriSizov

body:
> You would normally use any of them in a script that extends Control anyway, so it saves a few keystrokes

Well, I don't think that's true. It's the first time I need this, and it is in a 3D scene. 
In fact, it is specifically because I am not in a Control node's code that I needed to release the focus (in my case a Tree node was preventing me from catching some events on a 3D node, so I needed to make it release the focus)

I may agree to keep `get_focus_owner()`, but I really don't think this is something you would need more often in a Control node's code than any other node. This function, like the release_focus one, are returning a very global and generic thing. There's no real reason, IMO, why it should be in the Control API and not in the Node or the Viewport on, if you see what i mean.
author: groud

body:
It's a global thing, but it's a thing that is related only to controls, as only controls can have focus. I'm not sure what the use case is for working with focus outside of control nodes, but I believe you that you have one. I have plenty of uses for them in controls though, and `release_focus()` works as a nice pair for `grab_focus()` when scripting. I've also used `get_focus_owner()` to check if the current control is the focus owner plenty of times. Those are more direct use cases for them than doing something with focus outside of nodes, that have any concept of focus at all.

And I think it's notable that these methods existed as `Control`-exclusive all this time and only now have you found a use for it to be accessible outside of controls. Like I've said, if they didn't exist on `Control` already, I wouldn't argue in their favor, but we have them and they are useful mostly with controls in my experience, so it makes sense to keep them.
author: YuriSizov

body:
> release_focus() works as a nice pair for grab_focus() when scripting.

Ah right. It might help if you want to toggle a specific node's focus. I don't know why you would need that, but well...

>  I've also used get_focus_owner() to check if the current control is the focus owner plenty of times. Those are more direct use cases for them than doing something with focus outside of nodes, that have any concept of focus at all.

For that you have the `Control.has_focus()` function, which is a lot more adequate.
author: groud

body:
> For that you have the `Control.has_focus()` function, which is a lot more adequate.

True, and I have less strong feelings towards `get_focus_owner()` anyway.
author: YuriSizov

body:
So I guess the consensus could be:
- Remove `Control::get_focus_owner()`
- Keep `Control::release_focus()`?

I agree that the actual implementation detail of `release_focus` make it seem a bit useless, as only one Control can have focus anyway. But I see @pycbouh's point that Control is likely the most natural place to look for for focus-related interactions, and `mycontrol.release_focus()` as an echo to `mycontrol.grab_focus()` does make sense somewhat.
author: akien-mga

body:
> So I guess the consensus could be:
    Remove Control::get_focus_owner()
    Keep Control::release_focus()?

Yeah, seems like it is. Should I update this PR or create another one?

I'd say you can update this one if it's just removing `get_focus_owner`.

*Edit:* Actually, you'll have to change the codebase somewhat to handle cases where `get_focus_owner` is being used now. So it's likely best in its own PR.

body:
All compatibility code should be wrapped inside `#ifndef DISABLE_DEPRECATED`, mostly to make it easier to find when we decide to remove it.

EDIT:
Although in this case I think the code adds a system for deprecation hints, no? So it could be useful in future versions too, the problem is that it's hard-coded as Godot 4 compatibility, so if we e.g. decide to reuse it for Godot 5 the hints would need to be renamed. I think it should be more version-agnostic.
author: KoBeWi

body:
I'd imagine if Godot 5 needs deprecation hints the same way 3.0->4.0 does, it would also have a project converter in the same way we have now.
I think it'd make it more complex than it needs to be to support future versions before they arrive.

It would also contradict [#2 from the Best Practices](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/community/contributing/best_practices_for_engine_contributors.html#to-solve-the-problem-it-has-to-exist-in-the-first-place):
> Believing that problems may arise in the future and that the software needs to be ready to solve them by the time they appear is called "Future proofing" and its characterized by lines of thought such as:
I think it would be useful for users to...
I think users will eventually need to...
This is generally considered a bad habit because trying to solve problems that don't actually exist in the present will often lead to code that will be written but never used, or that is considerably more complex to use and maintain than it needs to be.

EDIT: Of course, I'm no expert, so if you still think it would be best to support future versions, I will do so :+1:
author: jordigulley

body:
tbh I'm not sure what's better >_> If we decide to do Godot 4 -> 5 converter, the old one will most likely be removed, which means we will have to rename Godot 4 compat stuff to Godot 5. Then again, the project converter is already named "Godot 3 to 4", so adding hints named after Godot 4 is probably ok.

I'd still wrap them in the deprecated macro though.
author: KoBeWi

body: I'll do the macro for now unless I get more input about the version agnostic details. If needed, the version agnostic impl could make ProjectConverter its own separate abstract class, and ProjectConverter3to4 and future versions can just extend/override it. I think that's a problem for way down the line though, as 4.0 isn't out yet.
author: jordigulley

body: I agree that compatibility code should be wrapped in `#ifndef DISABLE_DEPRECATED`. I wouldn't try to future proof it because we don't know if the current system is something we'll want to keep using in the future, or if we'll design something better. And future proof goes against the Godot best practices ;)

body:
Other than this one, there are other `ResourceCache` threading issues that I consider should be addressed in the future.
For example, it has a `Resource *ResourceCache::get(path)` method that is already being misused (there are even callers that don't try to reference it before using it). In general, we should avoid any kind of code that deals with `RefCounted`s as pointers, as that can cause data races.
I think that one should return `Ref<Resource>`. Additionally, all callers should check if the returned resource is valid (`is_valid()`/`!is_null()`). A previous call to `bool ResourceCache::has(path)` doesn't guarantee that.

body: @reduz The lock doesn't surround the `memdelete` or the `NOTIFICATION_PREDELETE` call nor the subsequent destructor call (that would not be correct either, if anything `Ref::unreference` should do this). Nothing is locked until `NOTIFICATION_PREDELETE` and all derived destructors finish. Only once the base `~Resource()` is called, the resource cache is locked for removal.

body:
We discussed this at length on Rocket.chat and in a PR review meeting, so I'll summarize some of the points we discussed to validate this core change:

- The use case this addresses is for an external node (an observer) to monitor changes in the child nodes of a given node. This should be done without having to add code to the monitored node or its children, so all `_notification` messages are not sufficient, as those can only be handled by the node which receives the notification (the child node, or its parent - not the observer).
- This use case _can_ be addressed using the `SceneTree.node_added` signal, but this signal is emitted for every single node added to the scene tree - it has a huge performance cost and is only really relevant for debugging / editor tools (that performance cost is only paid once a callback is connected to the signal - but that's what would be needed here).
- We raised concern that we have *a lot* of ways to tap into the enter tree / ready / exit tree states. They all fulfill different needs but it would still be worth trying to make some visualization of these APIs and see if there isn't some redundancy that can be reduced. From the top of my head:
  * `SceneTree`:
    - Signals: `node_added`, `node_removed`, `node_renamed`, `tree_changed` (expensive, mostly for debugging)
  * `Node`:
    - Own state signals: `tree_entered`, `ready`, `tree_exiting`, `tree_exited`, `renamed` (can be connected to from outside to monitor the state of a given node)
    - Child state signals: `child_entered_tree` and `child_exited_tree` added in this PR (can be connected to from outside to monitor the state of a given node's direct children)
    - Notifications: `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE`, `NOTIFICATION_READY`, `NOTIFICATION_EXIT_TREE`, `NOTIFICATION_MOVED_IN_PARENT`, `NOTIFICATION_PARENTED`, `NOTIFICATION_UNPARENTED` (these are all received by the node itself when it hits each of these conditions - external monitors don't have access to this)
    - Callbacks: `_enter_tree`, `_ready`, `_exit_tree` (these are convenience virtual methods which are equivalent to handling `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE`, `NOTIFICATION_READY` and `NOTIFICATION_EXIT_TREE` in `_notification(what)`.
- We discussed that the expensive `SceneTree.node_added` could maybe be moved to `Node` so that it can be used to monitor a tree branch (a node, its children, and all their children recursively). This was considered not to be a good option for the specific use case defined above (where only direct children are relevant), as it would still be quite expensive. It's only not clear how easy to implement it would be (`SceneTree.node_added` is just each Node telling the common `SceneTree` to propagate the signal - if moved to Node then all parent nodes need to be notified? which is also quite expensive).
- For users who want to keep track of all children and grandchildren recursively in a branch, the signals added in this method can still be used, you will just have to connect them recursively (i.e. for each direct child, connect to their `child_entered_tree` signals, etc.).

TL;DR: A lot of discussion because this is a pretty core API change and we want to do this right. But all things considered that seems to be a pretty good solution for a use case which was not yet well covered by the existing, somewhat redundant APIs.
author: akien-mga

body:
> Could this be backported to Godot 3.x?

I think so yes.

> What about a notification for this (perhaps as an addition to a signal)? There is one for a node getting parented.

I answer this in part in my big paragraph above. A notification like `NOTIFICATION_CHILD_ADDED` would only be received by the parent node, and therefore it would still not be suitable for this use case where an external node needs to monitor the parent node that got a new child (i.e. without changing the script of the parent node).

That _could_ work if we added a way for external nodes to subscribe to notifications for specific nodes, which could be an avenue to explore to reduce the redundancy between existing notifications and their matching signals... but I'm not sure how feasible and meaningful that would be, it could be a topic for a dedicated proposal/discussion.
author: akien-mga

body:
> * Notifications: `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE`, `NOTIFICATION_READY`, `NOTIFICATION_EXIT_TREE`, `NOTIFICATION_MOVED_IN_PARENT`, `NOTIFICATION_PARENTED`, `NOTIFICATION_UNPARENTED` (these are all received by the node itself when it hits each of these conditions - external monitors don't have access to this)

There is also `NOTIFICATION_POST_ENTER_TREE`, which is fired right before `NOTIFICATION_READY` but fires every time the child enters tree, unlike `NOTIFICATION_READY` which is only called once per node.
That too, is not exposed externally, but would be useful in the replication PR (and in cases when you want to track nodes).
But I agree that we have so many signals already. maybe it's worth exploring the idea of exposing a `node_notification` signal that groups them all (passing the `NOTIFICATION_*` parameter).

author: Zireael07

body:
@samdze Works fine here:

```gdscript
# main.gd, has child nodes Node1 and Node2
extends Node

func _ready():
	var child = Node.new()
	child.name = "Child"
	print("Adding Child to Node1")
	$Node1.add_child(child)
	print("Removing Child from Node1")
	$Node1.remove_child(child)
	print("Adding Child to Node2")
	$Node2.add_child(child)
	print("Removing Child from Node2")
	$Node2.remove_child(child)
```

```gdscript
# node.gd, used by Node1 and Node2
extends Node

func on_child_entered_tree(node: Node):
	print("Child node '%s' entered tree in parent '%s'." % [node.name, name])

func on_child_exiting_tree(node: Node):
	print("Child node '%s' exiting tree in parent '%s'." % [node.name, name])

func _ready():
	child_entered_tree.connect(on_child_entered_tree)
	child_exiting_tree.connect(on_child_exiting_tree)
```

Prints:
```
Adding Child to Node1
Child node 'Child' entered tree in parent 'Node1'.
Removing Child from Node1
Child node 'Child' exiting tree in parent 'Node1'.
Adding Child to Node2
Child node 'Child' entered tree in parent 'Node2'.
Removing Child from Node2
Child node 'Child' exiting tree in parent 'Node2'.
```

[TestChildNotify.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/9044026/TestChildNotify.zip)

But I understand this should be a rather rare use case.

Removing and re-adding a node it's another option but that's not something the user does spontaneously.

What do you mean by reparenting a node?
To reparent a node, you have to remove it from its parent, and then add it to its new parent. That's what my example does. There's no `reparent` method but if there was it would do exactly that.
